posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Why do 95% of freelance writers have wildly inconsistent income?

Because they think a retainer is a job for life.

Here's my 2-part magic formula to help you keep more clients & generate consistent income:

The truth is, no recurring revenue “recurs” forever.

Every month when you send an invoice, the client is asking themselves:

• Is this still working for me?
• Do I really need this?

Here's the secret to keeping your clients and earning consistent income:

Restructure your monthly retainers as “projects” to help you reduce churn and secure more cash.

There are 2 big benefits of structuring “monthly retainers” as projects:

1. Client churn will go DOWN
2. The likelihood they re-sign goes UP

By starting with a predefined project timeline, you remove complications and build trust.

So, here’s the magic formula:

𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒅𝒖𝒄𝒕𝒊𝒛𝒆𝒅 𝑺𝒆𝒓𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒙 𝑷𝒓𝒐𝒋𝒆𝒄𝒕 𝑻𝒊𝒎𝒆𝒍𝒊𝒏𝒆

Even “monthly” services should be thought of, and structured, with a project timeline.

Nobody gets excited about the idea of paying a contractor a sum of money, every month, forever.

My pro-tip? Think in 3 or 6-month increments.

And by having a Productized Service you are focusing on the OUTCOMES you deliver not the "tasks" you do.

So, when creating your service pitch, here’s (generally) the form it should take:

• Offer: What are you achieving?
• Deliverables: What are creating?
• Timeline: 3-6 months
• Total Cost: Deliverable Cost x # of Months
• Offer a pre-payment discount for payment in full

Boom.

Here's an example:

Instead of “4 newsletters per month for $4K,” offer this:

• Offer: “A 6-month library of newsletter content that can be used every time a new subscriber joins your list.”

• Deliverables: 4 newsletters per month

• Timeline: 6-month contract

• Total Cost: $4K/month x 6 months = $24,000

• Pre-payment discount = $20,000

Because the client knows it'll take 6 months to build out their newsletter library, they’re more likely to keep renewing with you to lock in the outcome.

And, if they want to get a deal, they'll take the discount — and you secure the money, faster.

In other words, everybody wins.

---

document.title: 7 Brutal Truths People Often Learn Too Late In Life

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I got married this past week in Cartagena, Colombia!

Here's something I wrote for my wife, and shared during our ceremony, about what it means to find your life partner:

~
One of my favorite writers, Charles Bukowski,
said the problem is,
we look for someone to grow old together,
When the secret is to find someone to stay a child with.

When I was a child,
I asked my mother for her definition of love.
She said love is the ability to grow together.
That life is hard, people will change,
But your love for each other should always remain.

I am not the same person I was when we met.
And in the best way, neither are you.
I had never been on a safari in South Africa.
You had never been to a Chicago Blackhawks hockey game.
I hadn’t lived anywhere other than the Midwest.
And you had lived all over the world, but weren’t sure which place to call Home.

In the beginning of our relationship we used to say,
“Home isn’t a place, it’s a feeling.”
Because we lived a thousand miles apart, and getting to know each other wasn’t convenient.
Being together meant choosing each other.
Getting on planes. Meeting up in different cities.
And sad goodbyes in airports,
Until the next time we’d see each other,
And finally feel at home again.

We’re getting married today, but we committed to each other a long time ago.
From the day we met, it felt like everything else in our life was negotiable—except for our relationship.
We could live anywhere.
We could do anything.
As long as we were together.

I know, and you know, that I wouldn’t be the man I am today without you.
You are my mirror,
My greatest motivator, my battery pack, my passport to the world.
You have taught me more about myself than I could have ever learned on my own.
You have made me stronger.
You have made me softer.
But most of all, you have taught me what it means to take care of someone.
To be selfless. To love without conditions.
And to meet each other halfway,
And not just love the things we share in common,
But love what makes us different.

So if love is the ability to grow together,
And the secret is to find someone to stay a child with,
Then I know you are my partner for life.
Because years have passed,
We’ve grown and changed,
I am not the same person I was when we met,
And in the best way, neither are you.
But I still feel like we’re kids
and our love has always remained.

text:
Between 2017-2020, my ghostwriting agency made millions of dollars.

We did this by understanding "The Art of Packaging"—here's how you can use it too:

There's an important distinction that most service providers (like writers, graphic designers, etc) forget when offering their service to potential clients:

• A Service is a “task.”
• A Packaged Service (sometimes called a Productized Service) is a “bundle of outcomes.”

This is how you distinguish yourself from the overworked and underpaid freelancers all competing to deliver a “task,” not an outcome.

And the best way to do this?

Start by packaging your services as products.

• Can you offer 2+ different versions?
• What are the differences in speed or volume?

Giving your clients a way to compare and contrast your service allows them to quickly & easily say, “Sure. I want X package.”

Here’s how to do it (in 3 simple steps):

---
Step 1: Name Your Packaged Service

Here's a simple framework for a Packaged Service name:

Problem, Person, or Way + Desirable Outcome + "Package"

Some examples:

• Twitter Thought Leadership Package
• Organic LinkedIn Book-A-Call Funnel Package
• Newsletter Autopilot Package

Giving your packaged service a name makes it feel like they are buying an OUTCOME rather than just a bundle of tasks.

And if you're selling outcomes, you charge higher, premium prices.

---
Step 2: Build Your Packaged Service

If your Packaged Service was called "Newsletter Autopilot Package," you could:

• A/B test different headlines
• Write and publish each email
• Optimize their newsletter landing page

In fact, you want to add as much to your package as possible, so it feels like a complete solution.

Then, offer it all together for one price.

Simple.

---
Step 3: Upsell Your Packaged Service To Premium

Think of some other things you could offer:

• Build surveys
• Design digital products
• Create an Educational Email Course

You can either bundle ALL of this into your primary Packaged Service (increasing the price accordingly).

OR you can call this entire second list of “outcomes” an additional Packaged Service the client can buy IN ADDITION to the first one.

---
Boom, that's a wrap!

As a recap, here's how to package your ghostwriting offer:

Step 1: Name Your Packaged Service
Step 2: Build Your Packaged Service
Step 3: Upsell Your Packaged Service To Premium

This is super simple but incredibly powerful when it comes to pitching and landing high-paying clients.

---

text:
99% of writers fall into the same trap:

Trying to write for everyone.

But writing for everyone means writing for *no one*.

Here's how I use ChatGPT to get specific and take 1 idea and turn it into a newsletter topic in 11 minutes.

---
Here are your prompts:

~~~Prompt 1: Pinpoint Writing~~~

Hello, ChatGPT!

Today, we are going to do an exercise I call “Pinpoint Writing.”

I want you to help me:

- Pinpont my point of view
- Refine my point of view
- And help me think clearly about a topic 

Ultimately, I want to get clarity on 5 things:

1. What problem am I solving?
2. Whose problem am I solving?
3. What benefit am I unlocking?
4. What emotion am I creating?
5. What action am I encouraging?

You will help me test the strength of my topic, identify underlying assumptions, examples, values, influences, etc. Feel free to play devil's advocate. Follow the direction of the conversation naturally. You can ask a variety of surface level, straightforward, and challenging questions to push my thinking. Point out inconsistencies or contradictions you find along the way. We don't need to dive too deep into psychology.

I will respond in brief or in depth.

I only want 1 question at a time, so that I can focus. You can ask follow up questions after each answer. I may ask you to pause along they way to summarize. We will continue until I say stop.

To start, I will give you the topic I want to focus on.

Are you ready?

~~~Prompt 2: Generate headlines to guide your newsletter topic~~~

Great, now using the summary of the "Pinpoint Writing" exercise I want you to write 10 “Irresistible Headlines”.

An “Irresistible Headline” has these 5 fundamental pieces. 

Beside each piece, I am going to give you some examples so you can learn:

- HOW MANY (a number): 6 tips, 7 reasons, 11 mistakes, etc.
- THE WHO (an audience): for project managers, for therapists, for founders, etc.
- THE WHAT (a noun): tips, reasons, mistakes, lessons, examples, templates, case studies, etc.
- THE WHY (a reason/outcome): to get promoted, to not lose your job, to buy your first house, etc.
- “TWIST THE KNIFE” (2-3 additional benefits/outcomes): to attract your first 5 high-ticket clients and move into your dream office.

That's a wrap! Let me know in the comments—did you find these prompts useful?

---

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Since 2010, I have journaled every single morning for at least 30 minutes.

And after testing hundreds of prompts, I *always* come back to these 4:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

---
Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

---
Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

---
Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

---
I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

---

text:
Over the past 10 years, I have read 500+ business books.

But 99% should have been blog posts. 

Save yourself the time and just read these 10:

1/ Marketing

The vast majority of tactical marketing books become outdated after 1-2 years.

Some, after 6 months.

Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout is some of the most mind-bending and timeless strategic marketing thinking out there.

2/ Growth

Did you know the top 10% of consumers in your category can drive 30-70% of the revenue growth? 

Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon will completely change the way you think about growing your biz.

You don't need "all consumers."

Just Supers.

3/ Motivation

Shoe Dog by Phil Knight, founder of Nike, is one of the most inspiring business stories you'll ever read.

And it all started by selling high-quality, low-cost running shoes from Japan. 

(A style nobody thought was "cool" at the time).

4/ Leverage

The Self-Made Billionaire Effect by John Sviokla & Mitch Cohen is all about how Producers can combine conflicting, opposite ideas at the same time to create massive value.

This is the playbook for thinking like a billionaire.

5/ Wealth & Happiness

The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson is one of my favorite business books I've ever read.

It perfectly reconciles the often conflicting desire to get "really rich" with the desire to get "really happy."

6/ Productivity

I first read Manage Your Day-to-Day by 99U (Behance) back when I was 23 years old.

I still think about it all the time.

Curating productivity work habits from industry thought leaders, this is a playbook for creative thinking.

7/ Copywriting

The Boron Letters by Gary Halbert and his son, Bond, is a classic in the direct response copywriting world.

Far from a formal business book, this is a series of life & copywriting advice letters Gary wrote his son while in jail.

8/ Sales & Offer Creation

$100M Offers by Alex Hormozi is an ultimate guide to upsells.

He set the Clickfunnels record with his $100M+ biz, and this book lays out his strategic thinking in flawless detail.

9/ Hubris

Billion Dollar Loser by Reeves Wiedeman tells, in stunning detail, how "growth at all costs" in Silicon Valley can turn into a nightmare.

Every founder should read this book—and take to heart that "more money" isn't always the answer.

10/ Negotiation

Never Split The Difference by FBI hostage negotiator Chris Voss is an unreal read.

This book will change the way you think about asking for what you want.

11/ Bonus: Category Design

In 2021, Christopher Lochhead🏴‍☠️, Eddie Yoon, and I published The Category Design Toolkit.

This is the seminal book on how to create new categories in the business world—with 15 actionable frameworks to get help you get started.

That's a wrap!

What's your favorite business book of all time?

text:
Here’s how to train ChatGPT to turn product feedback into sales (with the exact prompt I use):

Almost everybody *hates* talking about themselves.

But talking about your success is key to attracting more customers and clients.

Why?

Because social proof dismantles doubt.

Here's how to train ChatGPT to become your Testimonial Generation Machine:

---
Step 1: Grab a testimonial.

No “Best product ever!” stuff.

Longer is better.

• Quotes
• Specific details
• Transformations

All of it is gold!

---
Step 2: Ask the AI to identify wins, challenges, and transformation(s).

You can ask ChatGPT to “name the transformations in this testimonial.”

But this works better if you highlight the “thing” you want.

Example: "The most impactful change and significant improvements made."

---
Step 3: Choose a transformation and ask ChatGPT to write your email, article, post, etc.

All you need is:

• The testimonial
• Your customer’s name
• The name of your product or service
• The prompt

ChatGPT will take care of the rest!

See how easy this is?

Now, you can find all the prompts to create your testimonials in the comments below.

text:
5 ways to improve yourself by 1% each day:

Most people treat “life” as something that happens to them.

They are the victim.

• When things go wrong, it’s someone else’s fault
• When they don’t get promoted, it’s because of the environment
• When a project goes haywire, when their team falls short or when the end product doesn’t come to fruition, they are never the one to blame

These people react to life, and let outside circumstances decide their fate.

They live by Default.

And then there are those that walk into any situation and grab it by the horns.

• They wake up with intention
• They determine the outcome of their day
• They are clear on their goals and they know what needs to get done 
• And when something goes wrong they are the first to admit where they went wrong

These people create the life they want to live, and don’t accept anything less.

They live by Design.

So how do you live a life by Design instead of a life by Default?

1. Write Down Your “Chief Aim”

This is a tactic I’ve adopted from Napoleon Hill’s “Think and Grow Rich.”

He talks about the importance of writing down what it is you are ultimately working towards, and then repeating it out loud to yourself every morning and every night.

It might sound silly, but try it for a month and watch what happens.

It instills in your subconscious what you’re working on and helps attract into your life what you need to make your vision a reality.

2. Surround Yourself With People You Want To Be Like

People who live by Default hang around people who live by Default.

Similarly, people who live by Design hang around people who live by Design.

As they say, “You are a reflection of the five people you spend the most time around.”

Make sure the people who fill your life and your time live with the principles aligned to your own.

3. Set Goals, And Actually Achieve Them

It’s easy to set goals, but very few people actually follow through with them to the end.

People who live by Design practice the habit of setting goals and achieving them.

No matter how big or how small, they set goals and create a measure for success.

4. Don’t Be Afraid To Go Your Own Way

Since so few people live a life of Design, it can be extremely tempting to maintain a lifestyle of Default just to keep familiar company.

This is the hardest part.

If you want to Design your life, you have to be willing to let go of people and environments that no longer serve a Default way of thinking.

5. You Have To Practice Discipline

If you truly want to Design your life, you must master the golden trait of discipline.

This is so important is because the number one challenge in life design comes down to saying “no.”

For you to be who you know yourself to be, you have to say “no” a hundred times more than you say the word “yes.”

You have to stay so obsessively true to yourself and your mission, and the only way to do that is to have an extremely strong sense of self-awareness and discipline.

text:
I have spent hundreds of hours rewriting Ship 30's landing page.

These 7 mistakes were costing us millions of dollars in lost sales:

1/ "Learn how to..."

Brutal truth: nobody wants to learn.

Anyone who buys an online course wants the OUTCOME.

So on your landing page, don't promise them: "Here's what you're going to learn!"

Instead, promise all the things they're going to:

• Do
• Experience
• Unlock

---
2/ "Here's what you get!"

Customers don't care how much work you've put into your course (features).

They only care about what your course can do for them (benefits).

• In your landing page headlines, state the benefits.
• In your course descriptions, describe the outcomes.

---
3/ No templates

The value of your course is not in how many hours it is.

Or how many modules are included.

The value = the specificity & effectiveness of your action steps.

And action steps = templates.

"I do X -> I unlock Y."

Your course better come with templates.

---
4/ No bonuses

This is copywriting 101 stuff.

To increase the level of perceived value of your course, don't just "sell a course."

Sell your course, PLUS:

• Bonus modules
• Bonus templates
• Bonus Q&As
• Etc.

The more bonuses you add, the easier the purchasing decision.

---
5/ Few testimonials

So many courses settle for 2-3 testimonials on their landing page.

Giant mistake.

You don't want 2-3 people talking about your course.

You want HUNDREDS of testimonials:

• Text
• Video
• Long-form blog post success stories

---
6/ Listing solutions w/o stating problems

The customer can't care about the solution until they care about the problem.

So, first thing on your landing page:

• State the 10 biggest problems your target customer is facing
• Emphasize the top 3
• And show you understand them

---
7/ Not giving away enough "free" material

It's counterintuitive:

The more you give away for free, the more likely people are to sign up and pay.

Example: we took almost all our core frameworks from Ship 30 and put them into this free eBook for writers.

People thought online courses were "dead" 10 years ago.

The truth is: this industry is just getting started.

So many smart, talented people are launching courses.

But their landing pages are costing them 6 figures+ in revenue.

Don't make these mistakes!!!

---

Here’s a FREE 13,000-word Ultimate Guide + email course with everything you need to:

• Build a daily writing habit
• Generate hundreds of ideas
• Start going viral with ease

Download it here: https://lnkd.in/eGyGayyD

text:
A question I get asked every day:

“How much should I charge as a ghostwriter?”

The vast majority of ghostwriters are overworked and underpaid.

To break free, you need to understand the Ghostwriter Income Staircase 📈💰:

Level 1: Charging Per Word

This is the “accepted” form of monetization for writing things like:

• Blog posts
• Press releases
• Even newsletters

If you’re at this level, you think: “Nobody makes a living as a writer.”

WRONG!

Escape Level 1 as quickly as you can!

---
Level 2: Charging Per Hour

This is the default model.

Unfortunately, your income is directly tied to "effort."

Which means:

• You have no reason to create efficiencies
• You have no reason to care about output
• You "waste time" to rationalize "charging more"

Up we go!

---
Level 3: Charging Per Month

Charging per month isn’t much better than charging per hour.

It just means:

• You get paid more upfront
• You “lock-in” more hours
• Income becomes more predictable

You may even earn LESS than if you'd just charged hourly.

Let's keep ascending.

---
Level 4: Charging Per Asset

Ah, now we’re getting somewhere!

When you charge per asset, your success isn’t measured by:

• Time
• Effort
• “Month”

Instead, success is measured by:

Not having the asset → creating the asset.

Zero to 1 moment.

---
Level 5: Charging Per Outcome

This is the ultimate goal.

It can play out in two ways:

• Profit-sharing
• Equity

Both of these vehicles are typically rare and hard to unlock early in your career.

---
My recommendation:

Accelerate to Level 4 as quickly as possible, then stay there and stack cash:

• Raise your prices
• Create efficiencies
• Build financial security 

Then, start looking for unique opportunities to reach up to Level 5.

---
If you’re brand new to ghostwriting, I would love to be your mentor.

I will show you how to:

• Productize your service
• Charge a premium
• Find high-paying clients

Here’s a crash course to get you going:

https://lnkd.in/eWmsy9MF

text:
Thinking of starting a marketing agency?

Read this to avoid "The Valley of Death":

For context:

Before I started my ghostwriting agency, I was:

• Making $20k+/mo ghostwriting myself
• Working ~4 hours per day
• 0 obligations
• Total stress-free lifestyle business

But...

At the height of our agency, I was:

• Working 80 hrs per week
• Employing 22 full-time people
• Earning 50% less ($100k/year)
• On calls from 6am to 6pm (or later)
• Unable to go even 1 full day without working

I was so stressed, I ended up in the hospital with shingles.

Everyone thinks "building an agency" is just another way of saying "effortlessly scaling income up and to the right."

📈📈📈📈📈

This is a lie.

Here's why you don't want to fall into The Valley of Death:

There are 2 viable types of agencies:

1. Lifestyle agencies (1-5 people, $250k - $1M in revenue)
2. Big business agencies (50+ people, $10M+ in revenue)

Either you want to build a small, profitable, "fun" agency.

Or you want to be a mega-successful agency entrepreneur.

All the agencies in the middle fall into what I like to call "The Valley of Death":

• $2-10M in revenue.
• Big enough to be very stressful.
• Not big enough to get you fully out of the work.

Every agency owner I know in this range hates their life.

They're going bald.

💀💀💀

Whenever I talk to entrepreneurs who want to create agencies, I always ask them this question:

"Do you want to build a lifestyle business? Or do you want to be a hardcore entrepreneur with a giant big business agency?"

These are the 2 extremes.

You want to avoid the middle.

Agencies in "The Valley of Death" rarely get acquired.

They're not big enough to be "worth it," and not automated enough to be scalable.

So if you think you're going to build a $3M agency and then get acquired, think again.

Chances are low.

If I could do it all over, I would have capped our agency at 5 people MAX.

• I'd have paid myself more
• I'd have kept the team small and relentlessly automated every one of our processes
• I'd have created a "client wait list" and increased pricing

Instead, I did the opposite:

• I paid myself less so I could hire more people
• I took almost no money out of the business (and after 3 years was left with nothing to show for it)
• I had no time to improve/automate processes
• I scaled clients w/o increasing pricing

For 90% of talented creators, "starting an agency" really just means being a professional freelancer.

Maybe you hire 1-2 people to help you out, but that should be it.

Your goal should be to build a highly profitable lifestyle business.

That's it.

text:
Another 13 rules for a life well lived:

1. Reframe saying "No" to someone else as saying "Yes" to yourself.

The more successful you become, the more opportunities you will be offered—and the more you’ll have to say, “No.”

This is the burden of opportunity.

2. The higher you climb, throw down more rope.

Don't forget, you were once that beginner too.

3. Define "love" as "the ability to grow together."

You are not looking for a flame.

You're looking for a canoe partner.

4. In an increasingly digital world, make deliberate time to practice "focus."

Otherwise, you'll live your whole life distracted by dings, alerts, and whoosh notifications.

5. Never stop reading.

6. The older you get, make more time to play.

Your time on earth is limited.

Enjoy it.

7. Lead by example.

Nobody listens to anyone who says, "Do as I say, not as I do."

8. Strive for freedom, not recognition.

A lot of the people who are recognized and celebrated in ways you desire, had to trade a significant portion of freedom in order to get there.

All depends on which outcome matters more to you.

9. If you are going to do something, do it all the way.

Go all-in.

Commit.

And take responsibility for what happens (don't blame others if the path doesn't go the way you hoped).

10. Stay far away from anyone who tells you to "be realistic."

11. Surround yourself with people who are further along than you on the path you desire most for yourself.

Like a nearby tree, you will learn how to reach for the sun just like them.

12. Don't take yourself too seriously.

Life is like building a sand castle.

No matter how big and beautiful yours is, eventually, it'll get washed away.

13. And always remember: the “perfect time” never comes. 

Every day is the perfect time.

--

That's a wrap!

And if you want 17 more rules, check out the link in the comments—I wrote another post to help you 👇

text:
In 2017, Harvard Business Review published an article titled:

"Why Business Leaders Need To Read More Science Fiction"                     

Unfortunately, the sci-fi industry has more than 100,000 titles.

Where do you start?

Ignore the rest and read these 8:

1. Ender's Game

Fun fact: This is one of the only books I actually read in high school.

Ender's Game is about as close to military game theory in "Web3" as you can get.

2. 1984

George Orwell's most well-known work is also the inspiration behind Apple's groundbreaking ad in 1984, proclaiming the end of mindless following and the great beginning of technology-induced creativity.

3. Fahrenheit 451

Maybe more relevant than ever, Ray Bradbury's totalitarian fiction story paints a grim future where freedom of speech is completely suppressed. 

If you are building technology in this domain, read this book.

4. Ready Player One

This may be the defining sci-fi book of what it might look like to live an entirely digital life, first.

And considering the explosion of NFTs and investments pouring into building "the metaverse," it feels closer than ever.

5. Dune

Dune and its subsequent chronicles were the inspiration for some of the greatest sci-fi stories of our time—including Star Wars.

Every sci-fi fan says it all starts here.

6. Snow Crash

Credited as first coining the term "the metaverse," Snow Crash paints a horrifying picture of a not-so-distant future.

7. A Clockwork Orange

"The Korova milkbar sold milk-plus, milk plus vellocet or synthemesc or drencrom, which is what we were drinking. This would sharpen you up and make you ready for a bit of the old ultraviolence."

That's all you need to know.

8. The War Of The Worlds

H.G. Wells is one of the greatest sci-fi writers to ever touch a pen.

Want to brainstorm what the future might look like amidst a martian invasion?

document.title: Freelance Writing 101

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
My words have driven over $10,000,000 in direct sales.

These are the sales copy sentences I've been using for years:

1. "This small action will lead to a giant outcome."

Every single person on earth wants something big for something small.

• Lots of money for minimal investment
• Life-changing outcome for minimal effort
• Reaching the finish line in minimal time

Speak to these desires.

--

2. "This massive problem can be fixed with this simple solution."

Again, every single person on earth wants to conquer mountains while riding on a ski lift:

• Huge obstacle, overcome instantly
• Complicated problem, made easy
• You've been lost, now you're found.

--

3. "The outcome you want isn't far away."

Before people invest time/effort/money, they need to know it's possible.

Which means you need to educate them on why:

• It's closer than they think
• Small actions -> big outcomes
• Start today

Connect the future to the present.

--

4. "It's not your fault."

Sales copy 101: never blame the customer.

• Nobody showed you the way
• Someone else set you up to fail
• You were given the wrong information

Give them someone/something else to blame—so they feel empowered to move forward & try something new.

--

5. "You've already done the hardest part."

Help the customer see the progress they HAVE made.

• Celebrate the little steps they've already taken
• Show them they "have what it takes"
• Be their biggest believer

Remember: most people don't have support systems.

Be theirs.

--

6. "I couldn't do this either."

Let the customer see themselves in your journey:

• "Here's where I started—just like you."
• "Here were my problems—just like you."
• "Here's what I tried—just like you."
• "Here's where I ended up—and you can too."

--

7. "I'm going to let you in on a little secret..."

If you've been in your industry for a long time, chances are you know some things "most people don't."

Make the customer feel like you are their source of insider knowledge:

Pull back the curtain (crazy stats work well here).

Watch me eat my own dog food:

Most people think sales copy is about "tricking" the customer, or being so persuasive you hypnotize them into making a purchase.

It's not.

Legendary sales copy = being the customer's best friend.

Educating them.

Guiding them.

Helping them.

Do this, and you can change the world.

(And make a ton of money in the process.)

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
If you want to make $100,000+ per year (in 7 simple steps), read this:

Over the past 10 years, I have created a dozen side hustles for myself that have each (individually) unlocked 6-figure income streams.

I've also built two 7-figure companies doing millions of dollars in revenue.

Making more money in life is easy.

Just follow these 7 steps:

--

Step 1: Pick a skill you love.

My college degree is in fiction writing. 

Back then, EVERYONE told me, "You'll never make money as a writer."

Until I started making good money as a writer.

Then everyone said:

"Of course, it's easy to make money as a writer."

It's subjective.

--

Step 2: Work for free.

If you can't do it for free, you can't get paid to do it.

Period. Full stop. 

So, forget monetization ($$$).

Instead, focus on MASTERY. 

• Prove you can do "the thing."
• Prove people think it's great.
• Prove you are reliable.
• Prove it works.

--

Step 3: Say YES (to everything).

Once you get really good at something, people will GLADLY pay you to do it.

Say YES.

• Say YES to things you haven't done before.
• Say YES to things that feel like a reach.
• Say YES to anything and everything.

This will 100x your growth.

--

Step 4: Choose 1 thing, ignore everything else.

After saying YES to everything, slowly separate what you love from what you don't.

• What teaches you the most?
• What is most interesting?
• What makes you the most $$$?
• What is the most efficient?

Do that. Relentlessly.

--

Step 5: Become the Category King of that 1 thing.

For me, this was ghostwriting.

• It taught me a ton.
• Talking to execs was fascinating.
• I could charge $2k for an article.
• In less than an hour.

As a result: I became the Category King of "ghostwriting for executives."

--

Step 6: Test yourself.

Making money is easy when you never stop improving.

So, as you climb the ladder, who is the next person you look up to?

What can you learn from them?

How can you "get on their level?"

(Not from a dollar standpoint, but from a skill standpoint.)

--

Step 7: Repeat

The hardest part of making 6+ figures, or jumping into a new career/space, is Step 1.

Everyone wants to know how to make money.

But very few people want to know how to be GOOD ENOUGH at a skill worthy of money.

Stop focusing on the $.

Mastery is what matters.

text:
I firmly believe every writer should also be a ghostwriter.

There's so much opportunity out there (and you can easily make 6-figures a year).                       

Here are 5 reasons why you should become a ghostwriter (and get paid to practice your craft)

1/ You get paid to learn

I'll never forget getting off the phone with my first ghostwriting client.

I spent an hour asking him every question I could think of about marketing.

Then I sent *him* the invoice for the first month's retainer: $2,000.

Life-changing.

--

2/ You get paid to practice

Everything you write, you aren’t just writing for yourself, by yourself. 

You are writing for a specific person (the client).

• You learn how to write in different voices
• You get feedback on your work from smart people
• You have a continuous supply of topics to write about

And when you have a deadline to meet, you have to beat procrastination and stop yourself from endlessly fiddling with adjectives.

Ghostwriting accelerates your writing skills 10x over just writing by yourself.

--

3/ You get paid to build your network

Writing for 300+ CEOs, Silicon Valley founders, and Grammy-winning musicians helped me grow a billion-dollar network.

Because of that, I know I have job security for life.

And ghostwriting can do the same for you.

It unlocks incredible doors of opportunity.

--

4/ You get paid to listen (to very smart and successful people)

As a ghostwriter, you surround yourself with people who know about emerging trends and industries.

This is how you get ahead of most people.

It's how angel investors get involved in startups long before the general public finds out about them.

It's how company executives know about high-powered positions long before any sort of announcement is made.

And you're getting paid to listen in on the "inner circle".

--

5/ You get paid to consume content and crystallize your takeaways

If you're waiting to become an "expert" in something before writing about it online, then ghostwriting is a fantastic playground in the meantime.

You lend your pen to the client, download their knowledge, and get to write about these topics in the process.

There's no better way to learn and form your own opinions about a topic you're interested in - and get paid for it in the process.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The Ship 30 team moves at hyperspeed.

Here's a glimpse into how we do it:

From an operations perspective, we are living in the future.

I see it every day. 

And conversely, I see inside companies that DON'T operate the way we do—and can see how they move 100x slower as a result.

Here's how we do what we do:

1. Default No Meetings. 

We maybe have one single 30-minute team meeting once per week. 

Everything else is done asynchronously, only hopping on calls when necessary. How are we able to do this? Because everyone in the company is trained on our writing principles, making them all clear thinkers & effective communicators via text.

2. Loom Everything. 

This is one of our most-used applications. 

We Loom to train new team members. We Loom to crystallize processes. We Loom to brainstorm, Loom to give feedback, I even now ask people who email or DM me (asking for a call) to send me a Loom instead.

3. Airtable + Zapier. 

Zapier is our hardest-working employee. 

We run thousands of Zaps. When we're faced with a bottleneck, our first thought is not, "Who do we need to hire?" It's, "What Zap do we need to create?"

4. ChatGPT/Prompt Writing. 

Everyone on our team knows how to use AI, and everyone is encouraged to write prompts to create efficiencies for themselves.

5. Slack. 

Our Slack looks nothing like other companies. 

We have 3 emojis: 

• A brick (for "tasks")
• A Bentley (for "speed")
• A championship ring (for "extreme ownership")

And that's literally it. 

Those 3 emojis say everything that needs to be said for any given task.

That's it!

I'm so excited to watch our team move faster and faster as we hit the ground running in 2024.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.

8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here are 7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":

Lesson 1: Your habits in your apartment define your habits in your dream house.

I wrote every day in that studio apartment.

Even in the summer, in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers every 40 min to cool off.

Only difference today = AC.

Lesson 2: All you really need is a bed and a desk.

For the first 3 years out of college, I slept on a $79 air mattress from Target.

My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

When I look at my belongings today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed and my desk.

All you need.

Lesson 3: Discipline is the leading indicator of success.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of Internet for 4 years to write my first book.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

Lesson 4: Money doesn't fix everything.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed of how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities weren't "my apartment's." They were my own.

And money wouldn't fix them.

Lesson 5: If you can't do it here, you won't be able to do it there.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll start working on Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't yet convenient.

Lesson 6: The more you achieve, the harder it is to stay disciplined.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago.

I own a TV now. I have disposable income. I can travel.

In my 20s, I didn't even have those options.

Not-having can be a gift too.

Lesson 7: "Wanting" is more fun than "Having".

Two days after I moved into my dream condo, my weekly therapy session rolled around.

"Now what?" I asked him.

I'd spent almost a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

Only to get "here" and not know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I had the opportunity to ghostwrite for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home for a work weekend: a massive ranch with a multimillion-dollar home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different today. You have so many options!"

He shook his head and said, "Actually, it's killing me."

He would rent a studio apartment in the city to make his next album.

We all think the journey somehow gets easier once we've achieved our dreams, or made a certain amount of money.

From my own experience, and what I've learned ghostwriting for so many successful people, it's actually the opposite.

The journey & creative process gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Your bed, your desk, and your apartment might change.

But the work won't.

text:
9 entrepreneurship clichés everyone parrots, but are actually wrong:

1. "The customer is always right."

No, they're not.

You do not want to optimize your business for some rando who doesn't value your product/service/category.

The customers who ARE always right are your Superconsumers.

They spend 30-70% more.

Optimize for them.

--

2. "It's all about Product-Market Fit."

No, it's not.

The moment you decide to try to "fit" into an existing category, you are unconsciously competing for 24% of the economics.

You'll never create your own category.

And will forever be in the existing Category King's shadow.

--

3. "The best brand always wins."

No, it doesn't.

Google is one of the "best" brands in the whole world.

How come Google+ failed?

Because you can't take your BRAND and walk into someone else's category and expect it to matter.

Categories make brands.

Not the other way around.

--

4. "If you're too expensive, customers won't buy from you.

Yes, they will.

Price isn't what causes someone to buy or not buy.

Education is. 

The customer has to understand the CONTEXT of the price. 

• A photo on the Internet? Free.
• An NFT of the same photo? $758,253

--

5. "Marketing is what you do when your product sucks."

What?

It took humans 300 years to realize a better use case for the wheel was transportation, not pottery.

If the greatest product ever invented couldn't speak for itself, what makes you think your dashboard can?

--

6. "Customers care about your Founder Story."

No, they don't.

They only care if your Founder Story educates them on the transformation THEY CAN EXPECT to experience as well.

You, as the founder, are not the main character.

The customer is.

--

7. "The key to success is work ethic."

No, it's not.

Work ethic matters, but if you are banging away on a product/service/company in someone else's category (or worse, a declining category), how hard you work becomes irrelevant.

You're fighting a losing battle.

Change categories!

--

8. "The best companies are like families."

No, they're not.

The best companies are like championship-winning teams. 

• If you can't keep up, you're out.
• If you can't handle your responsibilities, you're out.
• If you can't make the whole team better, you're out.

--

9. "Just focus on shipping a Minimum Viable Product"

Please don't.

Listen to the words in any other context:

• Minimum Viable Wedding Proposal
• Minimum Viable Marriage
• Minimum Viable Children

The last thing you want is to ship customers an MVP.

Make it LEGENDARY.

--

90% of what we are taught about entrepreneurship, or what we hear surrounded by entrepreneur-speak on social media, is wrong.

It's people parroting thinking they heard from someone else.

But thinking about thinking is the most important kind of thinking.

text:
17 rules for a life well lived:

1. Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2. Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3. Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4. Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5. Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6. Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7. Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8. Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9. Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10. Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11. Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12. Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13. Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14. Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15. Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16. Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17. Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

text:
We held our first ever Premium Ghostwriting Mastermind here in Miami last month.

Here’s a quick recap of how it went:

• 3 of our all-star students joined our whole team
• We spent an entire day doing Hot Seats, stress-testing new offers
• We dug into how to sell, how to frame “value,” and how to tailor offers to different audiences

The whole thing was incredibly enjoyable, and confirmed this is something we are excited to do for years.

This time next year, I expect PGM to have 50+ Premium Ghostwriters.

All scaling themselves into ghostwriting agencies doing $30k+ per month—just like these all stars. 

I am a firm believer that being a ghostwriter is the best way to collect dividends on your writing talents.

And I am on a mission to remove the stigma around “ghostwriting.”

Here’s how I explain it to people:

• The people who have knowledge to share (real industry experts & leaders) don’t have the time, or the skill set, or the desire to write. It’s a horrible use of their time relative to how they “earn” in their careers. BUT, their knowledge is immensely valuable to the world.

• The people who have the time to write oftentimes lack decades of knowledge. They aren’t a seasoned executive, or a successful founder, or an investor with a long track record. BUT, their writing skills are immensely valuable to people who don’t have the time, or the skill set, to articulate their knowledge via writing.

There is a powerful combination that gets unlocked when these two people are paired together.

The industry leader gets access to someone willing to do the “manual labor” required to crystallize their knowledge and share it with the world.

And the writer gets access to someone with tremendous knowledge and pattern recognition, essentially being paid to learn.

It’s a magical relationship.

Some of my most treasured relationships in life & business are with former clients—from whom I’ve learned so many things, and are equally as appreciative of me and the work we’ve created together.

If you want to power-level your craft, your network, and your earnings as a writer, there is no better path than ghostwriting.

And through our programs, I hope to empower millions of writers to step into this little-known world and prepare them to do great things.

So, if you’re interested in exploring ghostwriting, we’d love to have you in our flagship program, Premium Ghostwriting Academy.

And when you’re ready, once you start landing high-ticket clients and internalizing all these new skills, we look forward to having you in our Premium Ghostwriting Mastermind.

👻

text:
9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:

1/ No one owes you a job.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. 

That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

2/ If you don't grow together, you grow apart.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

You change.

They change.

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

3/ Safety is an illusion.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves.

Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

4/ Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

5/ Status symbols only work on people "below you."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

6/ It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

7/ The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are.

If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama.

If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people.

If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs.

8/ Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more and more developed.

Actions tell your story.

9/ The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as a game.

Others, an adventure. An experience. A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

And live it fully.

document.title: The Instant Writing Remixer

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
10 (painful) lessons I’ve learned writing 3,000+ articles online over the past 10 years:

1. There is only 1 secret to online writing

Volume wins.

There isn’t a writing platform on the internet where this ISN’T the case. 

Social platforms. Major publications. 

Every growth period of my writing career happened during months/years of consistent volume.

Period.

--

2. Growth hacks are overrated

In my early 20s, I spent a LOT of time reading digital marketing blogs about how to get 50% more views here, or 20% more subscribers there.

A lot of it is a waste of time.

You’re far better off just consistently creating new content.

--

3. Writing that engages/goes viral is emotional

You don’t have to “air your dirty laundry,” but you do have to say something that makes the person on the other side of the screen pause, *feel you*, and engage.

This can be done through story, tone, before/after photos, etc.

--

4. You don’t go viral by getting lucky

You go viral by publishing day after day, week after week, year after year.

My Quora dashboard is a great example. 

Out of 1,200+ Answers, less than 100 have gone viral.

That’s not luck. That’s rolling the dice 1,200+ times.

--

5. Not everyone has something interesting to say

This is a brutal truth, and one of the first big hurdles you have to overcome as a writer online. 

If your writing isn’t engaging, you might not be saying anything unique/compelling/original.

So? Dig deeper.

--

6. There is more than 1 way to “make it” ($$$) as a writer

The publishing world celebrates best-selling authors.

The reality? 

Most are broke.

The writers with the most creative AND financial freedom are all independent.

They monetize in a variety of ways—not just book sales.

--

7. Ghostwriting is gasoline for your career

It’s lucrative. 

It’s a supercharger for building a powerful network. 

It teaches you how to write in different voices. It allows you to play with words all day long & practice your craft.

Every writer should also ghostwrite.

--

8. Your personal projects will always give you the biggest ROI

I became a ghostwriter after I had already built myself as a writer on Quora. 

Nobody paid me to write online. 

That was a personal investment in myself, and it changed my life.

Always, always invest in yourself.

--

9. Any writer can make $$ online. Not every writer can get rich

The easiest ways to make $$ as a writer is by providing a service or teaching. 

Both lower the barrier to entry.

Get here, first. 

THEN, once you’re established as a full-time writer, chase the Great American Novel dream.

--

10. Being a Writer is not a destination. It’s a habit.

Anyone who is actively writing is a writer.

Anyone no longer actively writing (IMO) is not. They’re a “retired writer.”

Don’t wait for the accolade to tell you who you are.

Let your habits speak for you.

text:
The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.

Or, you could spend less than $250 and read these 8 legendary books on marketing, positioning, and copywriting:

1. Play Bigger by Christopher Lochhead, Al Ramadan, and Kevin Maney,

Category Kings win 76% of the category economics.

Everyone else fights over the remaining 24%.

You cannot be a legendary marketer in today's day and age without understanding Category Design.

--

2. Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon

The best way to grow your business isn't "more customers."

It's more Superconsumers.

Supers spend 30-70%+ more than the average consumer, and buy more products/services more often.

Win them, and you win the game.

--

3. Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout

One of the most timeless marketing books ever written, Positioning is the art of creating new space in the customer's mind for your product/service/offering.

No differentiated position, no meaningful company.

--

4. What They Don't Teach You at Harvard Business School by Mark H. McCormack

There's the way the world works "in theory." (School)

And then there's the way the world actually works.

This book is a wake-up call: relationships drive everything.

5. How To Win Friends & Influence People by Dale Carnegie

One of the first books to straddle the line between business and self-help.

This is a guide on communication, relationship-building, and creating your own definition for success.

--

6. Think & Grow Rich by Napoleon Hill

The fact this book isn't required reading in MBA programs blows my mind.

In order to become successful, Hill says you must position your Chief Aim in your mind's eye.

And obsessively pursue it.

--

7. The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson

Curated wisdom from Silicon Valley investor & founder of AngelList, Naval Ravikant, this is one of my favorite "business" books ever.

It is a guide for success while also acknowledging the flaws of the game.

--

8. The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel

Finally, before you graduate and enter "the real world," you should probably know...

Getting rich is easy.

Staying rich, and not behaving poorly with money, is the hard part.

--

9. BONUS: The Art & Business of Online Writing by Me 

The single most valuable skill you can have in the world today is the ability to communicate clearly, at scale.

Aka: writing online.

document.title: Compounding Content.pdf

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Digital Writing Analytics 101:

Not sure what your niche is?

Here are 6 Big Data Points that make it easy to:

• Find your target readers
• Spot their biggest problems
• Create content that helps them
• Build products they can’t wait to buy

Let's dive in!

--

Data Point 1: Views

Views are an easy way to get a high-level sense of whether a topic is catching fire or not.

• Lots of views = lots of attention
• Minimal views = minimal attention

*Views are NOT the most important metric.

But they’re a starting point.

--

Data Point 2: Likes

Likes are:

• A signal something was “good enough” for someone to take action
• A nice A/B test comparison next to Views

They aren't the holy grail.

But lots of likes tell you you're trending in the right direction with your audience.

--

Data Point 3: Comments

Comments are an important metric for content that:

• Sparks a debate
• Asks your audience a question
• Encourages participation (giveaways, etc.)

Lots of comments means:

• You’ve struck a chord
• You’ve found a topic your audience is curious about

--

Data Point 4: Reposts and Retweets

RTs mean your content was so good, readers want it to represent them.

As a Digital Writer, your goal should be to:

• Write things people want to share publicly
• Have people attach your work to their Digital Identity

RTs are an excellent signal.

--

Data Point 5: Bookmarks and Saves

When someone bookmarks your tweet or post, you know:

• It had information worth coming back to
• It conveyed a meaningful message
• It sparked curiosity in the reader

Bookmarks are a real sign that your content has value.

--

Data Point 6: Peer Approval

It's a great sign when:

• Someone you look up to engages with your content.
• Someone with a large audience engages with your content.
• Someone with a high amount of credibility engages with your content.

Keep an eye on those notifications. 👀

--

When your writing resonates, you want to create more just like it.

• Story: Tell it again in a different way.
• Format: Repeat it with different topics.
• Category: Write more specifically about it.
• Same Content For A Different Audience: Repackage it.

Always double down.

--

That's it!

To recap:

There are 6 data points to pay attention to:

• Views
• Likes
• Comments
• Retweets
• Bookmarks
• Peer Approval

And 4 ways to double-down on them:

• Story
• Format
• Category
• Same Content, Different Audience

--

Ready to reach 6 or 7 figures as a writer in 2024?

Then you need to know more than *just* writing: 

• Offer Creation
• Email Marketing
• AI Prompt Writing

And 4 more in-demand skills.

This Black Friday, we're launching Full Stack Writer.

But it will only be available for 5 days.

Join the waitlist in the comments 👇

The scariest piece of this to me?

Sure, we want to work with humility and remain teachable so we can learn & grow in whatever goals we've got our mind on.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
It took me 4 years to write my 1st book.

And it took me 4 months to write my 2nd book.

Now, I can write a 60,000 word book in 30 days (here's how):

1. Your book's Main Title/Subtitle is 80% of the work.

• What question (of the reader's) are you answering?
• What problem are you solving?
• What solution are you unlocking?
• How are you going to get there?

Don't start writing till you can answer these 4 questions.

2. Make a list of all the Main Points you want to make in your book.

Again, don't start writing yet.

List out:

• The big ideas that matter most
• And decide in what order

If you can do this, with clarity, your book is 90% done.

3. Under each Main Point, list out all the little sub-points.

Again, don't start writing yet.

Keep listing:

• What smaller ideas do you want to include?
• What examples?
• What insights?
• What stories?

Do this, and your book is 95% done.

4. Now just go down your list of Main Points, section by section.

All your sub-points are your "notes" of what to write about.

This makes the writing 100x easier.

Because you literally have the words right in front of you.

"Say this here."

Now you're 99% done.

5. Do a final spelling/grammar read through.

Since you took the time to organize all your thoughts FIRST, by the time you "finish writing," you're done.

Give your book a once-over for spelling/grammar.

And you're 100% done.

text:
At 26 years old, I was ghostwriting for CEOs and startup founders.

My secret to earning their trust?

Knowing the answers to specific questions.

These are the 10 questions all high-profile clients ask (and exactly how to answer them):

1. "Why can't I just find someone on Upwork to do this for half the price?"

The worst thing you can do? Resist.

Instead, say they should do exactly what they think is best. 

Then educate them on your unique skills, guiding them to making the right decision (working with you!)

2. "Why can't I pay you per hour or per word?"

When a potential clients asks this, they're trying to pay less for your work. 

So explain how working on an outcome/project basis aligns incentives (so you produce high-quality work, don't let the product drag on, etc.)

3. "Why do I have to pick a niche? I want to reach as many people as possible."

Most people (even creators) don't understand this.

Explaim how the secret to growing online is to become known for a niche and that trying to create content for everyone usually results in creating something for no one.

4."Do you have any testimonials?"

If they're asking for testimonials, you haven't sufficiently educated them on the specific problem or opportunity in their business. 

Focus on giving more free consulting to help them really understand the Ultimate Dream Outcome you're providing.

5. "Can you send me any case studies you have?"

Again, this is because you haven't educated them on the probelm they have.

Focus on making them aware of a problem in their business that's holding them back.

6. "What's the difference between you and every other ghostwriter out there?"

The easiest way to tackle this is by agreeing with them.

But then educate them on your:

• Specialization in working with people like them
• Fluency in their industry (subject matter, language, etc)
• Understanding of their specific type of business

This is how you position yourself as the best option.
 
7. "That's too expensive. I don't think what you're proposing is worth it."

Help them understand the ROI by using napkin math. 

Show how many new customers they'd need for your services to pay for themselves, based on their product or service pricing.

8. "I don't want to be famous but I want organic traffic to my business. What should I do?"

Every client is scared of being "internet famous."

So reassure them: Becoming famous online takes immense effort. 

Remind them their goal isn't fame, but driving organic traffic to their business.

9. "I want my content to sound unique. How are you going to do that?"

Validate their feelings.

Then educate them on which of the 5 Client Voice Archetypes they fall into.

10. "This section needs some work. Can you clean this up?"

Never, ever guess what they mean. 

Instead, ask to jump on a quick call and ask specific questions to force them to point out exactly what changes they want made.

posted_at.relative: 11 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
9 brutal truths I learned in my 20s (that helped me quit my 9 to 5, put on 70 pounds of muscle, and build a 7-figure writing business):

1/ No one owes you a job.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

2/ If you don't grow together, you grow apart.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

• You change
• They change

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

3/ Safety is an illusion.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves.

Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

4/ Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

5/ Status symbols only work on people "below you."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

6/ It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

7/ The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are.

• If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama
• If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people
• If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs

8/ Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more and more developed.

Actions tell your story.

9/ The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as a game.

Others, an adventure. An experience. A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

And live it fully.

That's it!

Which one hit you the hardest? Let me know in the replies.

text:
I can write the same idea in 4 different ways.

It's called the 4A Framework—here's how it works:

Most writers don't realize you can write every idea in 4 different ways:

• Actionable (Here's how)
• Analytical (Here's a breakdown)
• Aspirational (Yes, you can)
• Anthropological (Here's why)

Let's take an example using the topic of personal finance.

• "I Will Teach You To Be Rich" by Ramit Sethi is Actionable
• "The Changing World Order" by Ray Dalio is Analytical 
• "Rich Dad Poor Dad" by Robert Kiyosaki is Aspirational 
• "The Psychology of Money" by Morgan Housel is Anthropological 

Now, for each "A" you have different ways of writing about it:

1/ Actionable

These are actionable, implemental pieces of content.

The reader should gain some new insight or instruction they didn't have beforehand.

• Tips
• Hacks
• Resources
• Ultimate guides

Take your core idea and help the reader put it into practice.

2/ Analytical

These are breakdowns involving numbers, frameworks, and processes.

Take your core idea and support it with numbers and analysis.

• Trends
• Reasons
• Numbers
• Examples
• Swipe files
• Teardowns

Help the reader unlock a new way of thinking.

3/ Aspirational

These are stories of how you or others put your core idea into practice.

• Traits
• Stories
• Quotes
• Lessons
• Mistakes
• Reflections

Help the reader understand the benefits they unlock when they see the world through this new lens.

4/ Anthropological

These are things that speak to universal human nature.

• Fears
• Failures
• Struggles
• Limiting beliefs
• Misconceptions

Create a sense of urgency for the reader to fully embrace your core idea or be forever left behind.

That's it!

If you use this framework, you'll never be short of writing ideas again.

text:
I’ve made $250k+ selling books.

Here’s the simple 5-step process I use to write & list my books on Amazon:

Step 1: Write your book

I can write a 60,000-word back in 30 days.

And I do this in 3 steps:

• Decide on a title
• Outline the book
• Write each chapter of the book

To help you get started, here’s a guide on each step:

• How to write a best-selling book title: https://lnkd.in/e5uyTXHR

• How to outline your book: https://lnkd.in/eknKuPav

• How to write a 5,000-word chapter in 90 minutes: https://lnkd.in/eVWp8cfM

---
Step 2: Hire a designer ($1k - $5k)

Writing complete. Now you need a cover.

Most of the designers in big publishing houses also freelance on the side.

I’ve worked with a designer from Penguin Random House for years.

I reached out over email & now she designs all my covers.

---
Step 3: Format the text for eBook & print

This is a lot easier than most people think.

You can either:

• Pay someone to do this for you (~$500)
• Or use a platform like Reedsy (takes a couple of hours)

Now, onto the most technical part:

---
Step 4: Upload your files to Amazon Kindle Direct Publishing (KDP)

No special software is required here.

You can upload your manuscript as a:

• PDF
• HTML
• Word doc

And more.

Amazon has tons of guides on how to do this—you can’t go wrong.

It really is that simple.

---
Step 5: Preview and hit Publish.

You’ve uploaded your book to KDP for free.

Now make edits, upload your cover, enter your price, etc., and then hit Publish.

Now every time someone orders a physical copy, Amazon will print it on demand (and deliver it for you!)

Now, the final part:

You have to do your own marketing.

Before you even write your book, you should be:

• Writing online
• Building an audience & your email list

These are your best attention engines (and are way more effective than paid ads).

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Ghostwriting completely changed my life.

It's how I quit my 9 to 5 & became a millionaire before the age of 30.

So, I wrote a book about it, sharing everything I know.

Here are 7 big ideas from my book "The Art and Business of Ghostwriting":

1. The Fears Holding You Back From Making More Money Aren't Real

If you ask for more money, you're afraid:

• The client will say "No"
• They will get upset with you
• You won't be able to deliver
• You aren't "worth" the number you charge

I know, because I believed this too.

But in this video, I give you a simple reframe you need to beat these fears.

2. There Are Only 5 Voices You Need To Ghostwrite For Anyone

If you master the "5 Archetypes Of Client Voices" you'll be able to ghostwrite for anyone—from startup founders to yoga teachers.

3. You Are Not A Freelancer, You Are A One-Person Business Owner

This simple mental shift is how you differentiate yourself and charge more as a writer (and I explain exactly how in the video below).

4. Ghostwriting Is A Dividend On Your Skill

Do you:

• Love writing Twitter threads?
• Love writing eBooks?
• Love writing newsletters?

Then start charging for those skills as a ghostwriter.

5. Don't Provide Every Service Possible

I generated $5,000,000 from a single ghostwriting service.

Pick one service and get and get really good at it.

6. If You Want To Make More Money, You Have To Charge Per Asset Or Per Outcome

Still charging per hour (or worse, per word)?

This is how you stay stuck, overworked, and underpaid as a writer.

In the video, I break down what you should be doing instead.

7. He Or She Who Frames The Problem, Owns The Solution

If you're looking for writing jobs on Upwork or Fiverr, you have zero pricing power.

Instead, if YOU frame the problem, you can charge (a lot) for the solution. I explain how in the video.

That's it! 

For a full deep dive into these 7 big ideas, check out the video below.

text:
I scaled my ghostwriting agency to $2 million/year.

But the stress put me in the hospital with shingles.

If I did it again, here’s how I'd avoid “The Valley of Death” with my agency:

Before I started my agency, I was: 

• Making $20k+/month ghostwriting
• Working ~4 hours per day
• 0 obligations
• Total stress-free lifestyle business

But, at the height of our agency, I was:

• Working 80 hrs per week
• Employing 22 full-time people
• Earning 50% less ($100k/year)
• On calls from 6am to 6pm (or later)
• Unable to go even 1 full day without working

I was so stressed, I ended up in the hospital with shingles.

Everyone thinks "building an agency" is just another way of saying "effortlessly scaling income."

This is a lie.

Here's why you don't want to fall into The Valley of Death:

There are 2 viable types of agencies:

• Lifestyle agencies (1-5 people, $250k - $1M in revenue)
• Big business agencies (50+ people, $10M+ in revenue)

Either you want to build a small, profitable, "fun" agency or you want to be a mega-successful agency entrepreneur.

All the agencies in the middle fall into what I like to call "The Valley of Death":

• $2-10M in revenue.
 • Big enough to be very stressful.
• Not big enough to get you fully out of the work.

Every agency owner I know in this range hates their life (and if they're a guy, they're going bald.)

Whenever I talk to entrepreneurs who want to create agencies, I always ask them this question:

"Do you want to build a lifestyle business? Or do you want to be a hardcore entrepreneur with a giant big business agency?"

These are the 2 extremes.

You want to avoid the middle.

Agencies in "The Valley of Death" rarely get acquired.

They're not big enough to be "worth it," and not automated enough to be scalable.

So if you think you're going to build a $3M agency and then get acquired, think again.

Chances are low.

If I could do it all over, I would have capped our agency at 5 people MAX.

• I'd have paid myself more
• I'd have kept the team small and relentlessly automated every one of our processes
• I'd have created a "client wait list" and increased pricing

Instead, I did the opposite:

• I paid myself less so I could hire more people
• I took almost no money out of the business (and after 3 years was left with nothing to show for it)
• I had no time to improve/automate processes
• I scaled clients w/o increasing pricing

For 90% of talented creators, "starting an agency" really just means being a professional freelancer.

Maybe you hire 1-2 people to help you out, but that should be it.

Your goal should be to build a highly profitable lifestyle business.

That's it.

document.title: 9 Simple Habits I Use To Stay Mentally Fit As A Pro Writer

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
An easy writing framework I use to beat writer's block:

For Who/So That.

If you're struggling to reach your target reader, then:

• Choose a topic
• Ask WHO am I writing for?
• SO THAT they can do… what?

Write this at the top of the page before you start writing.

For example, let’s say you want to write about “investing.”

Step 1: Choose your FOR WHO

This could be Investing Tips for...

• Ambitious Middle Schoolers
• Startup Founders
• Retired Moms

Every time you change the WHO, the topic itself doesn’t change but the way you explain that topic most likely does.

Different audiences require:

• Different context
• Different action steps
• And maybe even different language

For example, you wouldn’t explain “investing tips” to ambitious middle schoolers the same way you would explain “investing tips” to startup founders.

So, whenever you sit down to write, at the top of the page write your WHO.

This will give you clarity as to WHO exactly you are writing for.

Step 2: SO THAT

WHY are you writing for them? SO THAT they can do what?

Let's take Investing Tips for College Students:

• To Graduate Without Any Debt
• To Save Enough Money For Your First Car
• To Set You Up For Retirement By Age 65

Again, notice how even if you keep the audience the same, the SO THAT outcome drastically changes what you are planning to write about. 

“Investing to graduate without any debt” is very different than “investing to set you up for retirement by age 65.” These are different pieces.

So, whenever you sit down to write, at the top of the page after you write your WHO, write your SO THAT as well.

You are writing for this specific reader SO THAT they can do… what?

---
