text:
In 2016, I struggled to pay rent as a copywriter.

Two years later, I was making $180k/month at my ghostwriting agency.

Here's the 2-part magic formula I used to keep more clients & generate consistent income:

Before we dive in, a brutal truth:

95% of freelance writers are trapped.

They're stuck in the feast & famine cycle of online business:

• Making wildly inconsistent income
• Clients dropping them without notice
• Monthly retainers keeping them overworked & underpaid

Even with a monthly retainer, you aren't safe.

Why?

Because no recurring revenue “recurs” forever.

Every month when you send an invoice, the client is asking themselves:

• Is this still working for me?
• Do I really need this?

Instead, to keep your clients and earn consistent income, do this:

Restructure your monthly retainers as “projects” to help you reduce churn and secure more cash.

There are 2 big benefits of structuring “monthly retainers” as projects:

1. Client churn will go DOWN
2. The likelihood they re-sign goes UP

By starting with a predefined project timeline, you remove complications and build trust.

So, here’s the magic formula:

***Productized Service x Project Timeline***

Even “monthly” services should be thought of, and structured, with a project timeline.

Nobody gets excited about the idea of paying a contractor a sum of money, every month, forever.

My pro-tip? Think in 3 or 6-month increments.

And by having a Productized Service you are focusing on the OUTCOMES you deliver not the "tasks" you do.

So, when creating your service pitch, here’s (generally) the form it should take:

• Offer: What are you achieving?
• Deliverables: What are creating?
• Timeline: 3-6 months
• Total Cost: Deliverable Cost x # of Months
• Offer a pre-payment discount for payment in full

Boom.

Here's an example:

Instead of “4 newsletters per month for $4K,” offer this:

• Offer: “A 6-month library of newsletter content that can be used every time a new subscriber joins your list.”

• Deliverables: 4 newsletters per month

• Timeline: 6-month contract

• Total Cost: $4K/month x 6 months = $24,000

• Pre-payment discount = $20,000

Because the client knows it'll take 6 months to build out their newsletter library, they’re more likely to keep renewing with you to lock in the outcome.

And, if they want to get a deal, they'll take the discount — and you secure the money, faster.

In other words, everybody wins.

text:
I used to write my headlines last.

And it took writing 3,000+ articles to realize how wrong I was.

Now, I write my headlines first & spend 80% of my writing time making them super specific.

Here's my 3-part Headline Niching Framework (steal this to attract loyal readers):

First, why should you niche down your headlines?

It helps you:

• You attract a more targeted reader
• You exclude "general" audiences

And these are good things.

You don't want to create something for everyone.

You want to be specific and speak to your ideal audience.

Here's how:

1/ Name the Audience

If you write "How To Make More Money," that answers a pretty general question.

But what if you said:

• "How To Make More Money As A Writer" or
• "How To Make More Money As A Writer In Chicago"

Say exactly WHO your writing is for.

2/ Name the Outcome 

Let's stay with this example.

Instead of "How To Make More Money," try:

• "How To Make More Money So You Can Buy Your First House" or
• "How To Make More Money So You Can Build A Music Studio In Your Backyard"

Say exactly WHAT the reader will achieve.

3/ Name the Process 

You can also name the process to unlock the promised outcome:

• "How To Make More Money As A Writer Without Leaving Your Couch" or
• "How To Make More Money As A Writer Ghostwriting For CEOs"

Yet another point of context to attract who you want.

That's it! 

Use these tips to write your next headline.

Remember: specificity is the secret to legendary writing.

text:
The biggest faulty belief stopping people from writing:

(This keeps aspiring writers stuck for years)

Waiting for the “perfect” conditions.

I've been writing online for 10+ years. And I can tell you now, if you think the ideal time will magically appear in your day, then you're going to be waiting forever.

Instead, create your own Sacred Hours.

These are blocked-off chunks of time dedicated to writing:

• No distractions
• No other responsibilities
• Nothing else to check up on

Just you and the writing.

So how do you find your Sacred Hours?

There are two key questions you need to answer:

• What time of day am I most productive?
• What time of day can I be least responsive?

Your Sacred Hours lay in the intersection between these two times.

Block this time in your calendar. They are a meeting with yourself. And the first rule to building a daily writing habit is that you *never* cancel on yourself.

Find your Sacred Hours and protect them at all costs.

text:
I grew my ghostwriting agency to $180k/month in 18 months.

The secret?

Understanding the “Art of Packaging.”

Use these 3 dead-simple steps to earn more $$$ as a writer:

There's an important distinction that most service providers (like writers, graphic designers, etc) forget when offering their service to potential clients:

• A Service is a “task.”
• A Packaged Service (sometimes called a Productized Service) is a “bundle of outcomes.”

This is how you distinguish yourself from the overworked and underpaid freelancers all competing to deliver a “task,” not an outcome.

And the best way to do this?

Start by packaging your services as products.

• Can you offer 2+ different versions?
• What are the differences in speed or volume?

Giving your clients a way to compare and contrast your service allows them to quickly & easily say, “Sure. I want X package.”

Here’s how to do it (in 3 simple steps):

Step 1: Name Your Packaged Service

Here's a simple framework for a Packaged Service name:

Problem, Person, or Way + Desirable Outcome + "Package"

Some examples:

• Twitter Thought Leadership Package
• Organic LinkedIn Book-A-Call Funnel Package
• Newsletter Autopilot Package

Giving your packaged service a name makes it feel like they are buying an OUTCOME rather than just a bundle of tasks.

And if you're selling outcomes, you charge higher, premium prices.

---
Step 2: Build Your Packaged Service

If your Packaged Service was called "Newsletter Autopilot Package," you could:

• A/B test different headlines
• Write and publish each email
• Optimize their newsletter landing page

In fact, you want to add as much to your package as possible, so it feels like a complete solution.

Then, offer it all together for one price.

Simple.

---
Step 3: Upsell Your Packaged Service To Premium

Think of some other things you could offer:

• Build surveys
• Design digital products
• Create an Educational Email Course

You can either bundle ALL of this into your primary Packaged Service (increasing the price accordingly).

OR you can call this entire second list of “outcomes” an additional Packaged Service the client can buy IN ADDITION to the first one.

---
Boom, that's a wrap!

As a recap, here's how to package your ghostwriting offer:

Step 1: Name Your Packaged Service
Step 2: Build Your Packaged Service
Step 3: Upsell Your Packaged Service To Premium

This is super simple but incredibly powerful when it comes to pitching and landing high-paying clients.

---

text:
A faulty belief holding you back as a writer:

(This stops you from hitting publish and getting feedback)

Writing in private until your work is “perfect.”

I know, because I wasted so many years Practicing In Private:

• Writing in journals
• Hiding stories on my computer
• Spending years working on drafts I never shared with anyone

In hindsight, this was one of the most unproductive things I could have done as a writer.

There's a story Malcolm Gladwell tells in his book, Outliers, about how The Beatles performed live in Hamburg, Germany over 1,200 times from 1960 to 1964 (amassing more than 10,000 hours of playing time before they ever got famous). 

I remember reading that 15 years ago and thinking, "That's weird. So they practiced in front of other people?"

Then I read that Justin Bieber, Taylor Swift, Ed Sheeran... they all practiced in front of crowds (on the street, in subway stations, in bars) early in their careers.

Why?

Because practicing in front of a crowd gives you instant feedback loops. 

You immediately know whether something is resonating or not based on the looks on people's faces.

It's a brutal way of learning—but it's also the fastest path to proficiency.

Which is why, now, I am a firm believer in practicing as many things as possible in front of other people.

Hit publish.

Get feedback.

And write WITH your readers... not "for" them.

text:
Advice every beginner writer should ignore:

"Start a blog!"

Blogs worked in 2010.

But in 2024, they're a waste of time.

Why?

Because they have no distribution flywheel.

A blog is just a website. So the big question is, how are people going to find out your website exists?

• You’re going to have to tell them about it on social media. 
• You’re going to have to send it to your friends and family members via email. 
• You’re going to build an audience elsewhere and then say, “Hey, come check this thing out over here!” 

The metaphor I like to use to show how difficult it is to start a successful blog (and get people to read your writing there) goes like this: 

Imagine you are downtown. In a big city. Restaurants are full, bars have people walking in and out. Everyone is having a blast. 

And there you show up, in your overalls and straw hat (legacy writers, gotta love ’em) with a big sign.

PARTY AT MY HOUSE. 3 MILES AWAY IN A CORNFIELD.

Thousands of people walk straight past you. 

“Party at your house? 3 miles away? In a cornfield? Why? There’s dozens of restaurants and bars, and thousands of people right here?”

Long story short: nobody is going to come over.

That’s what it’s like starting a blog, and then asking readers on social platforms to come back to your house for a drink.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
If you want to make $100,000+ per year (in 7 simple steps), read this:

Over the past 10 years, I have created a dozen side hustles for myself that have each (individually) unlocked 6-figure income streams.

I've also built two 7-figure companies doing millions of dollars in revenue.

Making more money in life is easy.

Just follow these 7 steps:

Step 1: Pick a skill you love.

My college degree is in fiction writing. 

Back then, EVERYONE told me "You'll never make money as a writer."

Until I started making good money as a writer.

Then everyone said:

"Of course, it's easy to make money as a writer."

It's subjective.

--

Step 2: Work for free.

If you can't do it for free, you can't get paid to do it.

Period. Full stop. 

So, forget monetization ($$$).

Instead focus on MASTERY. 

• Prove you can do "the thing."
• Prove people think it's great.
• Prove you are reliable.
• Prove it works.

--

Step 3: Say YES (to everything).

Once you get really good at something, people will GLADLY pay you to do it.

Say YES.

• Say YES to things you haven't done before.
• Say YES to things that feel like a reach.
• Say YES to anything and everything.

This will 100x your growth.

--

Step 4: Choose 1 thing, ignore everything else.

After saying YES to everything, slowly separate what you love from what you don't.

• What teaches you the most?
• What is most interesting?
• What makes you the most $$$?
• What is the most efficient?

Do that. Relentlessly.

--

Step 5: Become the Category King of that 1 thing.

For me, this was ghostwriting.

• It taught me a ton.
• Talking to execs was fascinating.
• I could charge $2k for an article.
• In less than an hour.

As a result: I became the Category King of "ghostwriting for executives."

--

Step 6: Test yourself.

Making money is easy when you never stop improving.

So, as you climb the ladder, who is the next person you look up to?

What can you learn from them?

How can you "get on their level?"

(Not from a dollar standpoint, but from a skill standpoint.)

--

Step 7: Repeat

The hardest part of making 6+ figures, or jumping into a new career/space, is Step 1.

Everyone wants to know how to make money.

But very few people want to know how to be GOOD ENOUGH at a skill worthy of money.

Stop focusing on the $.

Mastery is what matters.

--

• Work with experts in your niche
• Get paid to learn & build your network

text:
95% of writers approach reading completely wrong.

How to be a world-class reader as a writer:

• Annotate what you read
• Question the author's intent
• Ask how they made you feel certain emotions

I don't read for pleasure, I ready to study.

The goal of reading isn't to get lost in the story.

Instead, the goal is to understand how a piece of writing has been assembled.

Here's a simple framework to help you draw out the building blocks:

While you read, you should have 5 highlighters: 

1. Red = Plot/Main Point
2. Yellow = Description
3. Orange = Backstory
4. Purple = Voice

Highlight each word/sentence so you can see exactly what’s happening.

Then, repeat the colors in your own writing.

text:
I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight. 

No fancy journal. No complex rules. 

Just a pen, paper, and 4 simple prompts to help focus my mind and find clarity.

Here's the dead-simple system I use every day:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

---
Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

---
Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

---
Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

---
I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 10 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

---

text:
In 2017, Harvard Business Review published an article titled:

"Why Business Leaders Need To Read More Science Fiction"

Unfortunately, the sci-fi industry has more than 100,000 titles.

Where do you start?

Ignore the rest and read these 8:

1. Ender's Game

Fun fact: This is one of the only books I actually read in high school.

Ender's Game is about as close to military game theory in "Web3" as you can get.

2. 1984

George Orwell's most well-known work is also the inspiration behind Apple's groundbreaking ad in 1984, proclaiming the end of mindless following and the great beginning of technology-induced creativity.

3. Fahrenheit 451

Maybe more relevant than ever, Ray Bradbury's totalitarian fiction story paints a grim future where freedom of speech is completely suppressed. 

If you are building technology in this domain, read this book.

4. Ready Player One

This may be the defining sci-fi book of what it might look like to live an entirely digital life, first.

And considering the explosion of NFTs and investments pouring into building "the metaverse," it feels closer than ever.

5. Dune

Dune and its subsequent chronicles were the inspiration for some of the greatest sci-fi stories of our time—including Star Wars.

Every sci-fi fan says it all starts here.

6. Snow Crash

Credited as first coining the term "the metaverse," Snow Crash paints a horrifying picture of a not-so-distant future.

7. A Clockwork Orange

"The Korova milkbar sold milk-plus, milk plus vellocet or synthemesc or drencrom, which is what we were drinking. This would sharpen you up and make you ready for a bit of the old ultraviolence."

That's all you need to know.

8. The War Of The Worlds

H.G. Wells is one of the greatest sci-fi writers to ever touch a pen.

Want to brainstorm what the future might look like amidst a martian invasion?

Here you go.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I get asked this question almost every day:

“How much should I charge as a ghostwriter?”

The vast majority of freelance ghostwriters are overworked and underpaid.

To break free, you need to understand the Ghostwriter Income Staircase:

Level 1: Charging Per Word

This is the “accepted” form of monetization for writing things like:

• Blog posts
• Press releases
• Even newsletters

If you’re at this level, you think: “Nobody makes a living as a writer.”

WRONG!

Escape Level 1 as quickly as you can!

---
Level 2: Charging Per Hour

This is the default model.

Unfortunately, your income is directly tied to "effort."

Which means:

• You have no reason to create efficiencies
• You have no reason to care about output
• You "waste time" to rationalize "charging more"

Up we go!

---
Level 3: Charging Per Month

Charging per month isn’t much better than charging per hour.

It just means:

• You get paid more upfront
• You “lock-in” more hours
• Income becomes more predictable

You may even earn LESS than if you'd just charged hourly.

Let's keep ascending.

---
Level 4: Charging Per Asset

Ah, now we’re getting somewhere!

When you charge per asset, your success isn’t measured by:

• Time
• Effort
• “Month”

Instead, success is measured by:

Not having the asset → creating the asset.

Zero to 1 moment.

---
Level 5: Charging Per Outcome

This is the ultimate goal.

It can play out in two ways:

• Profit-sharing
• Equity

Both of these vehicles are typically rare and hard to unlock early in your career.

---
My recommendation:

Accelerate to Level 4 as quickly as possible, then stay there and stack cash:

• Raise your prices
• Create efficiencies
• Build financial security 

Then, start looking for unique opportunities to reach up to Level 5.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
My 3 favorite books of all time:

(these might surprise you)

1. Portnoy's Complaint by Philip Roth

This book made me want to become a professional writer.

Hilarious. 

Smart. 

It's masterful.

Technically, it's fiction, but Roth has said in interviews that it's loosely based on his own upbringing—growing up with an overbearing mother in a Jewish household. 

And the twist at the end is one of my favorite endings to a novel ever.

2. Lolita by Vladimir Nabokov

One of the most controversial novels of all time. 

Also one of the most beautifully (and comically) written. It continues to be one of the most impactful works of literature ever written.

3. Confessions of an Unlikely Bodybuilder by Sam Fussell

A memoir about a guy who falls into the world of building. 

It inspired me to get into bodybuilding and was the template for my first book Confessions of a Teenage Gamer.

text:
I have spent hundreds of hours rewriting Ship 30's landing page.

These 7 copywriting mistakes were costing us millions of dollars in lost sales:

1/ "Learn how to..."

Brutal truth: nobody wants to learn.

Anyone who buys an online course wants the OUTCOME.

So on your landing page, don't promise them: "Here's what you're going to learn!"

Instead, promise all the things they're going to:

• Do
• Experience
• Unlock

2/ "Here's what you get!"

Customers don't care how much work you've put into your course (features).

They only care about what your course can do for them (benefits).

• In your landing page headlines, state the benefits.
• In your course descriptions, describe the outcomes.

3/ No templates

The value of your course is not in how many hours it is.

Or how many modules are included.

The value = the specificity & effectiveness of your action steps.

And action steps = templates.

"I do X -> I unlock Y."

Your course better come with templates.

4/ No bonuses

This is copywriting 101 stuff.

To increase the level of perceived value of your course, don't just "sell a course."

Sell your course, PLUS:

• Bonus modules
• Bonus templates
• Bonus Q&As
• Etc.

The more bonuses you add, the easier the purchasing decision.

5/ Few testimonials

So many courses settle for 2-3 testimonials on their landing page.

Giant mistake.

You don't want 2-3 people talking about your course.

You want HUNDREDS of testimonials:

• Text
• Video
• Long-form blog post success stories

6/ Listing solutions w/o stating problems

The customer can't care about the solution until they care about the problem.

So, first thing on your landing page:

• State the 10 biggest problems your target customer is facing
• Emphasize the top 3
• And show you understand them

7/ Not giving away enough "free" material

It's counterintuitive:

The more you give away for free, the more likely people are to sign up and pay.

Example: we took almost all our core frameworks from Ship 30 and put them into this free eBook for writers.

People thought online courses were "dead" 10 years ago.

The truth is: this industry is just getting started.

So many smart, talented people are launching courses.

But their landing pages are costing them 6 figures+ in revenue.

Don't make these mistakes!!!

---

text:
19 brutal truths about going your own way in life:

1. Nobody will believe in you until you believe in yourself.

2. The right way for someone else is the wrong way for you. (And the right way for you is the wrong way for someone else.)

text:
Most life advice sucks.

Platitudes don’t change your life.

Here’s the stuff that will.

17 rules for a life well lived:

1/ Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2/ Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3/ Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4/ Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5/ Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6/ Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7/ Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8/ Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9/ Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10/ Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11/ Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12/ Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13/ Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14/ Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15/ Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16/ Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17/ Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
9 entrepreneurship clichés that sound smart but are actually wrong:

1. "The customer is always right."

No, they're not.

You do not want to optimize your business for some rando who doesn't value your product/service/category.

The customers who ARE always right are your Superconsumers.

They spend 30-70% more.

Optimize for them.

--
2. "It's all about Product-Market Fit."

No, it's not.

The moment you decide to try to "fit" into an existing category, you are unconsciously competing for 24% of the economics.

You'll never create your own category.

And will forever be in the existing Category King's shadow.

--
3. "The best brand always wins."

No, it doesn't.

Google is one of the "best" brands in the whole world.

How come Google+ failed?

Because you can't take your BRAND and walk into someone else's category and expect it to matter.

Categories make brands.

Not the other way around.

--
4. "If you're too expensive, customers won't buy from you.

Yes, they will.

Price isn't what causes someone to buy or not buy.

Education is.

The customer has to understand the CONTEXT of the price.

• A photo on the Internet? Free.
• An NFT of the same photo? $758,253

--
5. "Marketing is what you do when your product sucks."

What?

It took humans 300 years to realize a better use case for the wheel was transportation, not pottery.

If the greatest product ever invented couldn't speak for itself, what makes you think your dashboard can?

--
6. "Customers care about your Founder Story."

No, they don't.

They only care if your Founder Story educates them on the transformation THEY CAN EXPECT to experience as well.

You, as the founder, are not the main character.

The customer is.

--
7. "The key to success is work ethic."

No, it's not.

Work ethic matters, but if you are banging away on a product/service/company in someone else's category (or worse, a declining category), how hard you work becomes irrelevant.

You're fighting a losing battle.

Change categories!

--
8. "The best companies are like families."

No, they're not.

The best companies are like championship-winning teams.

• If you can't keep up, you're out.
• If you can't handle your responsibilities, you're out.
• If you can't make the whole team better, you're out.

--
9. "Just focus on shipping a Minimum Viable Product"

Please don't.

Listen to the words in any other context:

• Minimum Viable Wedding Proposal
• Minimum Viable Marriage
• Minimum Viable Children

The last thing you want is to ship customers an MVP.

Make it LEGENDARY.

--

text:
Writers give 4 big excuses to explain why they can't sell their product or service:

• "There's no demand"
• "I don't have enough credibility"
• "I don't have a big enough audience to attract clients"
• "The market is too saturated for this product or service"

These are all wrong.

99% of writers can't make a (lucrative) living because they aren't packaging their service as an Irresistible Offer.

Here's an example:

What’s the difference between one ghostwriter charging $100 for a thought-leadership article and another charging $1,000?

𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐲’𝐯𝐞 𝐟𝐫𝐚𝐦𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐢𝐜𝐞.

The work could be identical. But that’s not what matters when you are selling a service. What matters is the outcomes you generate for the client.

So many writers get fixated on the amount of “work” they do for clients (in terms of hours worked, words written, etc). They believe the more “work” they do, the more they should get paid. This is wrong. 

It's a big lie we've been fed by society.

The amount of “work” is not inherently valuable. The value comes from the outcomes your service generates for the client. So, to increase the amount you charge a client, you need to package your service so they understand you solve expensive problems and create valuable outcomes.

document.title: 20 Copywriting Tips To Hang On Your Wall

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The “How To X Without Y” headline framework instantly hooks readers:

• How to achieve the "outcome" without the "obstacle"
• How to "get what you want" without the "hard part"

Here's how I use ChatGPT to create dozens of these headlines for any topic in 3 minutes:

And here's the prompt I mention in my Deck:

-- Prompt --

Here are some topics I want to write about:

- [Topic 1]
- [Topic 2]
- [Topic 3]

For each of these topics, I want you to generate article titles using the "How to X (without Y)" format.

"X" is the goal of the subtopic.

For example:

If the topic is "Developing a training plan for first time marathoners."
Then the headline should be something like "How to develop a training plan without Y"

"Y" will be common obstacles people would face or objections as to why they wouldn't be able to accomplish "X."

For example: 

If the topic is "Developing a training plan for first time marathoners." 
And "X" is "develop a training plan."
Then the headline should be something like "How to develop a training plan without spending all your time on it"

Step 1: You are going to generate 7 common problems or objections ("Y") someone might have in accomplishing each topic outcome ("X"). Wait for my confirmation before moving to Step 2.

Step 2: On my confirmation, you will make 7 headlines for each subtopic, one for each of the problems for that subtopic.

Remember: You are creating "How to X without Y" titles for each objection someone might have in accomplishing the goal of the subtopic.

Understood?

-- End Of Prompt --

---

Become a Ghostwriter.

And it's easy to get started:

• You don't need to post on social media constantly                       

Here are 5 reasons why you should become a ghostwriter (and get paid to accelerate your career):

1/ You get paid to learn

I'll never forget getting off the phone with my first ghostwriting client.

I spent an hour asking him every question I could think of about marketing.

Then I sent *him* the invoice for the first month's retainer: $2,000.

Life-changing.

--
2/ You get paid to practice

Everything you write, you aren’t just writing for yourself, by yourself.

You are writing for a specific person (the client).

• You learn how to write in different voices
• You get feedback on your work from smart people
• You have a continuous supply of topics to write about

And when you have a deadline to meet, you have to beat procrastination and stop yourself from endlessly fiddling with adjectives.

Ghostwriting accelerates your writing skills 10x over just writing by yourself.

--
3/ You get paid to build your network

Writing for 300+ CEOs, Silicon Valley founders, and Grammy-winning musicians helped me grow a billion-dollar network.

Because of that, I know I have job security for life.

And ghostwriting can do the same for you.

It unlocks incredible doors of opportunity.

--
4/ You get paid to listen to very smart and successful people

As a ghostwriter, you surround yourself with people who know about emerging trends and industries.

This is how you get ahead of most people.

It's how angel investors get involved in startups long before the general public finds out about them.

It's how company executives know about high-powered positions long before any sort of announcement is made.

And you're getting paid to listen in on the "inner circle".

--
5/ You get paid to consume content and crystallize your takeaways

If you're waiting to become an "expert" in something before writing about it online, then ghostwriting is a fantastic playground in the meantime.

You lend your pen to the client, download their knowledge, and get to write about these topics in the process.

There's no better way to learn and form your own opinions about a topic you're interested in—and get paid for it in the process.

text:
7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":

When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.

8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here's what I learned:

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟏: 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐝𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐞.

I wrote every day in my studio. Even in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers to cool off.

Only difference today? AC.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟐: 𝐀𝐥𝐥 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐧𝐞𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐬 𝐚 𝐛𝐞𝐝 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚 𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐤.

For the first 3 years after college, I slept on a $79 air mattress. My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

Today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed & desk.

It's all you need.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟑: 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐨𝐟 𝐬𝐮𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of internet for 4 years.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟒: 𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐟𝐢𝐱 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed of how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities were my own and money wouldn't fix them.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟓: 𝐈𝐟 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐜𝐚𝐧'𝐭 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞, 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐰𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll do Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't convenient.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟔: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐯𝐞, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐲 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐝.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago:

• TV
• Travel
• Disposable income

In my 20s, I didn't have these options.

This was a gift.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟕: "𝐖𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠" 𝐢𝐬 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐟𝐮𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 "𝐇𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠".

Two days after I moved into my dream condo, I had my weekly therapy session.

"Now what?" I asked.

I'd spent a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

I got "here" and didn't know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I ghostwrote for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home: a ranch with a home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different. You have so many options!"

He shook his head, "Actually, it's killing me."

He planned to rent a city studio apt to make his next album.

We think the journey will get easier once we've achieved our dreams or made a certain amount of money.

It's the opposite.

It gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Everything might change.

But the work won't.

text:
The internet wants to make you rich.

So if you're struggling to make a living online, then you're making one big mistake:

You don't understand the secret to differentiation (yet).

Here's how to differentiate yourself and make tons of $$$ online:

• Solve 1 specific problem
• For 1 specific type of person
• In 1 specific way

It's that simple.

Here's how to apply this simple framework to ghostwriting:

Step 1: Pick A Problem

In Ship 30 for 30, we teach a framework called The 2-Year Test.

Here’s how it works:

• List all the jobs/careers you’ve had
• List all the interests you’ve had

From this list, you'll be able to select an industry you're qualified to ghostwrite in.

Step 2: Name Your Target Customer

Who needs a ghostwriter?

Anyone with expertise!

• CEOs
• VPs
• Sales people
• Coaches

Your job is to help these types of people get their ideas out into the world.

Choose 1.

Step 3: Choose A (Lucrative) Service

The more specific you are, the more money you'll make.

Good vehicles:

• X/LinkedIn Ghostwriter
• Educational Email Courses
• YouTube Scripts

Bad vehicles:

• Books
• Speeches
• Landing Pages

Pick 1 & master it.

Step 4: Name & Claim Your Niche

You’re trying to get down to 1 very specific sentence:

“I ghostwrite {service} in {industry} for {person}.”

This whole exercise shouldn’t take more than 30 minutes.

Don't overcomplicate it.

text:
9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their *entire* lives avoiding:

𝟏/ 𝐍𝐨 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚 𝐣𝐨𝐛.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

𝟐/ 𝐈𝐟 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐝𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐠𝐫𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐨𝐠𝐞𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫, 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐠𝐫𝐨𝐰 𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

• You change
• They change

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

𝟑/ 𝐒𝐚𝐟𝐞𝐭𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐚𝐧 𝐢𝐥𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves.

Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

𝟒/ 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐟𝐥𝐚𝐰𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐯𝐢𝐝𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐬, 𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

𝟓/ 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐬 𝐬𝐲𝐦𝐛𝐨𝐥𝐬 𝐨𝐧𝐥𝐲 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤 𝐨𝐧 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 "𝐛𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

𝟔/ 𝐈𝐭'𝐬 𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐢𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐨 𝐛𝐞 𝐛𝐫𝐨𝐤𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐠𝐮𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐟𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐫𝐢𝐜𝐡 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚𝐭 𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐤 𝐨𝐟 𝐥𝐨𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐭 𝐚𝐥𝐥.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy & complain vs try to fix it & fail.

𝟕/ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐚 "𝐡𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐢𝐫𝐫𝐨𝐫𝐬" 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐰𝐡𝐨 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐫𝐞.

• If you're a dramatic person, you're going to attract drama
• If you're an insecure person, you're going to attract insecure people
• If you're an egomaniac, you're going to attract egomaniacs

𝟖/ 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐮𝐦 𝐭𝐨𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐨𝐟 𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more & more developed.

Actions tell your story.

𝟗/ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐮𝐫𝐩𝐨𝐬𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐠𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞 𝐩𝐮𝐫𝐩𝐨𝐬𝐞.

Some people view life as a game.

Others, an adventure. A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

---
📌 The best way to accelerate your progress?

Start a side hustle.

And ghostwriting is the easiest way to land your first $5k client.

text:
Upwork and Fiverr are a race to the bottom.

• Low wages
• Long hours
• Endless competition

Writers on these platforms make 3 big mistakes.

And these mistakes keep them stuck earning minimum wage and churning out blog posts for 60 hours a week.

Let's dive in.

---
Mistake 1: Calling themselves a "Freelance Writer".

When you hear “Freelance Writer,” what do you think of? 

• Underpaid
• Over-caffeinated
• Jack-of-all-trades writer, who spends hours on Upwork competing for the “highest” price they can “underbid” the competition on

Is that what you want? 

Probably not.

So stop calling yourself a Freelance Writer and start calling yourself a Premium Ghostwriter.

Here's why:

• It implies you're MORE than a Freelance Writer
• It means you're hired by business leaders, not just a business
• It positions you as a business consultant (who happens to be an all-star writer)

What you call yourself matters.

---
Mistake 2: They wait for clients to come to them

As soon as you wait for people to come to you, they have all the pricing power.

They can set the terms of the engagement and the price and determine the work you do.

Instead, if you pitch clients, you set the terms.

• What you offer
• How much you charge
• The outcomes and results you create for clients

Instant upgrade.

---
Mistake 3: They work with an employee mindset

Business owners who are looking for writers aren't looking for another employee to manage.

They are looking to hire an expert who can come educate and advise them on how to solve a problem or achieve an outcome. But writers who are on Upwork or Fiverr position themselves as "do-ers": people who will do the tasks allotted to them. 

Instead, you want to be the trusted partner and consultant who proposes the plan (and also gets it done).

The business owner wants to do as little thinking as possible (they already have enough on their plate). If you can prove your worth, you will become indispensable to the business owner, which means you'll never run out of work with them.

Because once people find all-star writers, they want to hang onto them!

That's it!

If you can fix these 3 big mistakes 99% of writers fall into, then you'll always be able to make a (lucrative) living as a writer.

---

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
7 steps to write, record, and launch your first 7-figure video course—even if you’ve never built a digital product before:

(I'm going to take you inside our Premium Ghostwriting Academy as an example. $3M+ revenue in our first year)

---
Step 1: Name The Modules

All digital products are just a series of modules, strung together, to help a customer achieve a specific goal.

So, start backwards:

• What's the end goal?
• What are the steps to get there?

Make each step a module.

---
Step 2: Prep The Page

Here's an outline you can use to "prep" an entire module:

• "In this module, we'll cover..."
• "To start, avoid these mistakes..."
• "Instead, take these action steps..."
• "You probably have these questions/objections..."
• "Here's your assignment!"

---
Step 3: Write Each Section

Once the page has been "prepped," all you have to do is fill it in!

Remember your toolkit:

• 1/3/1 Writing Rhythm
• Single-sentence openers
• Use subheads to separate sections
• Alternate paragraphs & bulleted lists

Make it easy to skim!

---
Step 4: Add Q&A After Each Section

Pro tip here...

After every section, your reader is going to be faced with 2 obstacles:

• Questions: "But what do I do if...?"
• Objections: "I don't agree with that because..."

Pause and address these before continuing on.

---
Step 5: Mock Example Looms

Don’t just “tell” the reader how to do something.

Show them!

• Pretend you’re in their shoes
• Perform the step you’re asking them to take
• Record yourself doing what you’re asking them to do

This helps them “imagine” themselves doing it.

---
Step 6: Loom Review (Of Your Example Loom!)

Here’s where it gets meta:

• Record a Loom
• Watching your Mock Loom
• Pointing out & explaining “why” you’re doing all the tiny things you’re doing.

This takes the reader 3 layers deep—and helps them internalize what’s happening.

---
Step 7: Finish Each Module With An Actionable, Measurable Assignment

The ONLY measure for success for your digital product is whether customers DO something with it.

• Give them an Assignment
• Make it objective & binary
• Hold them accountable

Action > Information

---
Bonus Step 8: Get Beta Customers ASAP!

Do not spend 6+ months trying to assemble the perfect product.

Get V1 in people’s hands as quickly as possible—and then iterate:

• Where are people confused?
• Where are people stuck?

Then, revamp to V2.

---

• Earn a dividend on your writing talent
• Get paid to learn & build your network

text:
Here's how I dropped the ball and missed out on my first million-dollar business:

In 2014, I published an eBook called Skinny to Shredded. 

• It was 20 pages
• I wrote it in a weekend
• And it's how I made my first $5,000 online

It made me 50x more money than my first memoir, Confessions of a Teenage Gamer, which took me 4 years to write. 

I got the idea when one of my Quora answers went viral. 

I told the story of how, all growing up, I was sick & undiagnosed with Celiac Disease.

• How I weighed less than 100 lbs as a senior in high school
• How I had a concave chest and a face full of acne
• How I was super insecure, socially anxious, and struggled to make friends

And how I went gluten-free, got into bodybuilding, put on 70 lbs of muscle, and completely changed my life.

Overnight, that Quora answer went massively viral—and ended up on the front page of Reddit.

And my inbox started flooding with 2 questions:

1. "What are your workouts?"
2. "What do you eat to put on muscle?"

This experience COMPLETELY changed the way I thought about writing.

For the first time, I saw it not from the perspective of the writer (me), but the perspective of the READER. They were telling me what they needed help with. And were more than willing to pay in exchange for the information.

That eBook is how I made my first "Internet money."

But more importantly, it changed my entire trajectory as a writer because it made me realize I am not the main character in the story. The reader is the main character. And "the secret" to becoming a successful writer is to be in service of the reader.

Even though it changed my trajectory as writer, I still made one fatal mistake: 

I didn't improve the product.

If I had:

• Spoken to my customers
• Asked them what they wanted next
• And then improved the product (and created new products from their questions)

I could have had a million-dollar business.

I have learned a lot since then, but I still keep coming back to that key lesson: 

You are in service to your reader and your customer. You should always ask what they want next (not just give them what you *think* they want).

You can make a good living as a writer if you follow this one golden rule. 

The rest is just execution.

Anyone with expertise (who wants to share it to earn money or status) needs a ghostwriter.

Instead of getting yourself into six figures of debt with a college writing program or MFA, ghostwriting allows you to get paid to practice your craft in front of a demanding audience.

Ghostwriters are hired by professionally successful people who have a lot of power in their business or the organization they work for.

posted_at.relative: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The easiest way to 10x your writing:

Formatting — here's why:             

Formatting makes your work:

• Easier to skim
• Easier to quote & share
• And easier for readers to “remember” and repeat to their friends

So here's my 4-step formatting framework (this will change your life as a writer): 

Step 1: Start every section with a single-sentence opener.

When you open each area of your work with a single sentence, you:

• Keep readers interested
• Make things easier to read
• Subconsciously encourage them to continue

Here are the 6 most effective openers:

• Open with a moment in time
• Open with a vulnerable statement
• Open with a controversial opinion
• Open with a weird, unique insight
• Open with a thought-provoking question
• Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence

These are my go-to proven ways of hooking the reader.

--
Step 2: Always look for opportunities to turn long paragraphs into bulleted lists.

If you are:

• Listing anything, ever, or,
• Rambling off a dozen quick examples, or,
• Making a series of distinct points to drive home a point

...make it into a bulleted list.

(Like this!)

--
Step 3: Where are your subheads?

Subheads are bolded sentences that help the reader more easily follow your line of thinking.

• They split the page up evenly
• They offer big, bolded “milestones”
• They help the reader identify where they’re going

Subheads give clarity.

--
Step 4: Use the 1/3/1 writing rhythm.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

You can also use:

• 1/4/1
• 1/5/1
• 1/2/5/2/1
• Etc.

The key to injecting skimmability into your writing is alternating the length of sentences and sections.

--
In all your formatting choices, your primary concern should always be:

"How can I make this easier for the reader?"

• Is a bulleted list easiest? Do that.
• Is a step-by-step guide easiest? Do that.
• Is a series of small, rapid-fire sections easiest? Do that.

Your North Star is the reader's enjoyment.

---

text:
5 dead-simple steps to write a 60,000-word book in 30 days—even if you've never written a book before:

For context:

It took me 4 years to write my 1st book.

And it took me 4 months to write my 2nd book.

Now, I can write a 60,000-word book in 30 days.

Here's the framework:

1. Your book's Main Title/Subtitle is 80% of the work.

• What question (of the reader's) are you answering?
• What problem are you solving?
• What solution are you unlocking?
• How are you going to get there?

Don't start writing till you can answer these 4 questions.

---
2. Make a list of all the Main Points you want to make in your book.

Again, don't start writing yet.

List out:

• The big ideas that matter most
• And decide in what order

If you can do this, with clarity, your book is 90% done.

---
3. Under each Main Point, list out all the little sub-points.

Again, don't start writing yet.

Keep listing:

• What smaller ideas do you want to include?
• What examples?
• What insights?
• What stories?

Do this, and your book is 95% done.

---
4. Now just go down your list of Main Points, section by section.

All your sub-points are your "notes" of what to write about.

This makes the writing 100x easier.

Because you literally have the words right in front of you.

"Say this here."

Now you're 99% done.

---
5. Do a final spelling/grammar read through.

Since you took the time to organize all your thoughts FIRST, by the time you "finish writing," you're done.

Give your book a once-over for spelling/grammar.

And you're 100% done.

---
Boom, that's how to write a 60,000-word book in 30 days.

Here's the 5-step framework I use:

1. Choose the Main Title & Subtitle
2. Make a list of all the Main Points
3. List out all the little Sub-Points
4. Fill in your Sub-Points
5. Do a final spelling/grammar check

---

text:
Most freelance writers are broke.

Why?

Because they're stuck at the *bottom* of the 5 levels of pricing power.

Here's how to ascend the levels and make $10-$20k per month as a writer:

There are 5 levels to increasing your income as a ghostwriter. 

• Charging per word
• Charging per hour
• Charging per month
• Charging per asset
• Charging per outcome

Let’s take a look at each one and where you should be thinking about positioning yourself as a writer.

(And for the full breakdown, check out the video below!)

---
Level 1: Charging Per Word

You want to escape this level as quickly as you can. 

Your value is tied to the number of words you write, which means you inherently have little long-term pricing power. 

Move on.

---
Level 2: Charging Per Hour

Getting paid per hour is a simple way to be compensated—in the beginning. 

But you do NOT want to be in the business of your value being tied to “effort” for long. There is no incentive to get better or get your work done faster. 

Why would you if it means you actually make less money? 

---
Level 3: Charging Per Month

Is this better than being paid per word? Yes. 

Is this better than being paid per hour? Usually. 

But getting paid in exchange for doing “anything” the client needs is a giant mistake—and almost always ends up earning you less than if you had just charged hourly. 

Make sure you are *very* specific about what you will and won’t do—otherwise you quickly become overworked and underpaid.

---
Level 4: Charging Per Asset

Ahh, now we're getting somewhere.

Sell a productized service—an asset you can create over and over again. 

All the client cares about is whether or not you successfully did what you said you were going to do (not how long you worked for or the number of words you wrote).

You create an asset (like a 5-day Educational Email Course), and you charge a fee for the creation of that asset. 

The measure for success has nothing to do with a) your time, or b) your effort. 

---
Level 5: Charging Per Outcome

This is the ultimate goal and where you will make the most money as a ghostwriter (or solopreneur of any kind). 

The only way to increase your pricing power above Level 4 is to price anchor what you do the the OUTCOME you generate. 

For example, if an Educational Email Course is going to drive $100,000 of new revenue for your client every month, then selling one for $7,000 is a no-brainer for the client. 

Accelerate yourself to Level 4 and Level 5 as quickly as possible and optimize at these levels for several years.

Raise your prices. 

Build efficiencies. 

This is how you make $10-$20k per month as a ghostwriter.

---

Use ChatGPT to leverage the 5 Stages of Customer Awareness (so you can create any offer, for any customer, and make $$$$$):

Act as a {TARGET AUDIENCE}. 

I help {TARGET AUDIENCE} who want {IMMEDIATE OUTCOME} so they can {ULTIMATE BENEFIT}. 

Describe your situation for each of the 5 Stages of Customer Awareness:

• Unaware
• Problem aware
• Solution aware

Write in a simple and clear tone using 1st person.

Avoid telling me what stage you are in.
Avoid sensing words.
Avoid emotional explaining.
Avoid jargon.
Avoid superlatives.

Please use:

• Specific actions or objects involved.
• Detailed context or condition.
• Clear goals or desired outcomes.
• Any quantifiable metrics or standards.
• Be as specific as you can about by price, problem, industry, location, situation, experience, and demographic.

--Prompt 2--

Turn my customer quote into 3 relevant hooks for each of the "5 Stage of Customer Awareness."

Focus on lived moments, real life examples, strong emotion, relatable experience, or numbers whe writing your hooks. 

Quote = "{QUOTE}"

Use a mix of the 6 most proven single-sentence openers for each hook. 

1. Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence.
2. Open with a thought-provoking question.
3. Open with a controversial opinion.
4. Open with a moment in time.
5. Open with a vulnerable statement.
6. Open with a weird, unique insight.

No labels.

• Deep expertise

Newsletters have no viral loops.

So you need to drive traffic from social media.

Think of your newsletter as the expanded, more valuable version of your social content.

4 Steps To Starting Your Newsletter

text:
The average tuition for an MBA costs $60,000 per year.

Or, you could spend less than $250 and read these 8 legendary books on marketing, positioning, and copywriting:

---
1. Play Bigger by Christopher Lochhead, Kevin Maney, and Al Ramadan

Category Kings win 76% of the category economics.

Everyone else fights over the remaining 24%.

You cannot be a legendary marketer in today's day and age without understanding Category Design.

---
2. Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon

The best way to grow your business isn't "more customers."

It's more Superconsumers.

Supers spend 30-70%+ more than the average consumer, and buy more products/services more often.

Win them, and you win the game.

---
3. Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout

One of the most timeless marketing books ever written, Positioning is the art of creating new space in the customer's mind for your product/service/offering.

No differentiated position, no meaningful company.

---
4. What They Don't Teach You at Harvard Business School by Mark H. McCormack

There's the way the world works "in theory." This is what you learn in school.

And then there's the way the world actually works.

This book is a wake-up call: relationships drive everything.

---
5. How To Win Friends & Influence People by Dale Carnegie

One of the first books to straddle the line between business and self-help.

This is a guide to communication, relationship-building, and creating your own definition for success.

---
6. Think & Grow Rich by Napoleon Hill

The fact this book isn't required reading in MBA programs blows my mind.

In order to become successful, Hill says you must position your Chief Aim in your mind's eye.

And obsessively pursue it.

---
7. The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson 

Curated wisdom from Silicon Valley investor & founder of AngelList, Naval Ravikant, this is one of my favorite "business" books ever.

It is a guide for success while also acknowledging the flaws of the game.

---
8. The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel

Finally, before you graduate and enter "the real world," you should probably know...

Getting rich is easy.

Staying rich, and not behaving poorly with money, is the hard part.

---
9. BONUS: The Art & Business of Online Writing 

The single most valuable skill you can have in the world today is the ability to communicate clearly, at scale.

Aka: writing online.

---

text:
I spent 3,000+ hours obsessing over how to write a perfect headline.

Why?

Because it doesn't matter how good your writing is, *no one* will read it if the headline fails to hook attention.

Here are the 5 pieces of an effective headline:

1. The Number
2. The What
3. The Who
4. The Why
5. Twist The Knife

If you apply these frameworks to your headlines, you will capture more attention and get more eyeballs on your work.

But so many writers push back on this way of thinking.

And this comes from 3 big misunderstandings about headlines:

1. “That’s clickbait!”

Writers think that if you include any of these in your headline, it's automatically clickbait:

• A number ("5 Steps To...")
• A triggering adjective (“Mind-boggling…”) 
• A hint at an outcome (”…so you can quit your 9 to 5”) 

But this is not true. It’s only clickbait if you fail to keep your promise.

2. “That headline is too long!” 

Newsflash: 

Length isn’t what matters. 

Some people think one 250-word Atomic Essay is too long and other people will read 9,567 words from their favorite digital writer and STILL want more.

What matters is whether you are speaking directly TO the reader. If you are, there’s no such thing as “too long.” And if you’re not, then even a 4-word headline is a waste of their time.

3. “My favorite writers don’t have to do this!” 

Seth Godin doesn’t write long headlines. His headlines are short and quippy. Things like, “Marketing Thoughts” or “Everyone Is Someone.” 

The problem is: you’re not Seth Godin. 

Don't compare yourself to someone who has been writing for decades. 

And I bet he would be 100x more widely read if he used these frameworks and wrote clear headlines instead.

So if you want to get people’s attention online, you need to learn how to write headlines that STOP them in their tracks and make them say, “Hmm… This looks like it’s for me. Maybe I should give this a read.”

In this video, I explain how.

---

text:
Here's how I've been able to earn millions of dollars as a writer:

In the beginning, I gave 99% of my work away for free.

Most writers want to *skip* straight to earning $$$.

But if you can't get people to read or use your FREE work, you'll never get them to buy your paid work.

“Working For Free” is really just a marketing cost—except with more upside potential:

• It's how I proved (and improved) my skills as a writer in the beginning

• It's how I landed hundreds of paying clients

• It's how I have become friends with some of the most well-connected entrepreneurs, executives, and investors in the world

Here's how it works:

When you put a price on something, people have to think extra hard about whether or not they want to buy what you’re selling. 

But if you say, “Look, I believe this is going to help you so much, I’ll do the first one for free—just so you can see what I’m talking about,” and you over-deliver, you just traded a few “free” hours for limitless upside. 

I’ve had clients that were so skeptical of working together, people who insisted ghostwriting would never work for them because “nobody could capture their voice,” who I knew I could deliver for—if they would just give me the chance. 

So, I did the first piece for free. 

I proved I was the right person for the job. And, almost immediately, they went from being hesitant to work together to asking me to help them with everything (and paying me whatever I asked for).

And this is where most people get it wrong.

Most writers and creators want to quit their jobs and instantly monetize their skills.

Don't do this.

Instead, leverage free work into paid work (on the side), then increase your rates until your side skill is out-earning your full-time job.

Once this happens, quit.

text:
Success isn’t a destination. 

It’s a daily habit. 

Here are 30 things successful people tell themselves:

(From ghostwriting for 300+ CEOs, industry leaders, and serial entrepreneurs):

1. Today is a great day to build.

2. No one is responsible for my success besides me.

3. I welcome the learning process with open arms.

4. Everyone was once a beginner.

5. Mastery over my craft is the highest accolade.

6. I will not let someone else define my self worth.

7. To unlock the upside, I must face the risk of the downside.

8. I need to be patiently impatient and have a bias toward action.

9. The enemy of long-term compounding is short-term results.

10. Wealth is built over decades, not days.

11. I have no competition, because I am only competing against myself.

12. The world rewards those who do what others do not.

13. I trust myself and my convictions.

14. Every failure is an opportunity to learn.

15. Every success is an opportunity to start again.

16. Forward is the only option. There is no going back.

17. I own my shortcomings—and leverage them to my advantage.

18. Dwelling on a mistake where the lesson has already been extracted is a waste of precious time.

19. My potential knows no limits.

20. Simplicity is velocity, because the simple thing is the hard thing.

21. It's better to move slowly in the right direction than quickly in the wrong direction.

22. I do not accept any obstacle in my way.

23. "Tomorrow" is an excuse. Do it now or don't do it at all.

24. I don't know how yet—but I will figure it out.

25. What I once wanted, I currently have. (The destination isn't what matters.)

26. Hours are a poor measure of effort. Intentional practice is the only thing that matters.

27. I will not judge my entire life based on any single day.

28. Better to be singular and deep than multifaceted and shallow.

29. Real progress is made in silence.

30. Life is not a zero-sum game. We can all win.

text:
Every writer should have a newsletter making $10k/month.

But most overthink, never make $1, and give up too soon.

So what's the secret to creating a successful paid newsletter?

Your positioning.

Or, in other words, the promise your newsletter makes to the reader.

This needs to be:

1. Tangible

Tangible is something you can touch or measure.

Intangible language: "Live the life of your dreams & retire early!"

You can't touch or measure this.

Tangible language: "Retire before age 45 with $1,000,000 in your bank account."

2. Have an objective outcome

Subjective outcome: "Become successful."

Ask 10 people their definition of success and you'll get 10 different answers.

Objective outcome: "Make $300k/year."

You either objectively achieve this or you don't.

3. Solve a problem 

If your newsletter doesn't solve a reader's problem, then there's no reason for them to subscribe!

This positioning is 90% of the game.

The rest is simple:

1. Write about your newsletter on social
2. Drive traffic to your newsletter
3. Send free snippets to your list
4. Create a cliffhanger in the newsletter to create FOMO so people upgrade to paid

text:
When I put in my 2-weeks notice, I met my mentor for breakfast.

He'd been my boss for 4 years.

But that morning, he gave me the best piece of advice I ever got as an entrepreneur:

I'd got the job working for his ad agency through a mutual friend, and from the beginning, he treated me like his younger brother.

We got to know each other extremely well.

• He took me out shopping and taught me what color shoes matched with what color pants, shirts, and belts.

• He took me to yoga workshops because he knew the value of staying centered and balanced.

• He always encouraged me to keep writing and working on my books.

And he would always introduce me as his “Padawan,” a Star Wars reference he knew I enjoyed, painting the picture that he was the Jedi master and I was his young apprentice.

The day I put in my 2-weeks notice was one of the scariest days of my entire life.

When we sat down at the table, Chicago’s brisk summer sun coming up over the pool in Soho House. He asked what was up, and I just started talking. 

And talking. 

I don’t even remember what I said, except for that I felt it was time I went my own way. 

He didn’t get upset, or say anything for a while.

He just sat there, calm.

What made the whole thing so hard was that he had become so much more than just my “boss.”

I wasn’t just putting in my 2-week’s notice. 

It felt like I was saying I didn’t want to be part of the family business.

After a long while, he shared his thoughts. 

He was sad to see me go, but more than that, excited for what the future held. He asked for the bill and said he was going to take the rest of the day off.

"Today is a sad day,” he said as we got up from the table to walk to the elevator.

As we were waiting for the *ding* he asked what I planned to do..

“I’m going to write,” I said. “Maybe become an entrepreneur, I don’t know yet." And I didn’t know — not really. All I knew was that it was time to take the leap.

“Well, if you do become an entrepreneur,” he said, “here are some words of advice: 

When everyone is rattled by the storm, you have to bring the calm.

When everyone else gets stressed, you have to stay cool, calm, and collected.

When everyone is freaking out, you have to reassure them. When there is doubt, you have to bring certainty.”

*Ding*

We rode the elevator down in silence.

I didn’t understand what he meant that day.

But 18 months and 20 full-time employees at Digital Press later, I understood.

When you’re building a company, your team, its culture, and the way you perform the work you do are all a reflection of the founder(s).

As the founder, “When everyone is rattled by the storm, you have to bring the calm.”

text:
The most creative company of the last century: 

Pixar. 

Back in 2017, Pixar storyboard artist Matthew Luhn shared their frameworks for Oscar-winning storytelling. 

Here's the breakdown of 4 rules everyone should know to tell captivating stories:

The human brain is a forgetting machine.

After 10 minutes, you'll only remember 5% of the data and statistics you hear. 

But if wrapped in a story, retention jumps to 65%. 

Stories trigger chemical reactions in our brains: 

• Endorphins when characters laugh 
• Cortisol during tense moments 
• Dopamine during victories 

Pixar mastered this "chemical rollercoaster" and it's why their storytelling framework is worth billions.

Here's how they do it:

1. You have 8 seconds to hook an audience. 

Pixar's formula for an irresistible hook: 

• Create unexpected conflict 
• Challenge assumptions 
• Present impossible scenarios 

"What if a rat wanted to be a French chef?"—Ratatouille's hook in under 5 seconds.

The Up opening sequence is a masterclass in emotional manipulation: 

• Set up joy (marriage, dreams)
• Sudden heartbreak (hospital scene) 
• Hope returns (saving for adventure) 
• Final gut punch (wife's death) 

This emotional whiplash makes grown adults cry.

2. The 3-Act Structure 

• Beginning: Set the scene 
• Middle: Introduce conflict
• End: Outcome and transformation 

This has worked for thousands of years because it mirrors life itself.

3. Characters must transform to keep audiences invested. 

• A car learning compassion over winning (Cars) 
• A monster choosing laughter over fear (Monsters, Inc.) 
• A helicopter dad learning to let go (Finding Nemo)

These stories make us ask:

"What would I do in this situation?"

4. Authenticity makes stories universal. 

• Finding Nemo? Written by new fathers. 
• Inside Out? Inspired by a director's struggling teen. 
• Wall-E? Born from an animator's environmental concerns. 

Every Pixar story comes from personal experience.

Or like how Inside Out's director tried forcing happiness on his depressed daughter. 

He turned this parenting mistake into a film about embracing all emotions. 

In the end:

• They'll forget what you said.
• They'll forget what you did.
• But they'll never forget how you made them feel. 

That's Pixar's billion-dollar storytelling secret.

That's it! 

📌 I love studying legendary writers. 

But the fastest way to monetize your writing skills is to become a Premium Ghostwriter.

It's how I went from a broke freelancer to $20k/month in 60 days. 

Get the 5 steps to become a Premium Ghostwriter here: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

document.title: 8 Steps To Becoming A Premium Ghostwriter

posted_at.relative: 7 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
These 8 ghostwriters are making a combined $253k/month. 

But they all started at zero.

Here are the 8 faulty beliefs they fixed to go from broke to rich: 

1. “Nobody can afford my services.”

I promise you:

Millions of people in the world can afford your services—you just haven't found them yet.

But here’s the brutal reason why you believe this:

You think what you’re selling is a lot of money.

It doesn’t matter if it’s $3,000, $4,000, or $5,000—if someone says, “This is too expensive!” you immediately agree with them because you secretly think it yourself.

2. “I need more clients.”

The game isn’t more clients. 

The trick is to get your current clients to pay you more and all new clients to pay you more than you've been charging.

3. “I’ll charge more when I’m more experienced.”

Give me a specific metric of what it means to be more “experienced.” 

Because even if you hit this milestone, you'll still feel inexperienced.

The real issue you're avoiding is why don't you think you deserve to charge more… TODAY.

4. “My niche got saturated. I think it’s time to pivot.”

A writer picks a niche. They land a few clients quickly. They’re a genius!

Then, two or three months later, a couple of those clients churn. 

And immediately the writer thinks the niche is dead and the offer doesn't work anymore. They rush into pivoting their niche.

Neither of these extremes is correct.

• You aren't a genius just because you landed three clients.
• And your business isn't dead just because clients churned.

The skill to build is managing the highs and lows of running a business.

5. “I need to hire someone."

One of the biggest faulty beliefs in business is thinking that once I hire someone, then it'll be easier.

Before you hire, you need to get your one-person business working efficiently and profitably.

6. “If I share too much with the client on the sales call, they won’t need me anymore.”

The fear is that if you give away too much away for free, then no one will want to buy from you. 

But the opposite is true. 

The more you give, the more people trust and buy from you.

7. “I need to build an audience.”

If you're under $20K/month, focus solely on cold outreach and free consulting.

Because that’s the beauty of services. You only need two clients to double your revenue.

Trying to get them through building an audience is playing the game on hard mode.

8. “I can just figure this out on my own.”

You can try and figure it out on your own or you can simply pay for the answer.

I’m not telling you this because you’re not smart or capable. 

I’m explaining this because I don’t want you to hit a brick wall and then throw in the towel because you’re so frustrated.

Pay for the ideal scene and work towards it.

• Digital Writing

text:
This is Greg Hoffman, the marketing genius behind Nike.

His framework made Nike one of the most iconic brands of all time:

Adopted into a white family in 1970s Minnesota, Greg Hoffman faced racial discrimination.

But art and sports became his retreat.

Those experiences fueled his marketing genius in his 27+ years working as Nike's Chief Marketing Officer.

At Nike, he lived by 3 principles:

• Empathy: Seeing through the consumer's eyes
• Curiosity: Seeking unexpected inspiration
• Courage: Taking risks to defy conventions

These guided Nike's marketing to new heights under his leadership. Here's how:

1. "Permission to fail" fueled innovation.

Greg modeled Nike's creative process after Barcelona's tiki-taka soccer: 

• Constant motion
• Seamless teamwork
• Short, quick passes

He encouraged his teams to take risks, like how MJ missed 9,000 shots to succeed.

In 2005, Greg gave a team a bold challenge to make a viral video. 

• Limited time. 
• No budget. 
• No brief. 

The result? 

The legendary Ronaldinho crossbar video. It was the first video to hit 1M views on YouTube.

2. Authenticity was the currency. 

In the 80s, the Air Force 1 was just a basketball shoe. 

But by tapping into the vibrant NYC hip-hop scene, Greg elevated it into a cultural symbol.

3. Rapid Prototyping. 

Seeing is believing. 

So, Greg pushed teams to visualize ideas in 3 days or less. 

This approach led to iconic campaigns like 2007's immersive House of Hoops retail experience.

text:
I have really bad ADD.

Here's how I stay focused while I read:

Underlining and annotating.

I look for hooks and how the writer is moving the narrative forward.

And I ask myself these 5 questions:

1. "What is the author really saying here?"

In the margins, I write what the author is *really* saying below the literal.

"She walked into the room without hesitation." 

OK, what's REALLY happening here? 

The unspoken is the loudest narrative.

2. "How did the author create this image/feeling?"

Don't just watch the movie.

Ask how the writer got you to see or feel what you did. 

Study that language closely. 

Then keep it in your back pocket for when you want to elicit a similar image in your readers' minds.

3. "What details are staying with me the most?"

As you read, and long after you finish reading, notice what ideas, images, traits, and dialogue you remember most.

The fact they are sticking with you means these details are *sticky.* 

How can you make your own writing sticky?

4. "How quickly is the author revealing new information?"

This is the Rate of Revelation.

Slow RoR = lots of description, lots of depth, moves like molasses.

Fast RoR = minimalist, bang-bang-bang, like coffee.

Is the author's choice right for the story?

5. "Why is the author saying this?"

Great writers make every word count.

Nothing is said by accident.

Like a painting that is realized pixel by pixel, question why the author is describing this detail, shining a light on this trait, etc.

Each choice has significance.

-

text:
This surprising habit helped me become a better leader:

(Spoiler: it's not reading more business books)

In 2017, Harvard Business Review published an article titled:

"Why Business Leaders Need To Read More Science Fiction"

So I started reading more sci-fi.

But the genre has more than 100,000 titles.

So, where do you start?

Read these 8:

1. Ender's Game

Fun fact: This is one of the only books I actually read in high school.

Ender's Game is about as close to military game theory in "Web3" as you can get.

2. 1984

George Orwell's most well-known work is also the inspiration behind Apple's groundbreaking ad in 1984, proclaiming the end of mindless following and the great beginning of technology-induced creativity.

3. Fahrenheit 451

Maybe more relevant than ever, Ray Bradbury's totalitarian fiction story paints a grim future where freedom of speech is completely suppressed. 

If you are building technology in this domain, read this book.

4. Ready Player One

This may be the defining sci-fi book of what it might look like to live an entirely digital life, first.

And considering the explosion of NFTs and investments pouring into building "the metaverse," it feels closer than ever.

5. Dune

Dune and its subsequent chronicles were the inspiration for some of the greatest sci-fi stories of our time—including Star Wars.

Every sci-fi fan says it all starts here.

6. Snow Crash

Credited as first coining the term "the metaverse," Snow Crash paints a horrifying picture of a not-so-distant future.

7. A Clockwork Orange

"The Korova milkbar sold milk-plus, milk plus vellocet or synthemesc or drencrom, which is what we were drinking. This would sharpen you up and make you ready for a bit of the old ultraviolence."

That's all you need to know.

8. The War Of The Worlds

H.G. Wells is one of the greatest sci-fi writers to ever touch a pen.

Want to brainstorm what the future might look like amidst a Martian invasion?

Here you go.

Now, I try to read a sci-fi book once a month.

What would you recommend?
