text:
Over the last 10 years, I've built multiple 6 & 7-figure businesses.

And in the beginning, I read hundreds of business books.

But 99% should've been blog posts.

Save yourself the time and read these 10:

1/ Marketing

The vast majority of tactical marketing books become outdated after 1-2 years.

Some, after 6 months.

Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout is some of the most mind-bending and timeless strategic marketing thinking out there.

2/ Growth

Did you know the top 10% of consumers in your category can drive 30-70% of the revenue growth? 

Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon will completely change the way you think about growing your biz.

You don't need "all consumers."

Just Supers.

3/ Motivation

Shoe Dog by Phil Knight, founder of Nike, is one of the most inspiring business stories you'll ever read.

And it all started by selling high-quality, low-cost running shoes from Japan. 

(A style nobody thought was "cool" at the time).

4/ Leverage

The Self-Made Billionaire Effect by John Sviokla & Mitch Cohen is all about how Producers can combine conflicting, opposite ideas at the same time to create massive value.

This is the playbook for thinking like a billionaire.

5/ Wealth & Happiness

The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson is one of my favorite business books I've ever read.

It perfectly reconciles the often conflicting desire to get "really rich" with the desire to get "really happy."

6/ Productivity

I first read Manage Your Day-to-Day by 99U (Behance) back when I was 23 years old.

I still think about it all the time.

Curating productivity work habits from industry thought leaders, this is a playbook for creative thinking.

7/ Copywriting

The Boron Letters by Gary Halbert and his son, Bond, are a classic in the direct response copywriting world.

Far from a formal business book, this is a series of life & copywriting advice letters Gary wrote his son while in jail.

8/ Sales & Offer Creation

$100M Offers by Alex Hormozi is an ultimate guide to upsells.

He set the Clickfunnels record with his $100M+ biz, and this book lays out his strategic thinking in flawless detail.

9/ Hubris

Billion Dollar Loser by Reeves Wiedeman tells, in stunning detail, how "growth at all costs" in Silicon Valley can turn into a nightmare.

Every founder should read this book—and take to heart that "more money" isn't always the answer.

10/ Negotiation

Never Split The Difference by FBI hostage negotiator Chris Voss is an unreal read.

This book will change the way you think about asking for what you want.

11/ Bonus: Category Design

In 2021, Chris Lochhead, Eddie Yoon, and I published The Category Design Toolkit.

This is the seminal book on how to create new categories in the business world—with 15 actionable frameworks to get help you get started.

That's a wrap!

What's your favorite business book of all time?

text:
This man was one of the most influential writers of the 20th century.

His 14 novels sold 20+ million copies for over 50 years.

Here is the secret storytelling technique Kurt Vonnegut used to write some of the bestselling fiction books of all time:

Vonnegut, a top American sci-fi/fantasy writer, wrote 14 novels including, "Slaughterhouse-Five" & "Cat's Cradle." 

His writing made him a vocal critic of American society in the 20th century. 

But he's most famous for his storytelling framework, the "Shapes of Stories":

In his "Shape of Stories" theory, Vonnegut plotted story arcs on a graph.

• The X-axis represents the timeline, from beginning to end.
• The Y-axis charts the protagonist's fortune, from ill fortune to good fortune.

Classic story shapes emerged. 

But how do they work?

In Vonnegut's analysis, the world's most beloved stories follow surprisingly simple shapes.

• Bad to Worse: The poor become poorer 
• Which Way Is Up: Unknown possibilities 
• Cinderella: A rollercoaster story

These are curves that make visualizing a story easy.

Where you begin sets the stage for the arc to come.

• Is your character high on the good fortune scale, like a fairy tale princess?
• Or deep in the ill fortune, like Cinderella at her lowest point?

As the narrative progresses, characters face challenges, setbacks, and triumphs.

These swings in fortune create the peaks and valleys of the story curve. Conflict and climax are the engines that drive the story's shape.

Then, the resolution gives the Story Shape its final form.

• Does your character end on a high note as their problems are resolved?
• Or do they plummet to a tragic ending, undone by flaws?

The end gives your story's shape its lasting impact.

To apply Vonnegut's theory: 

1. Plot your narrative arc.
2. Chart your character's journey from beginning to end.
3. Identify the setup, conflict, climax, and resolution.

Then, refine the shape. Emphasize the peaks and valleys. And craft a satisfying curve.

-
📌 That's it!

text:
I scaled my ghostwriting agency to $2,000,000 in less than 2 years.

But I did it without these 9 things (that everyone says you *must* have):

1. A professional website.

My ghostwriting agency before that signed 25+ clients ($500k+ in revenue) with nothing but cold email outreach.

Our cohort-based writing course, Ship 30 for 30, hit 7 figures in revenue (year 1!) with a Carrd landing page we made in a weekend.

Don't waste time on a website.

2. A social media "manager."

Social media isn't something you "manage." 

It's an Attention Engine. 

With the right system, you can easily batch & create your own content. 

Or, hire a ghostwriter to leverage existing assets & create for you. 

This should be a contractor, not a full-timer.

3. Paid ads.

99% of the time, companies turn on paid ads too early. 

First, you should focus on: 

• Creating an organic marketing flywheel 
• Validating the product offer 
• Gathering testimonials 
• Increasing LTV

Only turn on paid once you've pushed those as far as possible.

4. Funding.

Very few businesses actually require funding. 

Most can be validated by selling:

• Smaller versions of the same thing 
• Digital education about "the thing"
• Pre-selling "the thing" to gauge demand

Founders who say "I need money" are usually lost.

5. A technical co-founder (CTO).

There are 4 forms of leverage (h/t Naval):

• Labor 
• Capital 
• Code 
• Media

If you can't code, chances are a code-dependent product isn't your highest-leverage idea. 

And you'd be better off using a different form of leverage to start.

6. A pitch deck.

Here's an unspoken fundraising rule in Silicon Valley: 

The newbie startups raise funding using pitch decks & presentations.

The elite startups say, "Sorry we're busy building—you want in or no?"

Startups that need money don't get it. 

Startups that don't, do.

7. A "go-to-market strategy."

Here's your GTM Strategy: 

Build in public. 

• Educate others about your category on 𝕏/LinkedIn 
• Share monthly metrics & lessons learned publicly
• Ask people what they want—then build & launch it ASAP 
• Stop overthinking, start shipping

8. Monday morning "standup" meetings.

Most action items don't need to be discussed. 

They need to be delegated & measured.  

Your startup (especially early-stage) doesn't need to "talk" about things. 

It needs to be DOING things.

And most can be done asynchronously.

9. A physical office.

Even if you are selling physical goods, you probably don't need a physical office.

And if you are selling digital products, you definitely don't need one.

So, what does your startup actually need?

• An "offer"
• An LLC/formal entity
• A business bank account
• A way to reach prospective customers
• A way to follow fulfill your offer, consistently

Steps 2 & 3 can be done in an afternoon.

The rest is Entrepreneurship 101.

text:
A simple hack to hook your reader in any piece of writing:

Increase your "Rate of Revelation"—here's how it works:

The Rate of Revelation is how quickly you introduce new information to the reader.

• Low score: 1 idea stretched across hundreds of paragraphs/pages.
• High score: 10+ ideas compressed into a handful of paragraphs/pages.

This post is a great example.

I'm not spending 500 words explaining this framework. 

That would be a very slow Rate of Revelation! 

Instead, I'm explaining the concept in only 126 words.

Why does this matter?

Because in the digital age, people want information fast. 

So you should always be thinking about how quickly you can reveal information to the reader (while also making sure you explain your ideas properly).

text:
9 entrepreneurship clichés that sound smart but are actually wrong:

(I used to fall for these)

1. "The customer is always right."

No, they're not.

You do not want to optimize your business for some rando who doesn't value your product/service/category.

The customers who ARE always right are your Superconsumers.

They spend 30-70% more.

Optimize for them.

--
2. "It's all about Product-Market Fit."

No, it's not.

The moment you decide to try to "fit" into an existing category, you are unconsciously competing for 24% of the economics.

You'll never create your own category.

And will forever be in the existing Category King's shadow.

--
3. "The best brand always wins."

No, it doesn't.

Google is one of the "best" brands in the whole world.

How come Google+ failed?

Because you can't take your BRAND and walk into someone else's category and expect it to matter.

Categories make brands.

Not the other way around.

--
4. "If you're too expensive, customers won't buy from you.

Yes, they will.

Price isn't what causes someone to buy or not buy.

Education is.

The customer has to understand the CONTEXT of the price.

• A photo on the Internet? Free.
• An NFT of the same photo? $758,253

--
5. "Marketing is what you do when your product sucks."

What?

It took humans 300 years to realize a better use case for the wheel was transportation, not pottery.

If the greatest product ever invented couldn't speak for itself, what makes you think your dashboard can?

--
6. "Customers care about your Founder Story."

No, they don't.

They only care if your Founder Story educates them on the transformation THEY CAN EXPECT to experience as well.

You, as the founder, are not the main character.

The customer is.

--
7. "The key to success is work ethic."

No, it's not.

Work ethic matters, but if you are banging away on a product/service/company in someone else's category (or worse, a declining category), how hard you work becomes irrelevant.

You're fighting a losing battle.

Change categories!

--
8. "The best companies are like families."

No, they're not.

The best companies are like championship-winning teams.

• If you can't keep up, you're out.
• If you can't handle your responsibilities, you're out.
• If you can't make the whole team better, you're out.

--
9. "Just focus on shipping a Minimum Viable Product"

Please don't.

Listen to the words in any other context:

• Minimum Viable Wedding Proposal
• Minimum Viable Marriage
• Minimum Viable Children

The last thing you want is to ship customers an MVP.

Make it LEGENDARY.

text:
In 2015, Elon Musk asked the writer Tim Urban to decode his $258 billion net worth.

As the "Wait But Why" writer, Urban was known for dissecting complex topics.

He'd written about everything from EVs to space exploration. 

Six months and 1,000s of hours of research later, Urban found 5 cornerstones to the Elon Universe:

1. First Principles Thinking.

Elon has the superpower to ignore conventional wisdom. He reasons from the ground up based on facts.

When everyone said battery packs were expensive and impossible to scale, Elon used this framework to ask why and found ways to manufacture these at scale.

2. Dream big. Insanely big.

In the past 5 years alone, Elon has: 

• Sent +400 rockets to space
• Accelerated EV by +5 years
• Increased AI Compute Power by 500% 

Elon sees his projects as civilization-level endeavors—which is the burning fire behind his 120+ hour workweek.

3. Create a mission-driven culture.

Urban found Elon’s radical vision and obsession for the greater good of humanity ignited a culture of passionate innovation at his companies that you can’t find anywhere else.

4. Become obsessed with systems.

Elon believes life is intertwined with technology. 

He even described his children as "computers," indicating how he perceives most relationships through logic and utility rather than emotions.

This "systems-based thinking" helps him identify bottlenecks and create efficiencies.

5. Live life like it's a video game.

In a video game, there is:

• No need to protect your identity
• No fear of the opinion of others

Urban found that all great people (like Elon) live life like a video game and "stay hungry, and foolish" (to quote Steve Jobs) at all times.

By writing a deep dive on Musk, Urban gained exclusive access to learn from him directly:

• First Principles Thinking. 
• Dream big. Insanely big.
• Create a mission-driven culture.
• Become obsessed with systems.
• Live life like it's a video game.

These are the cornerstones of Elon's success.

text:
I have built multiple paid newsletters to 6 figures in revenue.

But right now, we are in a newsletter bubble:

• Plummeting open rates
• Fake subscriber counts
• Inboxes flooded with mediocre content 

To avoid this trap, here's my 4-step roadmap on how to start a newsletter:

↳ Step 1: Choose the right platform

Here are the Top 3:

• Substack: For casual creators
• Beehiiv: For advanced/marketing-minded creators
• ConvertKit: For expert-level business-minded creators

There are others, but these 3 are the most creator-friendly.

↳ Step 2: Free vs Paid

Depending on how "entrepreneurial" you want to be, decide if this is a free or paid publication.

• Free = value first, monetize later
• Paid = value is clear first & readers are willing to pay

There's no wrong answer, but you must decide.

↳ Step 3: Choose Timely or Timeless

1. Timely

• Save people time
• In a fast-moving industry
• By sifting through lots of information

• And having the "taste" to present the best stuff

2. Timeless

• Deep expertise
• In a niche
• Ideally that makes $$$

↳ Step 4: Leverage social platforms to drive your first 1K subs

Newsletters have no viral loops.

So you need to drive traffic from social media. 

Primarily:

• X
• LinkedIn
• Instagram

Think of your newsletter as the expanded, more valuable version of your social content.

text:
I journal every single morning for at least 30 minutes.

But on Sunday, I do a deep reflection with these 7 questions:

1. "What was I doing a year ago?"

This helps to keep things in perspective.

Looking back one single year reminds me that I have so much to be thankful for.

2. "Do I enjoy how I'm spending my time?"

If you're not enjoying your day to day, no amount of money or external rewards will make you feel more fulfilled. 

So, question what really makes you feel alive and what feels like a burden. Then, ask how you can remove those burdens in the weeks and months to come. 

3. "Who do I spend the most time with?"

I am such a firm believer that you are a direct reflection of the 5 people you spend the most time with.

Ask yourself if you're spending time with true friends, people who enrich your life, or people who are always asking for more and giving you less.

Your friends should be people who inspire you to become a better version of yourself.

4. "How is my money being spent?"

I see money as fuel. It's what powers your airplane and allows you to do the things you want to do in life.

There are two ways to make more money:

• Work harder and earn 
• Spend less (Consumerism tends to encourage us against the latter)

Do a quick audit of your spending habits, and see if you can control some impulse purchases.

5. "What do I want to learn next?"

Most people reach a plateau when they stop being curious and instead fixate on doing things purely for financial gain—or the approval of others. 

But curiosity never leads you astray. 

Ask yourself:

• What you want to learn next
• What will help you grow beyond where you're at 

And then nurture that curiosity.

6. "How are my 5 buckets being filled?"

Each of us has 5 buckets that we fill with our time:

• Career
• Fulfillment
• Community
• Relationships
• Personal health

It's worth taking time to question (on a regular basis) how each of these 5 buckets is being filled. 

Sometimes, you'll find you are spending far too much time being social and too little time working toward a project or a goal. Or sometimes, you'll find you haven't spoken to your family in months because you've been so busy.

Audit how your time is being spent, so that you can keep those 5 buckets balanced. 

7. "How can I be more helpful—to those around me and to myself?"

You can't spend all your time trying to make everyone around you happy.

You have to prioritize keeping yourself happy too. 

Which is why it's worth questioning both.  

• What can you do to be more helpful to those around you? 
• How can you be more encouraging and more patient? 
• And at the same time, how can you do those same things toward yourself?

Personal development is your ability to continuously ask yourself these questions on a daily basis, and slowly improve over time.

Like water over rocks, who you are is sculpted over years and years.

text:
I wasted years as a horrible writer.

But then I found Tim Ferriss's writing routine and the mental models behind his 5 New York Times best-selling books.

4 mental models you can start using today:

1. Two "crappy pages" per day

Tim sets a low bar to build a consistent daily writing habit.

While writing, Tim thinks, writes, and edits as if he is catching up with an old friend over a few drinks.

This immediately takes the pressure off producing "quality."

2. Voice notes and interviews

When Tim is stuck with a chapter, he hires someone to interview him about the topic.

This thinking out loud forces him to test and refine his core ideas.

3. Fear Setting Exercise

Self-doubt haunted Tim, so he turned to the Stoic philosophy of Seneca, who taught:

“We suffer more in imagination than in reality.”

Tim created a practice to see that he could handle whatever "bad" situation might arise. This lowered the stakes and helped him to start writing.

4. Copywork

To find his voice, Tim copied the styles of journalists he admired, like Chip Heath and Malcolm Gladwell.

• He'd find a piece he admired
• Then rewrite word-by-word digitally or by hand

Stephen King is also a big believer in this practice, too.

One final note:

Tim pitched "The 4-Hour Workweek" to 27 publishers. 

26 rejected him.

But his lucky break came when he met Jack Canfield, author of "Chicken Soup for the Soul."

Jack saw the book's potential and pulled strings to get it sold.

To date, Tim has sold over 2.1 million copies of the book.

The lesson?

Keep trying and putting in the work—you create your own luck.

That's it!

text:
A brutal truth every writer needs to hear:

(I struggled with this for years)

Word count doesn't matter.

Instead, focus on saying what needs to be said.

How many words that takes (in its most potent form) is the right number of words.

Nothing else matters.

Why?

Because word count is a horrible way of measuring value. 

The reader doesn’t care how many words you wrote. What they care about is whether or not you’ve successfully explained the thing you promised or answered their question.

Length would always come up with my clients when I was building my ghostwriting agency.

Clients would ask me if they’d get charged if a piece needed rewrites and it went over the 800-word limit. 

The simple answer was no. 

We wanted to do justice to the piece, so they wouldn’t get charged (unlike the SEO agencies they worked with!). Again, what matters is the value the reader gets from the piece, not how many words you wrote.

Here’s a good rule of thumb:

If you can’t say it in 8 words, you won't be able to say it in:

• 80 words 
• Or 800 words 
• Or 8,000 words

The smallest version of whatever you’re trying to write is always the hardest, but the most concise and clear.

document.title: 4 Big Mistakes Holding Ghostwriters Back

posted_at.relative: 8 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Every writer should save this to their desktop.

Pixar's 22 rules of storytelling:

1. You admire a character for trying more than for their successes.

2. You gotta keep in mind what’s interesting to you as an audience, not what’s fun to do as a writer. They can be very different.

3. Trying for theme is important, but you won’t see what the story is actually about til you’re at the end of it. Now rewrite.

4. Once upon a time there was ____. Every day, ____. One day ____. Because of that, ____. Because of that, ____. Until finally ____.

5. Simplify. Focus. Combine characters. Hop over detours. You’ll feel like you’re losing valuable stuff but it sets you free.

6. What is your character good at, comfortable with? Throw the polar opposite at them. Challenge them. How do they deal?

7. Come up with your ending before you figure out your middle. Seriously. Endings are hard, get yours working up front.

8. Finish your story, let go even if it’s not perfect. In an ideal world you have both, but move on. Do better next time.

9. When you’re stuck, make a list of what WOULDN’T happen next. Lots of times the material to get you unstuck will show up.

10. Pull apart the stories you like. What you like in them is a part of you; you’ve got to recognize it before you can use it.

11. Putting it on paper lets you start fixing it. If it stays in your head, a perfect idea, you’ll never share it with anyone.

12. Discount the 1st thing that comes to mind. And the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th — get the obvious out of the way. Surprise yourself.

13. Give your characters opinions. Passive/malleable might seem likable to you as you write, but it’s poison to the audience.

14. Why must you tell THIS story? What’s the belief burning within you that your story feeds off of? That’s the heart of it.

15. If you were your character, in this situation, how would you feel? Honesty lends credibility to unbelievable situations.

16. What are the stakes? Give us a reason to root for the character. What happens if they don’t succeed? Stack the odds against.

17. No work is ever wasted. If it’s not working, let go and move on — it’ll come back around to be useful later.

18. You have to know yourself: the difference between doing your best & fussing. Story is testing, not refining.

19. Coincidences to get characters into trouble are great; coincidences to get them out of it are cheating.

20. You have to identify with your situation/characters, can’t just write ‘cool’. What would make YOU act that way?

21. What’s the essence of your story? Most economical telling of it? If you know that, you can build out from there.

22. Take the building blocks of a story you don't like. Rearrange them until you have a story you do like.

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Here’s why Quentin Tarantino is the last great screenwriter of this century:

Not because of Kill Bill, Pulp Fiction, or Reservoir Dogs.

But because he found a secret method to irresistible storytelling:

Non-linear Storytelling.

Quentin Tarantino's special technique in arranging scenes helps create suspense and gets viewers hooked.

3 examples, why it works, and how you can master it:

1. Emphasis on Themes.

Kill Bill is split into 2 volumes along theme: 

• Vol 1: Vengeance: The Bride's singular focus to exact revenge.
• Vol 2: Confront: Uncover the emotional and moral complexities of the Bride.

2. Use Flashbacks To Create Curiosity.

In Reservoir Dogs, the story opens with the aftermath of a heist gone wrong.

Flashbacks allow the film to explain the tension between Mr. Orange (undercover cop) and Mr. White.

Mr. Orange is severely wounded but Mr. White refuses to take him to the hospital, believing Mr Orange might report him.

Ultimately, Mr. Orange's gunshot wound proves fatal. 

Flashbacks in Reservoir Dogs are crucial for deepening the audience's understanding of:

• Motivations
• Relationships
• Overall narrative tension 

Tarantino used 3 elements to keep the non-linear narrative tight:

• Strong thematic undercurrents
• Distinctive chapter titles like "The Gold Watch"
• Pop culture-laden dialogue, like the famous "Royale with Cheese" scene

Here's how to master the non-linear story:

1. Identify the emotional high points of the acts.

2. Shuffle events:

• Place high-impact scenes first to hook readers.
• Alternate between scenes to reveal motivations.

3. Use rich, context-laden dialogue to glue the timeline together

Here are 3 tips all storytellers need to hear:

• Focus on the dialogue and themes at all times.
• Add flashbacks for context & perspective shifts.
• Have the end in mind, but be flexible as you write.

text:
How do you make your writing stand out online?

Use "The Tequila Test" — here's how it works:

When differentiating your writing, make a list of all the things most people say about your topic.

For example, if you’re writing about the perfect morning routine, most people would say:

• Wake up early
• Drink coffee
• Stretch
• Etc.

Now, don't use anything on that list.

I call it "The Tequila Test" because if everyone else is saying:

"Stretch, drink coffee, wake up early, take a shower," and you say that too, you’re not DIFFERENT. 

But if you say, "First thing I do is take a shot of tequila..." 

Now you’re saying something DIFFERENT.

So, when writing, ask yourself, "What's my tequila?"

Because if your reader has never seen or heard that before the content is immediately different.

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Little words (that) ruin sentences:

What's the difference between a good sentence and a GREAT sentence?

One of the most interesting “writing rules” I came across in college was Hemingway’s feelings on adverbs.

He hated adverbs.

He called them “lazy writing.”

(Adverbs being any descriptor word ending in -ly: Lazily, Eerily, Honestly, Mostly, etc.)

Learning about this rule changed my writing forever.

And while I still make the mistake of using one-too-many adverbs here and there, I try very hard not to fill my sentences with them. Adverbs are passive ways of saying the same thing the sentence is already saying.

You don’t need them.

But since learning about this rule, and realizing the ways it improved my writing, I started looking for more rules.

And a big one I’ve come across has been the importance of removing “little words.”

• It
• So
• Just
• That
• Well
• Like
• This
• Much
• Often
• Really
• Better
• Usually
• Probably
• Sometimes
• And, and, and

Little words (especially in turns of phrase like “In so forth” or “So much as”) have a knack for killing what could be a great, powerful, concise sentence — and turning it into a soupy mess.

For example:

[Original sentence]: “One of the most common changes that occurs when people go off to college is they become different versions of themselves.”

[Rewrite]: “When people go off to college, they become different versions of themselves.”

Both sentences are saying “the same thing,” and yet the second sentence reads 10x easier than the first.

Why?

We removed all the little words:

• One
• Of
• The
• That
• Is

So, the next time you’re staring at a sentence and can’t quite put your finger on why it’s not reading the way you want it to read, ask yourself: “What little words can I delete here?”

With writing, less is always more.

document.title: 7 Steps To Build A Valuable Skill & Quit Your Job

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Still trying to find your voice as a writer?

Then here's a question for you:

“What's your favorite book on finance?”

Chances are one of these books is going to pop into your head:

• Ramit Sethi’s: I Will Teach You To Be Rich
• Or Ray Dalio’s The Changing World Order
• Robert Kiyosaki’s Rich Dad, Poor Dad
• Morgan Housel’s: The Psychology of Money

Now, it doesn’t matter which title is your favorite, or which one pops into your head first.

These are all finance-related books.

But do they all have the same VOICE?

Not even close.

But you can describe each voice by using the 4A Framework.

The 4A Framework reveals that every non-fiction topic can be categorized into 4 different voices:

• Actionable (Ramit Sethi’s I Will Teach You To Be Rich)
• Analytical (Ray Dalio’s The Changing World Order)
• Aspirational (Robert Kiyosaki’s Rich Dad, Poor Dad)
• Anthropological (Morgan Housel’s The Psychology of Money)

In some way or another, all 4 of these books are writing about finance.

But they are approaching the topic in DIFFERENT WAYS—which is what gives them a very different voice.

As readers, we’re super aware of this.

You ever have a friend say, “Oh you absolutely have to read this book. It’s my favorite!”

And then you pick it up, and you can’t even get through the first page? 

Whenever this happens, if you listen closely, the person usually says:

“Yea it just wasn’t for me. I didn’t like the voice.”

But was it actually the voice?

Not really.

It was just a mismatch in preferences.

Maybe your friend loves Aspirational content, whereas you love Analytical content. Or maybe your friend loves Anthropological content, whereas you prefer Actionable content.

The 4A approach is what dictates the voice and style of writing & content, not the other way around:

1. Actionable

This is a piece of writing that describes HOW TO do something.

• Tips
• Tools
• Hacks
• Advice
• Resources

2. Analytical

This is a piece of writing that describes WHY something works, based on data.

• Trends
• Reasons
• Numbers
• Examples
• Teardowns

3. Aspirational

This is a piece of writing that helps the reader believe that if you (the writer) were able to do it, they can too.

• Lessons
• Mistakes
• Reflections
• Personal stories
• Stories of growth

4. Anthropological

This is a piece of writing that explains the underlying (and usually psychological reasons) why human beings or society as a whole does something.

• Fears
• Failures
• Struggles
• Paradoxes
• Observations

This is how you quickly find your voice as a writer (and not waste 30 years in the process!)

text:
Mark Manson has sold 20 million copies of his 3 NYT best-selling books.

But one day, Will Smith came knocking—here's what happened next:

Will Smith wanted to write a memoir but struggled with the writing process.

Mark Manson, a best-selling author, was seen as a serious contender to be his ghostwriter.

So Will got in touch.

But Mark quickly saw how this would be unlike any other project he'd worked on before:

• Restricted meeting time
• Capturing Will's distinct voice
• Balancing honesty and privacy
• Curating from 1000s hours of footage

But Mark was determined to create something truly "Will Smith."

After an extended interview, Mark spent 3-4 days with Will and his family.

One morning, at breakfast, Mark pitched an outline structuring the book around the emotional themes of Will's life:

• Fear in childhood
• Anger in adolescence
• Pride in adulthood
• Ending with love

Will Smith loved it, so they got to work. 

But Mark only had 30-60 minutes with Will at a time. And to realize the book's vision, Mark needed to know everything.

So over 2 years, Mark was given access to terabytes of personal archives like home videos and press interviews.

Mark visited every key location to recreate descriptions vividly: 

• Will's childhood home in West Philadelphia
• The church Will attended growing up

Like every ghostwriter knows, capturing Will Smith's distinct voice was crucial:

• Bombastic
• Larger-than-life
• Prone to enthusiastic exaggeration

As Mark said, "Whatever Will tells you, you've got to dial it back like three notches to get to the truth."

Mark and Will split the work based on their strengths.

Mark did the:

• Research
• Outlining
• First drafts

Will then revised the drafts to infuse his voice.

"Will" was published in November 2021, and it became an instant New York Times best-seller.

The book is:

• Humorous
• Authentic
• Inspirational

And this experience probably opened up more ghostwriting opportunities for Mark in the celebrity memoir niche.

So, how do you ghostwrite for famous people?

Mark Manson got his name from taking a single blog post and turning it into a best-selling book.

This happened after *years* of writing and testing different ideas.

text:
I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight. 

No fancy journal. No complex rules. 

Just a pen, paper, and 4 simple prompts to help focus my mind and find clarity.

Here's the dead-simple system I use every day:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

---
Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

---
Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

---
Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

---
I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 10 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

posted_at.relative: 9 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I've made $10,000,000+ selling digital products.

How?

By using these 7 simple copywriting phrases:

1. “This {small action} will lead to {giant outcome}.” 
2. "This {massive problem} can be fixed just by doing this {simple solution}."
3. “The {outcome} you want isn’t far away.” 
4. “It's not your fault.”

text:
I used to think all writers died broke.

But then Charles Bukowski's words changed my life forever:

As a German immigrant with a strong accent, Bukowski was bullied at school.

In his 20s, Bukowski worked menial jobs as he wrote hundreds of short stories.

Most got rejected.

Demoralized, he quit writing for nearly a decade.

At 35, a near-fatal bleeding ulcer jolted Bukowski back to the typewriter. Crushed by the pain of reality, Bukowski started writing like his life depended on it.

Over time, Bukowski's work changed millions of lives.

He sold millions of books worldwide and influenced writers like:

• Henry Miller
• Charles Portis 
• Allen Ginsberg

Here are 5 lessons from one of America’s most controversial writers that will change your worldview:

1. Write Because You Have To

"When everything works best it's not because you chose writing but because writing chose you. It's when you're mad with it..."

You have to feel a relentless burning passion inside of you to do your best work.

2. Use Rejection As Fuel, Not Failure

Bukowski's room was wallpapered with his rejection slips.

Each "no" motivated him to write more until the world recognized his talent.

This is why Bukowski "loved" rejection so much.

3. Your Deepest Depths Hold Your Best Work.

Bukowski’s poem "So Now?" reflects on these deep, human themes:

• Resignation to life and death
• Embracing your youth will you still can

4. "You can't write without living and writing all the time is not living."

Bukowski's troubled childhood and later alcoholism served as a source of inspiration.

In the poem "Bluebird" Bukowski uses the bird to symbolize an inner vulnerability he cannot show.

5. Let Yourself Go Crazy. 

"Some people never go crazy. What truly horrible lives they must lead."

Bukowski believed comfort is the worst enemy of creativity.

That's it! Which lesson stood out to you? Let me know in the comments!

-

text:
In 2016, I quit my full-time job to go all in on freelance writing.

Horrible decision.

I had less than 60 days runway left and had zero clients and zero income.

So I reached out to everyone I knew, trying to find clients:

• Family
• Friends
• Ex-colleagues

Then, one day, I got an email back.

The guy who responded had been reading my writing on Quora.

He said he really enjoyed my conversational writing voice, and was wondering if I could help him write thought leadership pieces in a similar tone. 

In his email, he said, “I saw you mention that you do this type of work with other industry leaders—I trust your work as a ghostwriter is confidential?” 

That email was the first time I’d ever heard the term “ghostwriting.”

And within the next 18 months, I scaled my ghostwriting agency to $180k/month.

The lesson here?

I didn't start by pitching people I didn't know.

Instead, I pitched people in my network because they already knew, liked, and trusted me.

So, if you're looking for your first (or next) ghostwriting client, don't just focus on pitching big names in your niche.

You'll have an easier time finding your first client closer to home.

text:
When I was 23 years old, I kept asking my boss for a promotion.

After asking three times, he said:

"You have Early 20s Syndrome.... BAD."

I asked him what that was.

Early 20s Syndrome is a paralyzing obsession with the future, depriving yourself of the present—when being present is what's required to get you to the future you desire.

It's counter-intuitive.

When you're in your 20s, you think obsessing about the future will "get you there faster."

As a result, you:

• Constantly change focus (or jobs, or even careers)
• Constantly chase the next shiny object
• Constantly question where you are "today"
• Constantly compare yourself to everyone else—where they are, how fast they're progressing, etc.

You're exhausted, but you aren't actually moving.

You're running in place.

How do you cure Early 20s Syndrome? 

You have to let go of the future.

Anyone "successful" in their 20s is far less focused on where they're headed, and far more focused on:

• Acquiring skills
• Getting in the reps
• Practicing good habits
• Obsessively improving 1% each day

Because the only way "Future You" comes to be is if "Present You" puts in the work.

Which means every minute you spend daydreaming, wondering, questioning "Future You," that version of yourself gets further and further away.

Whereas every minute you spend working on "Present You," the closer and closer you get to your imagined "Future You" self.

Until enough days pass.

And enough skills are acquired.

And suddenly, "Future You" is You, today.

The secret to earning more $$$ from your email marketing:

(I've made over $10,000,000 with this)

Sending more emails.

Now, I know what you're thinking:

“If I send too many emails, people are going to see it as spammy. They’ll unsubscribe. They’ll never buy anything from me!”

But after enrolling 10,000+ students in Ship 30 for 30 and making millions of dollars through email marketing, here's the rule:

The more emails you send, the more money you make. The less emails you send, the less money you make.

So here's how I construct every email campaign I send:

Part 1: Education

These emails are intended to do 2 very specific things:

• Show the customer you are the right person to help them.
• Remind them you have a product/service for sale.

These emails should be 99% free value + 1% “Oh, by the way, you can sign up here” CTA at the end.

Part 2: Sales

You’ve given them a TON of free value with Part 1. So here, you SHOULD hard pitch people on your list.

And the goal should be:

• Buy
• Or Unsubscribe

You do not want people sitting on your list doing nothing. 

This second part is a good way of figuring out who is actually going to buy from you and who was never going to buy in the first place.

So if you are about to launch:

• A book
• A new service
• A digital product
• A cohort-based course
• A group coaching offer

And you want to increase sales, here's the trick:

Send more emails.

text:
Everyone says the greatest writers are:

Stephen King, Henry David Thoreau, or Virginia Woolf.

But this genius is head and shoulders above them all.

Here’s the one secret technique Ernest Hemingway used to become a legendary writer:

Imagine an iceberg floating in the ocean.

Only about 1/8 is visible above the waterline.

This was Hemingway's philosophy for great writing.

Earlier in his life, as a young journalist, Hemingway learned to pack maximum meaning into minimal words.

He was fascinated by the Cubism art movement pioneered by Picasso in the early 1900s.

Cubism focused on using fractured pieces to represent subjects in an abstract manner.

• Minimalism
• Multiple angles
• Limited color palette (focus on form, not color)

Like Hemingway's Iceberg theory, Cubism creates a canvas for the viewer's imagination.

The Iceberg Theory, at its core, is:

"Show, don't tell."

Let readers infer the meaning behind the scene and character by giving as little information as possible.

As the story progresses, predictability reduces the need for explanations.

Hemingway's "Hills Like White Elephants" is a masterclass in the Iceberg Theory.

It is an entire conversation about an impending abortion without the word ever being mentioned. The characters' tension and despair bubble up from beneath the surface of mundane dialogue.

(I dropped a copy in the comments, take a look!)

In many ways, the Iceberg Theory mirrored Hemingway's own struggles.

As a WWI veteran, he grappled with trauma he could not openly discuss. Like an iceberg, a lot of this was hidden from view.

So, what can modern writers learn from the Iceberg Theory?

1. Avoid excessive backstory.

↳ Hint at character pasts through brief flashbacks or dialogue.

2. Use sensory details sparingly.

↳ A few vivid descriptions can set the scene.

3. Trim the fat.

↳ Cut any word, sentence, or scene that doesn't serve the story.

For example, Hemingway once said his best work was a story he wrote in just 6 words:

"For sale: baby shoes, never worn."

It's the ultimate example of the Iceberg Theory in action. Great writing is concise, to the point, and with no wasted words.

That's it!

text:
I built the first ghostwriting agency for founders and executives and have sold over $5,000,000 worth of ghostwriting services.

But if you’re at the very beginning of your ghostwriting journey, it can be difficult to know who needs a ghostwriter.

So here are 3 questions to ask when you're looking for your first (or next) client:

• Question 1: Does this person have a business? 

If they don’t have a business, or don’t have anything for sale, it really doesn’t matter how many followers they have. 

They can’t rationalize paying you because they don’t have a cash-generating machine.

• Question 2: How long has this person been in business? 

The longer, the better. 

Not to say you can’t help new business owners, but whenever you see someone has been in business for a while, that means they’ve been making SOME money for a while. 

Which means they can rationalize hiring you.

• Question 3: On a scale of 1-10, how “digital” is this business already?

A mistake many ghostwriters make is they try to go after people who are already doing so many of the “digital” things right. 

They’ll pitch Ali Abdaal and tell him why his funnel isn’t working properly.

People do this to me all the time too. They’re like, “Your emails could be converting more people.” 

And I’m like, no they couldn’t. Because either I wrote the emails, or someone on our team wrote the emails, who I trained. 

So you don’t want to pitch people who are already 8, 9, 10/10 on the digital stuff. 

What you’re looking for are businesses that are 5/10 or below.

Because it’s SO much easier to help someone get going, rather than pitch someone who's already doing everything right. 

Even if you can help them, it’s going to be incremental at best.

One final point:

A lot of ghostwriters will ask me, “What’s the best niche? Where will I make the most money? Which industry?” 

The truth is: 

They all work. 

And a helpful reframe is that: if it wasn’t making money, it wouldn’t be an industry. The mere fact it EXISTS means money is floating around in there. 

So if that’s an industry you know a lot about, why can’t you be the go-to ghostwriter for that industry? 

You can. 

You just have to allow yourself to see the opportunity, instead of constantly looking over the fence at what your neighbor has going on, thinking the grass is greener over there. 

It’s not. 

Every industry and every niche has its own set of challenges.

text:
99% of creators and writers get personal branding wrong.

It's not about:

• Doing viral TikTok dances
• Posting pictures of what you ate for breakfast
• Keeping your feed up-to-date with your latest selfie

You shouldn’t be aiming to become known in “general.”

Instead, aim to become known for a niche (or category) that you own.

For example, I don't want to just be known as "Nicolas Cole." I want to become known for my association with Digital Writing.

When someone asks:

"Who should I follow to understand how to be a writer in the digital age?"

I want the answer to this question to be me, Nicolas Cole. That's why I'm building my personal brand.

The same goes for your story.

All over the internet, you’ll hear marketing “influencers” explain how the single most important thing for your personal brand is to share your story.

But your story, by itself, does not matter. It only matters when it serves as an example to your reader or customer.

And the only purpose of providing an example is to drive home a point and explain something that matters to them, the customer.

It has very, very little to do with you.

text:
I spent 300+ hours studying the fiction industry:

• How to make $$$ writing stories
• What works & what doesn't
• How to build an audience

Here are the 7 steps to become a money-making fiction writer:

Step 1: Don't try to write a novel. Start by writing shorter stories.

Novels take too long in the beginning.

The feedback loop is too slow. Trying to write some epic 800-page work right out the gate is too daunting.

So start by writing shorter stories.

Step 2: Play in a smaller, less competitive sub-category.

Almost every fiction writer I know wants to start out in a mega-category.

But it's very hard for new authors to break into categories like:

• Mystery
• Horror
• Science Fiction

An easier place to start is by picking a sub-category within one of these mega-categories.

For example, you don’t want to be a “sci-fi writer," you want to niche down and be a "Viking Sci-Fi” writer.

Step 3: Treat Amazon as your primary publishing platform.

If you truly want to make a living writing fiction, then ramp up the volume on Amazon. 

A lot of the top fiction writers on Amazon don't even have big social followings.

They just pump out books on Amazon consistently for years.

Step 4: Build an organic following on X, LinkedIn, and Medium.

Build an audience by writing non-fiction ABOUT how you write fiction.

For example:

• "3 Tricks To Write Better Dialogue (According To John Grisham)"
• "Create Memorable Characters in 15 Minutes"
• "How To Hook Your Reader: 5 Proven First Page Techniques"

This is more effective than trying to write fiction on social platforms.

Step 5: Find your Royal Road and get some early feedback.

There are free platforms where people publish web novels and serial fiction all the time.

Many of the most popular LitRPG books, for example, started for free on Royal Road then went to Amazon later. Same with Wattpad and romance novels.

The strategy here is to get FEEDBACK.

Step 6: Build an email list and/or a Discord.

The 2 most valuable assets you can own as a fiction writer are:

• An email list
• And/or a Discord server

Why? 

Because these are direct lines to your readers.

An email list lets you:

• Announce new releases directly to fans
• Share behind-the-scenes content
• Run exclusive promotions or pre-orders

A Discord server allows you to:

• Create a community around your work
• Get instant feedback on ideas
• Host live Q&As or writing sessions

Make it easy for you to get access to your readers and for your readers to get access to you (and your books).

Step 7: Monetize with "side-quest content" on Patreon.

Lastly, create a Patreon.

Fill it with content you don't cover inside your novels or short stories, but are related to a series or world you're building. 

• Planet maps
• Backstories of characters
• Word definitions or dictionaries

That's it!

text:
A decade ago, I was a broke copywriter living in a run-down studio apartment in Chicago.

But today, I run a portfolio of writing businesses doing over $6,000,000 in annual revenue.

Want to make money as a writer?

You need to hear these 8 brutal truths: 

Brutal Truth 1: If you want to earn a full-time living as a writer, you have to work twice as hard as everyone else to get there.

You need to invest as much of your extra time as possible into practicing your craft. 

Brutal Truth 2: If you want to become a successful non-fiction writer, you need to become a world-class marketer. And if you want to become a successful fiction writer, you need to become relentlessly prolific.

• Non-fiction writers: you need to invest heavily in becoming a world-class marketer (and ideally building a business that can fuel your marketing efforts).

• Fiction writers: You need to do whatever you have to do to write and publish a LOT of fiction. If you don’t, good luck achieving your goals.

Brutal Truth 3: Making money exclusively as a “legacy writer” is unnecessarily hard.

You unlock so much more upside when you change the definition of “what it means to be successful” from:

“I can only write books” to “I can monetize my writing in different ways.”

This can be:

• Courses
• 1:1 coaching
• Paid newsletters
• Services, like ghostwriting 

Brutal Truth 4: You can’t measure success over days. You have to measure it over decades. 

To “make it” as a writer, you’re not playing a year-long game. You’re playing a decade-long game—ideally a multi-decade long game. 

Brutal Truth 5: Writing is a lottery, so optimize for volume. 

This is true for pretty much every creative industry because you never know what’s going to resonate.

So stop trying to plan it all out:

• The more shots you take, the more likely you are to succeed.

• The less shots you take, the less times you spin the lottery wheel, the less likely you are to succeed.

Let your ideas rip. The chips will fall where they fall.

Brutal Truth 6: Reading doesn’t make you a better writer. Writing makes you a better writer.

The way you get better at writing is BY WRITING.

It's that simple.

Brutal Truth 7: There’s no such thing as “good” or “bad” writing. Only effective and ineffective writing. 

Good and bad are subjective.

Instead, a clearer aspiration is to be an effective writer:

• Are you able to speak to your target reader?
• Do they see themselves in your writing?
• Can you reach, help, or entertain them? 

Because if you can, you are a great writer.

Brutal Truth 8: If you aren’t achieving your goals as a writer, it’s your fault.

This is an empowering statement, not a demoralizing one.

Instead of pointing the finger, pull the thumb. Ask yourself how you need to improve.

Then you have a path forward.

Want to watch my full video on these 8 brutal truths?

Then head on over to my YouTube channel.

Watch it here: https://lnkd.in/eeYAGc7H

text:
Everyone talks about legendary writers like Hemingway, King, or Thoreau.

But there is a British author who redefined the writing game forever.

Here’s the secret technique J. K. Rowling used to write one of the best-selling book series of all time:

At 32, J.K. Rowling was a single mom on welfare, struggling to make ends meet. 

One day, while stuck on a delayed train, the character Harry Potter came to her fully formed.

And she spent the next 5 years meticulously planning the 7-book series before writing a single word.

Today, Harry Potter is the best-selling book series of all time (closely followed by Goosebumps).

Here's a breakdown of the 10-column outline method she used to create Harry Potter:

Rowling's 10-column outline was divided into 2 key areas:

1. Progress & Progression (Time & Chapters) 
2. Plot & Characters (Main Plot, Subplots, Characters)

By focusing tightly on all these interconnected elements, she kept readers engaged and the story moving forward.

Each chapter in the series features 3+ interwoven subplots:

• The central mystery (e.g. The Chamber of Secrets)
• Character development (e.g. Ron's insecurities)
• Hogwarts life (e.g. Quidditch matches)

These subplots add depth to each chapter.

In Order of the Phoenix, Rowling streamlined her story:

• 1 main plot (The Prophecy & Voldemort's return)
• 5 subplots (Umbridge, Dumbledore's Army, etc.)
• Under 10 key characters

To make sure she never missed a key plot point, Rowling did a detailed outline.

And to add more complexity, Rowling's narrative over the 7 books spans 3 generations:

1. Harry, Ron, & Hermione
2. James, Lily, & the Marauders 
3. Dumbledore & Voldemort's origins

Each layer builds on the others, creating a dense wizarding world that feels real to the reader.

Rowling's consistent structure provided a roadmap and built anticipation for the series' climactic finale.

• 1 school year at Hogwarts
• 1 central mystery to solve
• 5 subplots intertwine with the main story

That's it!

Rowling's 10-column outline can be any writer's Marauder's Map:

1. Time (When does the story take place?)
2. Chapter (How will you structure your tale?)
3. Plot (What's the main narrative throughline?)
4. Subplots (How will you add complexity?)
5. Characters (Who will drive your story?)

Every storyteller should learn from Rowling's approach:

• Craft a compelling premise
• Develop a detailed outline
• Interweave subplots to add depth
• Create layered generational stories
• Narrow the focus to intensify drama

I love studying the art of writing.

It's how I went from a broke freelancer to $20k/month in 60 days.

text:
A faulty belief *killing* your earning potential as a writer:

(Beating this is how I hit $20k/month as a ghostwriter)

"Cold outreach is dead!"

Trust me, it's not.

When I was building my ghostwriting agency, I'd spend 1 hour every day doing cold outreach. 

80% of our deals came from cold outreach. Getting clients this way is how I scaled my agency to $180k/month.

So, what's the secret to successful cold outreach?

Relentless follow-up.

Here are the 5 steps to master the art of follow-up as a ghostwriter:

Step 1: Kill the idea that following up is “annoying.” 

This is one of the biggest faulty beliefs you have to break as a writer. 

As Alex Hormozi says, people need to be reminded more than they need to be taught. 

The same applies to cold outreach—people need to be reminded more than they need to be sold to (at first).

Step 2: Give them a gentle nudge. 

The go-to line here is: “I’m sure you’ve been swamped.”

The people who need ghostwriters are VERY busy, so empathize with their situation. it shows you understand what they’re up against and signals that you work with people like them all the time.

Step 3: Give them something extra. 

• Noticed a trend in their industry that you think they’ll find useful? Let them know. 

• Found an idea that expands on your initial outreach? Shoot them a quick message. 

They’ll be grateful for the free help!

Step 4: Keep it short and give a clear CTA. 

You’re trying to reduce the mental load on the potential client as much as possible. 

So keep it short and give a clear Call To Action (e.g. “Shall we talk about this next week?”). 

This makes it easier for them to say, “Yes, let's do it!” 

Step 5: Follow up every 4-7 days (and never stop).

This is the real secret to follow up. 

Never. Stop. Trying. 

Inside PGA, I've seen ghosts get new clients after 12 months of follow-up. 

Yup, that’s right, 12 months! 

It doesn’t always take that long, but people need to be educated on what you provide (which is what good follow-up is all about) and this takes time.

text:
How many emails does it take to convert someone from a free subscriber to a paying customer?

It has taken us:

• 20 cohorts of Ship 30 for 30
• Over 10,000 students enrolled
• And millions of dollars generated in revenue

But we have finally cracked the code.

It can take up to *20* emails to get a subscriber to purchase your product.

And this 20-email sequence is split into 2 parts:

Part 1: Education

These emails are intended to do 2 very specific things:

• Show the customer you are the right person to help them.
• Remind them you have a product/service for sale.

These emails should be 99% free value + 1% “Oh, by the way, you can sign up here” CTA at the end.

Part 2: Sales 

The second part of this 20-email sequence are emails intended to do the opposite of the first 10 emails.

You’ve given them a TON of free value. So here, you SHOULD hard pitch people on your list.

And the goal should be:

• Buy
• Or Unsubscribe

You do not want people sitting on your list doing nothing. 

But so many people jump to the second part and get poor sales as a result. 

Why?

Because a lot of business owners don't understand why people aren't buying.

It's not usually due to price or credibility.

99% of the time it's an education deficit.

text:
In 2018, I dropped $6,000 on a book I didn’t even want.

Why?

To try and land my biggest ghostwriting client to date.

Here’s the story of how I helped one man become a New York Times best-seller (and discovered an ugly truth about the publishing industry):

Back in 2018, I had a ghostwriting client who ran an events business.

He decided that becoming a best-selling author would boost his “credibility” (and so scale his business).

He got a book deal, and a year away from launch, told me was determined to pull out all the stops to make sure he hit the list.

Little did I know this would involve me.

One day, he came to me with an offer:

“Cole, I love you and, you know I love your agency, so I have an introduction that would be really impactful for your business... but I would like something in return.”

“OK, shoot,” I said.

“I know the CMO of one of the largest technology companies in the world. This is a multi-billion dollar company. I'll introduce you directly to him and I will put in such a good word that it's almost guaranteed they will want to work with you….

But I want you to buy 300 of my books.”

At the time, ordering 300 books at $20 a piece was no small amount of money for my agency.

It would cost us $6,000.

But I had a feeling this would pan out and I liked him. He’d been a good client and I wanted to help him become a NYT best-seller.

“Alright, I'm game.”

So I forked out $6k and ordered 300 copies of the book.

(By the way, the intro he made became our biggest client and would pay us a FULL YEAR in advance.)

When the book launched, 300 copies of this guy's book showed up at my door.

Box after box after box piled up, all across my house.

“What am I gonna do with all of these books?!”

I gave them away to everyone I knew and when people came over, I’d be like “Hey, I have some extra books, why don’t you take one?”

But did my client his the list?

On the week of the launch:

• Every interview he’d done dropped
• Every article we’d written was published
• And every influencer in his network promoted his book

And the week after, he hit the NYT bestseller list.

Then, one week later, the book completely fell off the charts. 

The book made him zero money:

• He spent tons of money getting his team to promote the book.

• He called in every favor possible (and had to return all those promises he made, costing him money).

Getting the New York Times as a badge probably cost him $250,000+.

I realized that becoming a best-selling author is not about who writes the best book.

It's about who's willing to do the most work on the marketing side and spend the most money. Becoming a New York Times best-seller is 100% just a marketing game and a budget game. 

The whole experience changed my perspective on whether I should aim to be a best-seller.

There are situations where having the badge is valuable.

But if you get the badge, but nobody reads the book, does it even matter?

posted_at.relative: 10 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The easiest way to 10x your writing:

(Without spending hours editing & rewriting)

Formatting.

But why is it important?

Because it makes your writing:

• Easier to skim
• Easier to quote & share
• And easier for readers to “remember” and repeat to their friends

Here's a dead-simple, 4-step method to format your writing:

Step 1: Start every section with a single-sentence opener.

When you open each area of your work with a single sentence, you:

• Keep readers interested
• Make things easier to read
• Subconsciously encourage them to continue

Here are the 6 most effective openers:

• Open with a moment in time
• Open with a vulnerable statement
• Open with a controversial opinion
• Open with a weird, unique insight
• Open with a thought-provoking question
• Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence

These are my go-to proven ways of hooking the reader.

--
Step 2: Always look for opportunities to turn long paragraphs into bulleted lists.

If you are:

• Listing anything, ever, or,
• Rambling off a dozen quick examples, or,
• Making a series of distinct points to drive home a point

...make it into a bulleted list.

(Like this!)

--
Step 3: Where are your subheads?

Subheads are bolded sentences that help the reader more easily follow your line of thinking.

• They split the page up evenly
• They offer big, bolded “milestones”
• They help the reader identify where they’re going

Subheads give clarity.

--
Step 4: Use the 1/3/1 writing rhythm.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

You can also use:

• 1/4/1
• 1/5/1
• 1/2/5/2/1
• Etc.

The key to injecting skimmability into your writing is alternating the length of sentences and sections.

--
In all your formatting choices, your primary concern should always be:

"How can I make this easier for the reader?"

• Is a bulleted list easiest? Do that.
• Is a step-by-step guide easiest? Do that.
• Is a series of small, rapid-fire sections easiest? Do that.

Your North Star is the reader's enjoyment.

---

• Skills
• Experience
• And understanding of their specific industry

You’re subtly explaining why it’ll be MORE work and effort to find someone with your particular skill set at a lower price point.

Remember, your goal is to position yourself as a premium service provider NOT compete with the lowest bidders in the marketplace (who spend all their time fighting over gigs on Upwork and Fiverr).

text:
We are in a newsletter bubble:

• Plummeting open rates
• Fake subscriber counts
• Inboxes flooded with average content

But I've built multiple newsletters to 6 figures in revenue:

1. Category Pirates which became a top five business paid newsletter on Substack in less than a year.

2. Write With AI hit six figures in its first 18 months and recently crossed $200,000 in annual recurring revenue.

There are really only two types of newsletters that I've seen have the ability to go paid:

1. Short & Curated

Newsletters in a fast-moving category that compress a ton of information on a specific subject into a quick read.

Examples:

• Morning Brew: “Become smarter in just 5 minutes.”

• The Hustle: “Get the 5-minute newsletter keeping 2M+ innovators in the loop.”

• Robinhood Snacks: “The 3-minute newsletter with fresh takes on the financial news you need to start your day.”

2. Long & Original Thinking

Newsletters in a dense, thoughtful category that provide in-depth insight, analysis, and unique information on a specific subject.

Examples:

• Category Pirates: “The authority on Category Creation and Category Design.”

• Stratechery: “Stratechery provides analysis of the strategy and business side of technology and media, and the impact of technology on society.”

• The Generalist: “Every Sunday, we send you a free email unpacking one of the most important companies in tech and crypto. You’ll understand how these businesses work, why they matter, and what it means for you.”

Everything else falls into “Hey! Subscribe to my fun newsletter where I share my learnings along the way!” territory.

And, to be blunt: 

Nobody reads these. 

Because what it’s offering you isn’t “worth the read.” 

The best newsletters either save you 1,000 hours of annoying work “finding” everything you’re looking for, or save you 1,000 hours of trial and error by accelerating your knowledge in a particular category.

So make your choice between these types of newsletter.

text:
The client objection every writer dreads:

(Overcoming this helped me make $180k/mo as a ghostwriter)

"This is too expensive, what you're proposing isn't worth it."

Ouch.

What the client is really doing here is asking you to defend your pricing.

So hold your ground and don't discount (at least not right away!)

Instead, when you’re faced with price objections, here’s what to do:

• Step 1: Don't immediately lower your price or get defensive.

• Step 2: Do some napkin math to help the client understand the potential return on investment (ROI) from working with you.

Find the price of their product or service. Work out how many they'd need to sell to get their investment in you back. Then explain how having this asset will help them sell more.

This will make it easy for the client to agree to your price.

Because if they don't agree, they wouldn’t have this asset.

Handling an objection like this is how I went from earning $0 as a struggling freelance writer to making $180k/month as a Premium Ghostwriter in less than 2 years.

text:
I'm convinced becoming a prolific writer comes down to one skill:

(Here's an AI prompt to help you get started)

Testing ideas in public and doubling down on proven data points.

Here's how it works:

Step 1: Choose a topic you want to write about.

Step 2: Get specific within that topic to come up with a "headline."

• Pick 1 problem inside that idea
• Get more specific on that problem
• Do it again and again until you can't anymore

Here's an example:

V1: “I want to write about real estate.”

V2: “I want to write about buying your first rental property.”

V3: “I want to write about what questions to ask current owners of rental properties to figure out whether it’s a good deal or not.”

See where I'm going?

Step 3: Create “lists” around your headline.

Let’s say your headline is “Teach beginner writers the fundamentals of copywriting so they can sell their first digital product.”

Your list would look like:

1. Don't sell. Help the customer transform.
2. Ditch formal language. Be simple.
3. Be a painkiller, not a vitamin.
4. Speak to 1 person—not "everyone."
5. Emphasize benefits, not features.
6. Share 1 shocking stat.
7. Be clear, not clever.

Add an intro to this list, and you have a social post.

This is what I call an “Outline As Content.”

And you don’t have to just give the reader a list of tips.

You can use any of the 10 Magical Ways to create your headline and outline:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

Publish each piece as its own “Outline.”

Then onto the final step:

Step 4: Double down on what works.

Did one post get more likes, comments, views, etc. than all the others?

That's signal to double down on that idea and talk about it more.

You can either expand each point within it or use all the points in the list in a long-form piece.

This is the Lean Writing Method in action.

To help you quickly create these "Outlines As Content", here's an AI prompt to help:

== Start Of Prompt ==

I am going to train you to write LinkedIn short-form content (1000 characters or less).

Here are the rules:

1. It must open with a strong declarative sentence.

2. 3-5 succinct bullets organized in a list

3. Do not use hashtags and emojis. Ever.

I am going to give you the headline and you are going to write a short-form piece based on that headline.

Do you understand?

== End Of Prompt ==

The output won't be perfect, but you can use this as a starting point.

That's it!

Now you can create these "Outlines As Content" to test your ideas with faster than ever before.

Want more AI writing prompts?

Here are 5 of my favorite (proven) ChatGPT prompts to get you started: https://lnkd.in/gQSrC3F5

text:
This journalist created 007 but never lived to see James Bond become a $7 billion empire:

His wartime exploits inspired the British icon, and his spy operations changed WWII history.

Here's the untold story of Ian Fleming:

• Born May 28th, 1908, to a wealthy London family
• Attended Eton, a prestigious English public school
• Become editor of the school magazine "The Wyvern"

But privilege wouldn't prepare him for the dangerous world he'd later find himself in:

In the early 1930s, Fleming worked as a Reuters journalist in Moscow, where he covered the show trial of six British engineers.

But then the outbreak of WWII changed everything.

Fleming became personal assistant to Naval Intelligence Director John Godfrey, who later inspired Bond's boss "M."

He was surrounded by spies and intelligence officers who shaped Bond’s character:

The Yugoslav double agent Duško Popov was one of several real-life inspirations for 007’s exploits.

Then, in 1943, he helped plan "Operation Mincemeat":

• Planted fake Greece invasion plans on a body
• Germans fell for it
• Troops moved away from Sicily—the Allies' actual target

Fleming visited Jamaica during WWII while working in naval intelligence and he developed a deep attachment to the island.

After the war in 1946, Fleming bought land in Jamaica and built his retreat, "Goldeneye."

It was here, at the age of 44, that Fleming wrote the first Bond novel "Casino Royale" in 1952.

By 1954, Bond had been turned into a TV show.

The first on-screen bond wasn't Sean Connery but American actor Barry Nelson.

He played “Jimmy Bond” in a CBS TV special—8 years before Dr. No.

It flopped.

But Fleming’s storytelling wasn’t just limited to spy thrillers.

In 1964, he wrote 'Chitty-Chitty-Bang-Bang' for his son, Caspar.

The magical flying car later became a famous film—with a screenplay co-written by Roald Dahl. 

Fleming's personal life was as complicated as any spy novel:

• A heavy smoker and drinker
• Troubled marriage with Ann Charteris
• His messy personal affairs influenced Bond's relationship problems

In 1964, he suddenly died of a heart attack at just 56.

Fleming saw Sean Connery bring Bond to life in the first two films—but he never lived to see 007 become a global phenomenon.

Fleming's secret to creating a cultural icon?

Turning real experiences into fiction.

Bond became his alter-ego, and the villains were based on people he'd encountered.

Years of writing in the intelligence community taught him how to write in different voices and contexts.

Luckily, if you want to get paid to write and practice your craft, you don't have to become a spy.

Instead, become a ghostwriter.

I've ghostwritten for 300+ industry leaders, including CEOs, Silicon Valley Founders, and NYT best-selling authors.

text:
I write 10,000 words every single day.

My secret?

I follow this dead-simple, 4-step routine:

Step 1: Prep The Morning Writing Session The Night Before

Every night, I take 10-15 minutes to get clear on what I'm going to wake up and write first thing in the morning. 

As the saying goes: 

"Failure to prepare means preparing to fail."

Step 2: Write The Hardest Thing, First

Whatever is on my list that is going to take the most mental bandwidth, I do it first thing in the morning. 

That way, my "writing tasks" get easier as the day goes on—not harder.

Step 3: Always Prep The Page

I never sit down and just "start writing." 

Instead, I Prep The Page with:

• A working title
• Section headers
• Sub-section headers

Then, once I have everything laid out, I'll start brain-dumping notes inside each section/container. 

And I won't "start writing" until I have 90% of the chess pieces out in front of me, on the page.

Step 4: Choose Your Writing Blocks

These are my 3 writing "blocks" each day:

• 7 am to 8:30 am
• 9:30 am to noon
• 1 pm to 3 pm

And then, if I've got some energy left in the tank, I'll do one last writing session 7 pm to 9 pm.

For context, I've been writing on a daily basis for over 10 years. 

And it has taken me years to get up to this level of output. 

The biggest leap I saw in my daily writing process came when I quit my job to become a full-time ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I got paid to practice my writing craft. And now writing is all I do.

I ghostwrote for 300+ industry leaders, including CEOs, NYT best-selling authors, & Grammy award-winning musicians.

text:
After coaching over 10,000 writers, I've found the bottleneck to mastery:

It isn't what you know.

It's your commitment and work ethic.

Here's a simple 4-step framework for mastery anyone can use:

Step 1: Do you know the right thing to work on?

If not, who do you need to talk to and/or what do you need to read/consume to know?

Step 2: Do you know the right thing to work on but aren't doing it?

If not:

• What new habits do you need to build?
• What distractions do you need to get rid of?
• What luxuries do you need to deprive yourself of?
• What escapes & coping mechanisms do you need to avoid?

Step 3: Are you doing the right thing but could do it better?

If so:

• Where do you need to live?
• What habits do you need to improve?
• What community do you need to join?
• What techniques do you need to learn?
• What "teacher" do you need in your life?
• Who do you need to learn from/surround yourself with?

Step 4: Are you doing the right things, as best as you can do them, and are just waiting for time to catch up?

If so, optimize for consistency, keep showing up, get the reps in, and focus on falling in love with "monotony" until it's clear you've plateaued.

Then start back at Step 1 again.

text:
How Game of Thrones was created is WILD:

• Multiple plots
• No single hero
• No clear villains

But the TV show won 59 Emmys and held the World Record for "Most pirated TV show" for years.

I studied its storytelling and psychology tricks.

Here's what I found:

George R.R. Martin didn’t just write multiple plots—he made four unstoppable forces clash:

• Power struggle over the Iron Throne
• The Stark-Lannister war
• The White Walker threat
• Rise of Daenerys

The brilliance was in how these plots connected.

While most writers struggle with 1-2 storylines, GOT made its complexity an advantage.

Every major moment in one plot created ripple effects in all others.

Instead of following predictable fantasy structures, they mastered emotional timing.

Just when you're deeply invested in Jon Snow’s journey at The Wall—cut to chaos in King’s Landing.

This relentless pacing kept audiences hooked and redefined fantasy TV storytelling.

Each subplot has its own structure, but their major plot points overlapped.

When Ned Stark is executed:

• The Stark-Lannister war explodes
• Jon Snow almost deserts the Night's Watch
• Power dynamics shift everywhere

This storytelling magic can be summed up with this framework:

1. Give each plot its own complete structure
2. Make their key moments overlap
3. Let each plot affect all others
4. Show ripple effects across storylines

But most writers follow the "Hero's Journey":

• 1 clear goal
• 1 main character
• 1 central conflict

GoT proved breaking these rules is more engaging.

Just when you pick a side, they show you the other perspective.

This created a unique viewing experience:

• Every scene & detail mattered
• Every character's decision has consequences
• Every plot twist affected multiple storylines

The result shook the entertainment industry:

• It broke Emmy records with 59 wins
• Over 400,000 people shared a single episode simultaneously
• The Season 8 premiere was pirated 54 million times in 24 hours

They didn’t just sell "fantasy TV movies."

They crafted an interconnected universe that invested audiences in characters, narratives, and cliffhangers.

They mastered *real* storytelling.

And great storytelling is everywhere.

The biggest brands—Nike, Apple, Patagonia—don’t just sell products.

They sell narratives that make people *feel* something.

And every single industry leader needs a writer to tell their story on the internet:

More than ever before, people need to build personal brands online.

So, the demand for ghostwriters is going through the roof.

They don't just write—they turn ideas into stories that build brands and businesses.

How do I know?

Because ghostwriting is how I went from a broke freelance writer to making $20k/month in less than 60 days.

I wrote for industry leaders & got paid to practice my writing.
