posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I wasted years as a horrible writer. But then I studied the writing routine behind Tim Ferriss's 5 New York Times best-selling books:

Here are 4 simple habits you can start using today:

1. Two "crappy pages" per day

Tim sets a low bar to build a consistent daily writing habit:

• Two "crappy" pages per day
• Write as if you're catching up with an old friend
• Quantity first, then edit for quality later

This immediately takes the pressure off producing "quality."

2. Voice notes and interviews

When Tim is stuck with a chapter, he hires someone to interview him about the topic.

This thinking out loud forces him to test and refine his core ideas.

Not all of us can hire people to interview us—so record yourself talking about your ideas on a walk.

It works just as well.

3. Fear Setting Exercise

Self-doubt haunted Tim, so he turned to the Stoic philosophy of Seneca, who taught:

“We suffer more in imagination than in reality.”

Tim created a practice to see that he could handle whatever "bad" situation might arise:

• List out everything that could go wrong
• Figure out what to do in that situation

This lowered the stakes and helped him to start writing.

4. Copywork

To find his voice, Tim copied the styles of journalists he admired, like Chip Heath and Malcolm Gladwell.

• He'd find a piece he admired
• Then rewrite word-by-word digitally or by hand

Stephen King is also a big believer in this practice, too.

One final note:

Tim pitched "The 4-Hour Workweek" to 27 publishers. 

26 rejected him.

But his lucky break came when he met Jack Canfield, author of "Chicken Soup for the Soul."

Jack saw the book's potential and pulled strings to get it sold.

To date, Tim has sold over 2.1 million copies of the book.

The lesson?

Keep trying and put in the work—you create your own luck.

That's it!

But the fastest way to monetize as a writer is to become a Premium Ghostwriter.

It's how I made $40k/month in less than 3 months as a writer.

text:
At 24, I was a broke copywriter and a World of Warcraft addict. But 3 years later?

You see how many people unconsciously compete against each other.

Your goal is to create your OWN category.

• You have a languaging problem
• You have a category problem

Don't get stuck forever in a game of competition.

text:
When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment:

• No AC
• Heater from the 70s
• A desk from Goodwill

But 8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here are 7 brutal lessons I've learned about "success":

Lesson 1: Your habits in your apartment define your habits in your dream house.

I wrote every day in that studio apartment.

Even in the summer, in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers every 40 min to cool off.

Only difference today = AC.

Lesson 2: All you really need is a bed and a desk.

For the first 3 years out of college, I slept on a $79 air mattress from Target.

My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

When I look at my belongings today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed and my desk.

All you need.

Lesson 3: Discipline is the leading indicator of success.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of Internet for 4 years to write my first book.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

Lesson 4: Money doesn't fix everything.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed at how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities weren't "my apartment's." They were my own.

And money wouldn't fix them.

Lesson 5: If you can't do it here, you won't be able to do it there.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll start working on Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't yet convenient.

Lesson 6: The more you achieve, the harder it is to stay disciplined.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago.

I own a TV now. I have disposable income. I can travel.

In my 20s, I didn't even have those options.

Not-having can be a gift too.

Lesson 7: "Wanting" is more fun than "Having".

2 days after I moved into my dream condo, my weekly therapy session rolled around.

"Now what?" I asked him.

I'd spent almost a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

Only to get "here" and not know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I had the opportunity to ghostwrite for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home for a work weekend: a massive ranch with a multimillion-dollar home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different today. You have so many options!"

He shook his head and said, "Actually, it's killing me."

He would rent a studio apartment in the city to make his next album.

We all think the journey somehow gets easier once we've achieved our dreams, or made a certain amount of money.

From my own experience, and what I've learned ghostwriting for so many successful people, it's actually the opposite.

The journey & creative process gets harder.

So, enjoy where you are today.

Your bed, your desk, and your apartment might change.

But the work won't.

text:
This author holds the Guinness World Record for the most NYT bestsellers in history. But many refuse to call him a "real" writer:

Why?

Because of this controversial writing method that powers his $800M empire:

James Patterson was born in 1947 and grew up in Newbrugh, NY.

• He was a good student at school but didn't enjoy reading
• After college, he worked as a copywriter at J. Walter Thompson
• He wrote novels in his spare time, stacking up rejections

His first novel, "The Thomas Berryman Number", racked up *31* rejections. Once published, it won the Edgar Allan Poe Award in 1977. 

But despite the award, commercial success only came in 1993 with "Along Came a Spider."

Then, in 1996, he left his life as an ad executive to focus entirely on writing.

But he didn't plan to write all his books alone.

No, he used ghostwriters.

To this day, he uses this simple formula:

• Patterson sketches out all the action in extensive outlines
• The ghostwriter then turns them into complete books
• Patterson reviews, suggests revisions, & rewrites when necessary

This "co-authoring" model works like a TV showrunner.

Patterson's vision directs each novel or series, while co-authors handle the line-by-line writing.

Critics call him a "brand manager," but readers have bought over 300 million of his books.

The numbers speak for themselves:

• Up to 30 books per year
• Around 147 novels published
• $70-90 million earned annually

But not everyone's a fan.

Many literary experts frown upon his methods. Stephen King described his work as "terrible," and some reviewers called it "subliterate."

With this model, he's worked with more than two dozen different writers.

On top of this, Patterson is intimately involved in the entire publishing process: 

• Jacket design
• Advertising campaigns
• Each step of publication
• Timing of book releases
• Placement in stores

This complete, hands-on approach is rare for authors.

And despite the criticism, Patterson gives back.

He's made substantial donations to:

• School libraries ($8.5 million)
• Independent bookstores
• $500 bonuses to bookstore workers and librarians
• Books for schools and military

His advocacy for literacy extends beyond publishing.

Patterson's model works beyond books:

Every successful:

• CEO
• Founder
• Industry expert 

Needs a ghostwriter.

Industry leaders have the expertise but no time to write.

So, they use ghostwriters as "co-writers" to scale their ideas, save time, and improve messaging.

As more people see the importance of building online, top ghostwriters are in high demand.

I know because ghostwriting is how I went from being a broke freelance writer to making $20k/month in less than 60 days.

text:
I'm 34—and I've made tons of mistakes in the last few years.

Here are 8 I won't ever repeat again:

1. Chasing too many shiny objects (better to do fewer things at a world-class level).

2. Thinking wealth gets made by investing (lots of time wasted watching the stock market, should have been building my businesses).

3. Pushing to failure too many days (better to be sustainable at a "great" pace over a long period of time than "super-duper-great" for a day and then have to spend 2-3 days recovering).

4. Reading mediocre books to completion (you can usually tell if a book is worth your time in the first chapter—and if it's not, put it down).

5. Waiting too long to give ice baths a try (they're amazing & now a crucial part of my fitness recovery, wish I had started doing them sooner).

6. Giving up my nightly creative writing sessions to "work more" (seemed productive at the time, but ended up being a net-negative on my mental & emotional health).

7. Focusing on "spending less" vs. "making more" (spending less is incremental, making more is exponential).

8. Not blocking enough time in my calendar to catch up with friends (business has grown over the last few years, and my friendships suffered as a result).

Let me know in the replies—what's the biggest lesson you've learned in the last few years?

text:
The Rick and Morty approach to storytelling is fascinating:

• 1 circle
• 8 plot points
• Repeated over 71 episodes

Dan Harmon adapted The Hero's Journey framework to create one of the most successful TV shows ever.

Here's how it works (and how to master it):

Before Rick and Morty, Dan Harmon was struggling as a writer—he kept starting scripts but could never finish them. 

He wanted to use Joseph Campbell's Hero's Journey, but there was a problem: 

It was too complex for TV writing. 

So, he stripped it down to its core and created the Story Circle.

The Circle is 8 steps that mirror life itself:

• Evolution (Adapt or Die)
• Personal growth (Face darkness)
• Society (Order → Chaos → New order)

The first half seems straightforward:

1. Character in their comfort zone
2. They want something
3. Enters an unfamiliar situation
4. They must adapt to survive

But the second half reveals the true cost of change:

5. Find what they wanted
6. But pay a heavy price
7. They return changed
8. Face the consequences

This is where most writers would stop but Harmon takes it one step further:

Inside the writers' room, they start with seemingly stupid ideas:

"Rick turns himself into a pickle."

But Harmon asks:

"Why would Rick do that? It has to be the most complex possible reason."

And here's where Harmon's greatest insight emerges:

Each episode runs TWO parallel stories:

• A-story (Main plot)
• B-story (Subplot)

Both follow the same circle.

Consider the episode "The Ricklantis Mixup":

The writers wove together multiple character arcs, each following this pattern.

Behind the crude humor and sci-fi adventures lies something deeper:

• Nihilism
• Depression
• Family trauma
• Existential dread

Why is this important?

Because before adding a single joke, they ask:

"Why are these characters miserable? What personal conflicts drive the story?"

Every crazy adventure masks a deeply human story:

• Cronenbergs = Fear of commitment
• Pickle Rick = Avoiding therapy
• Unity = Addiction

The consequences are permanent.

No reset buttons, easy fixes, or return to the status quo.

And this leads us to the most important lesson of the Story Circle:

The Story Circle is about making complex ideas accessible through character-driven storytelling.

The secret to Rick and Morty storytelling isn't complexity:

It's taking something stupid seriously, giving it structure, adding depth, and making it matter.

And as a writer, understanding story structure is crucial.

And the fastest way to master it?

Ghostwriting.

Being a ghostwriter means you get paid to practice the craft of writing:

• You don't need an English degree
• You don't need decades of experience
• You don't even need to be a "natural-born writer"

This is the quickest and easiest way to monetize as a writer.

Want to get started?

text:
These 8 ghostwriters are making a combined $253k/month. 

But they all started at zero:

Here are the 8 faulty beliefs they fixed to go from broke to rich: 

1. “Nobody can afford my services.”

I promise you:

Millions of people in the world can afford your services—you just haven't found them yet.

But here’s the brutal reason why you believe this:

You think what you’re selling is a lot of money.

It doesn’t matter if it’s $3,000, $4,000, or $5,000—if someone says, “This is too expensive!” you immediately agree with them because you secretly think it yourself.

2. “I need more clients.”

The game isn’t more clients. 

The trick is to get your current clients to pay you more and all new clients to pay you more than you've been charging.

3. “I’ll charge more when I’m more experienced.”

Give me a specific metric of what it means to be more “experienced.” 

Because even if you hit this milestone, you'll still feel inexperienced.

The real issue you're avoiding is why don't you think you deserve to charge more… TODAY.

4. “My niche got saturated. I think it’s time to pivot.”

A writer picks a niche. They land a few clients quickly. They’re a genius!

Then, two or three months later, a couple of those clients churn. 

And immediately the writer thinks the niche is dead and the offer doesn't work anymore. They rush into pivoting their niche.

Neither of these extremes is correct.

• You aren't a genius just because you landed three clients.
• And your business isn't dead just because clients churned.

The skill to build is managing the highs and lows of running a business.

5. “I need to hire someone."

One of the biggest faulty beliefs in business is thinking that once I hire someone, then it'll be easier.

Before you hire, you need to get your one-person business working efficiently and profitably.

6. “If I share too much with the client on the sales call, they won’t need me anymore.”

The fear is that if you give away too much away for free, then no one will want to buy from you. 

But the opposite is true. 

The more you give, the more people trust and buy from you.

7. “I need to build an audience.”

If you're under $20K/month, focus solely on cold outreach and free consulting.

Because that’s the beauty of services. You only need two clients to double your revenue.

Trying to get them through building an audience is playing the game on hard mode.

8. “I can just figure this out on my own.”

You can try and figure it out on your own or you can simply pay for the answer.

I’m not telling you this because you’re not smart or capable. 

I’m explaining this because I don’t want you to hit a brick wall and then throw in the towel because you’re so frustrated.

Pay for the ideal scene and work towards it.

text:
I'm a TV addict—and I thought I'd seen it all.

But when I found Seinfeld, it blew my mind:

On the surface, this is a show "about nothing."

But everyone should understand the psychology that created one of the most successful TV sitcoms in history.

Here's how it works:

Larry David had two simple rules for every episode: 

1. No hugs 
2. No learning 

While other sitcoms taught life lessons and showed character growth, Seinfeld did the opposite.

Most sitcoms follow 4 key themes:

• Work
• Hobbies
• Romance
• Friends/Family

Instead, Seinfeld pioneered 3 episode types:

1. The "Nothing" Episode: An entire plot about waiting for a restaurant table or finding your car.

2. The "Car Crash": Characters desperately try to avoid an outcome that becomes inevitable.

3. The Character Conflict:

• Self-Sabotage–George’s cheapness backfires
• Absurd Obsessions–Kramer chases wild schemes
• Disaster Relationships–Elaine's dates/work with weirdos

Unlike other sitcoms where friends help each other grow, Seinfeld's characters were unreliable allies:

They'd try to help, but:

• Screw up by mistake
• Let self-interest take over
• Put friends in awkward situations

The show perfected the art of social overthinking.

Characters would spend entire episodes debating the minutiae of social rules:

• "Is it okay to double-dip chips?"
• "When is it too late to say 'congratulations'?"
• "How long should you keep a thank you card?"

Then, every character developed signature traits that seemed innocent at first:

• George's "baby" obsession
• Elaine's "Get out!" with a shove
• Kramer's "Oh yeah!" with a fall

Once you understood the formula, you knew disaster was inevitable. 

But how would it unfold? Always unpredictable. 

While other sitcoms tried to make you feel good, Seinfeld chose a different path:

• Nothing is fair
• Nothing really matters
• Good things and bad things happen randomly

The show wasn't really about "nothing."

It was about EVERYTHING we think but never say. That's why it still feels relevant today (its last episode was in 1998!)

They tapped into something deeper. Something universal. 

And that's exactly what great writers do.

After writing for over 10 years, here are some of my favorite ways to develop this observational skill:

• Write every day
• Study human behavior
• Notice tiny details in everyday moments

But there's a faster path to practice your writing craft:

Ghostwriting.

• You don't need an English degree
• You don't need decades of experience
• You don't even need to be a "natural-born writer"

This is the quickest and easiest way to monetize as a writer.

I know because ghostwriting is how I went from a broke freelance writer to making $20k/month in less than 60 days.

And today, the demand for ghostwriters has never been higher.

Want to get started?

Most writers hate money.

But I’ve earned over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter.

While I got paid to practice my craft, other writers thought I was "selling out."

Spoiler alert: 

They were wrong.

Here are 5 reasons why every writer should also ghostwrite:

1. You get paid to learn

I'll never forget getting off the phone with my first ghostwriting client.

I spent an hour asking him every question I could think of about marketing.

Then I sent him the invoice for the first month's retainer: $2,000.

Life-changing.

2. You get paid to practice

Everything you write, you aren’t just writing for yourself, by yourself.

You are writing for a specific person (the client).

• You learn how to write in different voices 
• You get feedback on your work from smart people
• You have a continuous supply of topics to write about

And when you have a deadline to meet, you have to beat procrastination and stop yourself from endlessly fiddling with adjectives.

Ghostwriting accelerates your writing skills 10x over just writing by yourself.

3. You get paid to build your network

Writing for 300+ CEOs, Silicon Valley founders, and Grammy-winning musicians helped me grow a billion-dollar network.

Because of that, I know I have job security for life.

And ghostwriting can do the same for you.

It unlocks incredible doors of opportunity.

4. You get paid to listen (to very smart and successful people)

As a ghostwriter, you surround yourself with people who know about emerging trends and industries.

This is how you get ahead of most people.

It's how angel investors get involved in startups long before the general public finds out about them.

It's how company executives know about high-powered positions long before any sort of announcement is made.

And you're getting paid to listen in on the "inner circle".

5. You get paid to consume content and crystallize your takeaways

If you're waiting to become an "expert" in something before writing about it online, then ghostwriting is a fantastic playground in the meantime:

• Lend your pen to the client
• Download their knowledge
• Write about these topics in the process

There's no better way to learn and form your own opinions about a topic you're interested in - and get paid for it in the process.

That’s it!

I’ve ghostwritten for 300+ industry leaders, and it accelerated my understanding of the craft of writing faster than hiding away in my office writing a novel.

This is why, if you’re not already ghostwriting, highly recommend getting started.

You need to look at the relationship you have with yourself.

So, here are my 5 relationship rules for a life well lived (with yourself and others):

1. Be selfish and selfless. 

Give to yourself, first, so that you can give more to others. 

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return. 

Enjoy being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

True friends are hard to find and honest conversations are rare.

3. Who you are is more important than what you do. 

Your job title is irrelevant.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair. 

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down. 

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

5. Surround yourself with people who are further along than you. 

Name the path you desire most for yourself. 

Then join the others who are 2 steps ahead of you on the journey. 

Like a nearby tree, you will learn how to reach for the sun just like them.

text:
I bet my mortgage Brandon Sanderson is the last great fantasy writer of this century.

Not because of:

• 40M books sold
• The Stormlight Archive
• Or his $41M Kickstarter

But because he cracked the code for world-building—and made it irresistible.

Here's how it works:

Most fantasy authors make ONE fatal mistake when building their worlds.

They overload readers with:

• Culture
• History
• Geography

But Sanderson flips the script:

Instead of world-building for "realism," he does it with one goal:

Everything you learn matters.

This creates a world that feels real—without overwhelming the reader.

The best example of this is in Mistborn and its brilliantly integrated magic system, Allomancy.

Allomancy isn't "just magic."

It shapes:

• Power
• Society
• Everyday life

It does this in 3 ways:

1. The rich wear metal jewelry—and they're weapons

In most fantasy worlds, jewelry is just decoration. 

But in Mistborn, it’s a death trap.

Allomancers can Push and Pull on metal—turning rings, earrings, and even belt buckles into lethal projectiles.

2. Coins become tools of war

Allomancers use coins as both weapons and propulsion, and the world’s combat style is born directly from its magic system.

3. Magic reshapes the economy

Gold in our world is a symbol of wealth.

Now imagine if consuming it gave you superpowers.

In Mistborn, different metals give different abilities, so they become the most valuable resource:

• Mining isn’t just about wealth—it’s about power
• Empires hoard metal like they would weapons
• Smugglers risk everything to get it

Magic doesn’t just exist—it shapes the world.

And the genius part?

Sanderson never pauses to explain it.

Instead, he shows you.

Tolkien built Middle-earth first, then wove a story inside it. His lore exists beyond the plot.

But Sanderson's world-building IS the plot.

Then, finally, there's the Sanderlanche—his signature storytelling technique.

Instead of slowly revealing information, he builds tension until the final avalanche of twists:

• Small hints
• Unanswered questions
• A slow burn

Then BOOM. 

Everything clicks at once.

This makes his books impossible to put down and satisfying. He makes you work for the payoff, but the reward is massive.

Older fantasy authors mastered this. 

But as publishers chase trends, fewer can pull it off.

That’s why storytelling is more valuable than ever.

And the fastest way to learn it?

Not by reading more books

But by:

• Writing daily
• Adapting to different voices
• And mastering different mediums

That’s why ghostwriting is the ultimate way to practice your writing.

And the best part?

The top ghostwriters make $10K, $20K, even $50K per month doing it.

Ghostwriting is how I went from a broke freelancer to $20K/month in 60 days. 

Here’s a 5-step roadmap to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

text:
I've made $250,000 self-publishing my books.

But everyone told me in college, "Nobody makes a living as a writer."

Well, they were wrong.

So, if you want to self-publish your own book, steal my process.

Here’s how I write & list my books on Amazon:

Step 1: Write your book

I can write a 60,000-word back in 30 days.

And I do this in 3 steps:

• Decide on a title
• Outline the book
• Write each chapter of the book

Deciding on your title FIRST gives you a North Star.

Then outlining gives you a container to write in.

Simple.

Step 2: Hire a designer ($1k - $5k)

Writing complete. Now you need a cover.

Most of the designers in big publishing houses also freelance on the side.

I’ve worked with a designer from Penguin Random House for years.

I reached out over email & now she designs all my covers.

Step 3: Format the text for eBook & print

This is a lot easier than most people think.

You can either:

• Pay someone to do this for you (~$500)
• Or use a platform like Reedsy (takes a couple of hours)

Now, onto the most technical part:

Step 4: Upload your files to Amazon Kindle Direct Publishing (KDP)

No special software is required here.

You can upload your manuscript as a:

• PDF
• HTML
• Word doc

And more.

Amazon has tons of guides on how to do this—you can’t go wrong.

It really is that simple.

Step 5: Preview and hit Publish.

You’ve uploaded your book to KDP for free.

• Make edits
• Upload your cover
• Enter your price, etc.

And then hit Publish.

Every time someone orders a physical copy, Amazon will print it on demand (and delivers it to you!)

Now, the final part:

You have to do your own marketing.

Before you even write your book, you should be:

• Writing online
• Building an audience & your email list

These are your best attention engines (and are way more effective than paid ads).

Looking to start writing online so you can test ideas & build an audience for your book?

document.title: 5 Steps To Make $1 Million As A Writer

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I'm a ghostwriter. 

And I've spent 10,000+ hours writing for:

• Startup founders
• Olympic athletes
• NYT best-selling authors

And more. 

But most ghostwriters overthink, stay perfectionists, and never start. 

Why?

Because they think ghostwriting is saturated & AI is taking all the jobs. 

But I turn down $100,000+ worth of ghostwriting gigs every single WEEK. It's laughable how much every business needs writing help. 

And over the years, I've used these 5 templates to ghostwrite anything for anyone:

Template 1: The Mistakes & the Lessons

What's the fastest way to provide value to someone else?

Point out the CAUTION signs and tell them where to go instead:

• Decision
• Outcome
• Mistake
• Lesson

All great business writing follows this same format.

Template 2: The Frameworks

"Want to solve X? Follow 1-2-3."

Every great business "How To" article or post can be reduced down to this simple formula.

• Name the problem
• Pinpoint actionable steps
• Celebrate outcomes

Voila—your client is now a "business thought leader."

Template 3: The Future—Here's Why

Business readers love speculating about the future.

So, to position yourself as an authority, speculate.

• Where is the future of your industry headed?
• Why? What's your thesis?
• Cite 2-3 studies/stats

Hoorah—your client is now an authority in their niche.

Template 4: Category POV

Master-level business writers don't hammer away about the brand.

They educate readers on the category.

• Share what's wrong with the old category
• Name & Claim new category
• Describe the outcome after moving from old to new

Take your reader on a journey.

Template 5: Myths

Everybody loves "insider information."

Your client's domain expertise is an opportunity to pull the curtain back and share what's REALLY going on.

• What do most people think but get wrong?
• What does the world NEED to know?
• What does this knowledge unlock?

I have been ghostwriting for business founders, executives, and investors for years:

• Thousands of articles
• 300+ industry leaders
• Tens of millions of views

Chances are, you've read something I've written online—you just didn't know it.

These are my secret frameworks.

But it took me years to figure these out, understand how to price my services, and hit $40k/month.

So, I put together this free, 5-day email course to help you:

• Land your first client
• Charge premium prices 
• Get paid to learn & build your network

This is everything I wish I had 10 years ago.

text:
This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon.

He turned 'Think Different' into $3 trillion, crafted the iMac story, and helped save Apple from bankruptcy.

When Ken Segall joined Apple's ad agency in 1997, Apple was:

• 90 days from bankruptcy
• Its brand message was a mess
• And sales were plummeting 

Jobs needed someone who could turn complex tech into simple stories.

Enter Ken Segall, the creative director who'd write the words to define Apple.

His first task? 

Create a campaign to tell the world Apple was back.

Segall's team presented several campaigns to Jobs—but one stood above the rest: 

"Think Different"

Two simple words that would become the foundation of Apple's revival. 

Segall used 5 writing rules to bring Apple back from the brink:

1. Simplicity is power. 

• Remove every unnecessary word
• Cut complex explanations
• Focus on emotional impact

The "Think Different" tagline is just 2 words, and the campaign was full of black & white photos.

2. The name tells the story.

Before Segall, Apple's computers had names like Performa 6400, PowerBook 5300, & Macintosh Quadra 800.

Segall convinced Jobs to simplify everything to "i" products:

• iMac
• iPad
• iPhone

Steve Jobs wanted to call Apple’s new computer "MacMan," even though it sounded like a toy. 

Segall pitched "iMac," with a focus on simplicity and the internet—but Jobs hated it.

Yet, after rejecting other names, Jobs quietly approved it.

Segall later joked, "Without it, we’d have PhoneMans and PadMans today."

3. Write like you talk.

Before Segall, Apple's marketing was filled with technical jargon and specs.

He transformed it into copy that was:

• Simple
• Conversational
• Easy to understand

4. Find the drama.

Every product needed a story:

• iPod: 1,000 songs in your pocket
• iPhone: A revolutionary mobile phone
• iMac: The internet computer for everyone

This would capture the customer's imagination.

5. Follow proven frameworks.

His writing framework was simple:

• Start with the problem (make it personal)
• Build emotional tension (highlight the stakes)
• Introduce the solution (make it irresistible)
• Show the transformation (paint the "after")
• End with a clear CTA (drive action)

The power of great writing transformed Apple from near bankruptcy to the world's most valuable company.

But the modern-day Segalls aren't writing for Apple. 

They're ghostwriters scaling the message of founders, CEOs, and businesses.

Because every founder & business leader needs a Ken Segall to scale their message online.

But founders are too busy building to write—creating massive demand for skilled ghostwriters.

I know because I've made $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter.

It's how I learned from industry leaders & got paid to practice my writing.

I created this free Premium Ghostwriting Blueprint to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

document.title: Write A 60,000-Word Book In 30 Days (Beginner's Guide)

posted_at.relative: 4 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The shortcut to massive personal growth isn't:

• Taking ice baths
• Meditating every day
• "Hacking" your dopamine

It's writing.

I've been writing every day for 10+ years.

These are the 5 unexpected ways writing daily has transformed my mind, actions, and life:

1. Accountability. 

Journaling on a daily (or even weekly) basis allows you to: 

• Document your state of mind
• Write (and rewrite) your goals
• Compare what you write vs how you act
• Learn to merge your inner desires with your outer world

There is no better accountability tool.

2. Clarity of feelings. 

Everyone talks about how writing leads to clarity of thought. 

What doesn't get talked about is how writing leads to clarity of your emotions.

When you force yourself to write how you feel, you peel back the layers—until you find the root of the root.

3. Self-confidence. 

I was a very socially anxious, insecure teenager. 

And I was terrified of speaking up in class. 

But when you write, you practice:

• Your ideas
• Your beliefs
• Your convictions

The more you write, the better you get at articulating them. 

4. Natural voice. 

So many writers stress over "finding their voice." 

But when you write & journal (a lot), your natural voice can't help but appear on the page in front of you. 

Even as I write this, I can "hear" how this voice is the same as my journal voice. 

It's all me. You learn to trust yourself.

5. Patience. 

Writing takes time because thinking takes time. 

• It's not about putting words on the page. 
• It's about stacking thoughts to form a perspective. 

When you can learn to be patient with yourself & your thoughts, it's much easier to be patient with others and theirs.

text:
I've needed to shave for a week—but I keep forgetting.

Why?

Because I'm too busy doing "the thing" to reach my goals.

And I want the same for you (the goals, not to forget shaving!)

So here's my advice:

• I wish you would do what you said you were going to do.
• I wish you would get out of your own way. 
• I wish you would follow through.

This means:

• Stop journaling about the thing.

text:
Hayao Miyazaki is often called the “Walt Disney of anime."

With 11 films and 100+ awards, the creator of Spirited Away and My Neighbor Totoro made Studio Ghibli a global icon.

Studio Ghibli’s films are beloved worldwide—and their success speaks for itself:

• Spirited Away grossed $395M, becoming Japan’s 2nd highest-grossing film for 19 years.

• Studio Ghibli has earned over $1.5B at the box office.

Here are Miyazaki’s 5 unconventional creativity and storytelling philosophies:

1. Start with nature

Miyazaki’s films make nature a living, breathing part of the story.

In Totoro, a giant tree becomes a place of healing and connection for two children.

For Miyazaki, nature isn’t just a backdrop—it’s a character.

Conflict in Miyazaki’s films often comes from an imbalance between humanity and the natural world.

In Castle in the Sky, a harmony between technology and nature is destroyed when humans turn the floating city of Laputa into a weapon.

2. Quiet moments tell big stories

Miyazaki slows down to let his worlds breathe.

Small, reflective moments in storytelling give readers time to connect with characters and the world. 

Don’t rush. Those pauses make the big moments hit even harder.

3. Characters make the story

In Spirited Away, Chihiro begins as a frightened child but grows into a brave and determined heroine.

In Totoro, Satsuki and Mei feel like real kids, with curiosity and fears.

Nature → Characters → Plot

4. Mystery adds magic

Miyazaki trusts his audience. He doesn’t overexplain. 

In Spirited Away, the spirit world’s rules are never fully explained. They simply exist.

5. Great storytelling connects everyone

Miyazaki proves that a well-told story can transcend:

• Borders
• Cultures
• And languages

In writing, focus on universal themes and emotions. When readers see themselves in your work, it will resonate across audiences.

Miyazaki shows us that great storytelling isn’t about complexity.

His films remind us of the importance of respect:

• For nature
• For others
• And for ourselves

It’s this philosophy that makes his work timeless.

-
📌 I love to study legendary writers.

text:
J.R.R. Tolkien spent 60 years creating over 15 languages while writing The Hobbit, Lord of the Rings, and Roverandom.

Some called it madness. 

But now Pixar, Universal, and Marvel use his ideas.

This is Tolkien's 4-part framework for world-building and storytelling:

At Oxford, Tolkien was obsessed with languages.

By day, he taught Old English and Norse mythology & literature.

By night, he created entire linguistic systems from scratch—and shared his ideas with friends, including C.S. Lewis, who encouraged his creativity.

Before writing The Hobbit, Tolkien had already created:

• Thousands of words
• Multiple writing scripts
• 15 different Elvish dialects
• Complete grammatical systems

His world-building method focused on 4 key foundations:

1. Language & Communication
2. Cultural Systems
3. Historical Depth
4. Geographical Logic

Here's how they work:

1. Language & Communication

• Created complete linguistic systems
• Developed multiple writing scripts
• Designed cultural expressions
• Built communication patterns
 
This always came first for Tolkien.

2. Cultural Systems 

Every people in Middle Earth had unique:

• Art traditions
• Social hierarchies
• Religious beliefs

These weren't random. Each element is connected to their language.

3. Historical Depth

Tolkien created:

• Complex family trees
• 10,000 years of history
• Ancient legends and myths
• Detailed migration patterns

This is why Middle Earth feels so real & tangible.

4. Geographical Logic

His world had:

• Trade routes
• Natural barriers
• Climate patterns
• Resource distribution

Every mountain and river served a purpose in the story.

Modern studios apply Tolkien’s framework to create worlds:

• Marvel: Built Wakandan & Asgardian cultures
• Disney: Coco’s intricate Land of the Dead with its rules and vibrant afterlife
• 20th Century Fox: Avatar's world of Pandora

What's fascinating is how long each project took:

• Black Panther: 4 years of development
• Coco: 6 years of research
• Avatar: 9 years of world-building

The pattern? Great worlds take time.

But here's what most miss:

Tolkien wasn’t just writing a story—he was crafting a world.

A system so rich it could generate endless stories for as long as he wanted.

This is where many modern creators/writers fall short:

They rush without laying the groundwork, leading to burnout or giving up too soon.

text:
I paid Alex & Leila Hormozi $5,000 to attend their 2-day workshop.

And then another $35,000 to join their smaller-group workshop.

Here are 5 takeaways of what I've learned so far:

1. Take out cash from your business to feel “secure” personally… so you can take more risk inside your business.

This is something I messed up building my first company—my ghostwriting agency back in 2017.

As your business grows, you will need to reinvest profits back into the business to keep it growing.

But that’s high risk. 

So, before you take that risk, make sure you’ve had a few months (or years) taking money out of the business so that you feel comfortable/financially secure in your personal life… 

SO THAT you feel good taking that risk inside your business.

2. Above $5M/year in revenue, you don’t bootstrap departments. You hire subject matter experts to build the department.

I am a bootstrapper, always have been. 

Whenever I have an idea, the first thing I do is go tinker and figure out how to duct-tape together something myself. 

At $1M or $2M or even $3M in revenue, that makes sense.

But above $5M, you don’t bootstrap departments anymore:

• You take your cash.
• You go find someone who has already built the department you want.
• And you buy their subject matter expertise and empower them to build it.

3. Before you change anything in your business, test it.

As you grow, the riskiest thing you can do is change something inside your business. 

Any change has some percentage of risk.

Instead, test your theory inside a contained environment:

• Have 1 closer try a different pitch.
• Send a different offer to 10% of your email list.
• Have 1 coach try a different method with a student.

4. When you change something, expect a 20% decline in the short term.

This is usually because, especially in a more mature business, when you change one thing that one change ends up affecting 5 or 10 other things, which inherently “change” as a result of the original change you made.
 
• Which causes short-term inefficiencies. 
• Or causes short-term declining profitability.
• Or causes some other metric to go down. 

This isn’t a problem. 

It’s just something to expect (so you don’t see a metric go down in the short term and freak out).

5. More/Better/New

The path to improving anything goes like this:

• More: Until volume is the constraint, do more.

• Better: Once volume is the constraint, focus on increasing the quality of your volume by doing better.

• New: Once quality volume is the constraint, and you can’t do any more, and you can’t do it any better, consider doing something new or different. 

I love the simplicity of this.

But here’s a different way of interpreting the above framework:

• Doing “More” is the least risky thing you can do. 
• Doing “Better” has some risk, but also has some incremental reward
• Doing “New” has the most risk. 

Want my other 10 takeaways? 

Check the link in the comments for the full 15 takeaways!

text:
At 27, I pitched a crypto founder $10,000 to ghostwrite his whitepaper.

But then he said something that floored me:

"I just wanted you to know, that I was prepared to pay 3x that but you already said the number so $10,000 it is."

I'd just lost out on a $30,000 deal.

I walked away from that meeting realizing I had no idea how to "value my value."

And I see this all the time with writers.

They flinch at the thought of raising their rates:

• "I'm afraid the client is going to say No." 
• "I'm afraid the client is going to get upset."
• "I'm afraid I'm not worth what I'm asking for." 

These faulty beliefs are all self-inflicted. 

And they're all wrong.

Instead, charge for the value you provide, not the hours it takes to provide the value.

The client is paying you for are all the years it took you to acquire knowledge in order to deliver the outcome.

So, think about the potential ROI the client will get with the asset and outcome you provide, and charge accordingly.

This is how I went on to make $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter with my agency.

posted_at.relative: 5 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
This is the legendary TV writer behind Grey's Anatomy, Scandal, & Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story. 

From unemployed scriptwriter in Hollywood, Shonda Rhimes is now worth $240M. 

Here's her storytelling philosophy (in 10 rules):

1. "Start in the middle of the story."

In the Grey's pilot, we meet Meredith Grey waking up after a one-night stand, late for her first day as a surgeon.

No backstory. No setup. Just drama.

This hooks viewers instantly.

2. "Write what you want to watch."

Grey's Anatomy writers spend 3 to 4 weeks planning each episode's emotional arcs BEFORE writing any dialogue.

This is why viewers can't stop watching.

3. Rhimes's character creation framework:

• Give them secrets
• Make them flawed
• Create impossible choices
• Force them to grow

4. Make every scene count.

Rhimes's shows dominated Thursday nights on ABC for over a decade because of one principle:

Her technique is to end scenes with:

• A decision
• A question
• A revelation

5. "Focus on specifics."

Great storytelling isn't about general ideas—it’s about moments that feel real.

Example: 

In Grey's Anatomy, Meredith and Cristina's “You’re my person” scene became iconic because it was raw and personal.

Specific details stick with viewers.

6. Her key dialogue principle:

"Characters should never say what they're actually feeling."

Watch Scandal. The most powerful moments come from subtext, not direct speech. 

This creates natural tension that drives stories forward.

7. Rhimes's world-building secret:

Create a "shorthand universe"—specific phrases that become part of culture:

• "My person" (Grey's)
• "It's handled" (Scandal)
• "I burn for you" (Bridgerton—which she helped produce)

These spread like wildfire.

8. The "Rhimes Reset" technique.

Every 6-8 episodes, she completely changes the narrative.

Example: The Grey's Anatomy hospital shooting episode reset every relationship and dynamic.

This keeps stories fresh for years.

9. Her approach to casting was different.

"I cast actors who can make viewers feel."

She focused on emotional range over star power, discovering talents like:

• Sandra Oh
• Kerry Washington
• Regé-Jean Page

10. Rhimes's pacing rule:

"Move 3x faster than you think you should."

In Scandal, plots that would last a full season on other shows were resolved in single episodes.

This creates addictive momentum.

These aren't just screenwriting principles.

They're the building blocks of ANY compelling content:

• Hooks that grab attention
• Stories that create emotion
• Characters people care about

All writers should be using these principles in their work.

text:
In 2014, my Quora post went viral with 1.2M views.

But what I did next was the biggest mistake of my career. 

Here's the story of how I lost a $1M business idea:

Growing up, I was undiagnosed with Celiac Disease.

• Had a concave chest & face full of acne
• Weighed less than 100 lbs in high school
• Was super insecure, socially anxious & struggled to make friends

Everything changed when I discovered I was gluten intolerant.

I went gluten-free, dove into bodybuilding, and put on 70 lbs of muscle.

My entire life transformed—physically and mentally.

One day, I shared my transformation story on Quora.

I'd been writing there for almost a year, making mistakes and learning as I went.

This post went viral overnight and hit the front page of Reddit.

My inbox exploded with two questions: 

• "What are your workouts?" 
• "What do you eat to put on muscle?" 

For the first time, I saw writing from the READER'S perspective.

They were telling me exactly what they needed—and were willing to pay for it.

This COMPLETELY changed how I thought about writing on the internet.

So "Skinny to Shredded" was born—and I made $5,000 selling it online.

Not long after, I quit my job to go all in on freelance writing.

But I was: 

• Hunting for clients on Upwork
• Charging pennies per word
• Living project to project

I was working harder than ever. 

Looking back, I realize my mistake:

I had built an audience on Quora and made $5,000 from 1 ebook. 

But I'd only mastered 2 of the 7 "Full-Stack Writer" skills needed to build a sustainable business.

The viral Quora post and that $5,000 meant I had 2 skills:

• Viral Writing
• Basic Offer Creation

These helped me get so far, but I was missing 5 crucial skills:
 

• Newsletter Writing (to build loyal customers)
• Landing Page Copy (to sell more effectively)
• Sales Email Marketing (to convert readers)
• Advanced Offer Creation (to scale products)

Each skill multiplies the power of the others.

If I'd known even half of what I know today, that $5,000 eBook could have been a $1M business.

That's the $1,000,000 opportunity I missed.

Since then, I've learned from my mistakes, made millions, and built multiple revenue streams as a writer:

• Digital products
• Paid newsletters
• High-ticket ghostwriting

All using the exact skills I wish I had back then.

But if you're just starting out as a writer (and want to monetize fast) I'd recommend becoming a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting helped me master all 7 of the Full-Stack Writer skills.

In less than 2 years, I scaled my ghostwriting agency to $2 million.

text:
People think they know what it takes to succeed.

But most don't know what real patience & persistence looks like.

Those who win at everything in life possess these 8 qualities:

1. A winner never stops practicing what got them to where they are in the first place.

They never stop practicing their original skill, that foundational piece that was so influential in their journey. 

They don’t forget where they came from.

2. A winner never says, “I’ve figured it out. I’ve got the answer.” 

There is no answer—not a permanent one, anyway. 

A winner doesn’t see the finish line. 

They see the next wall they’re prepared to run through.

3. A winner is hyper-aware of how they spend every single moment of their day.

To be successful, you need to be prepared to be intentional with the entire structure of your routine and day to day lifestyle, start to finish. 

Every single aspect of your life has to be done purposefully.

4. A winner owns their mistakes so they can learn how to improve, faster. 

You’re not a winner if you can’t own up to your own missteps. 

It’s not just about accountability.

It’s about having the ability to take a good, hard look at yourself and question deeply how you can continue to improve.

5. A winner is focused on mastering their craft, not proving their talents. 

A winner cares far more about becoming the best at their craft, regardless of how many people know it.

A wannabe wants the entire world to know how great they are and simultaneously struggles to spend adequate time mastering their craft.

6. A winner sees their failures as opportunities to prove themselves again. 

When a wannabe falls down, they stay down.

A winner does the opposite. 

They see their downfall as another opportunity to climb the climb again.

7. A winner knows that success can’t be judged by the day or even the week. 

It has to be judged over the long term. Nothing great happens overnight. 

That’s why winners have unrelenting patience. They weren’t born with it. 

They’ve just learned, the hard way, that great things take time.

8. A winner does not care about a title. 

A true winner knows their value. 

And their value is not defined in a title. 

It is exemplified in who they are, what they do, and the way they do it.

text:
How to write a story your readers can't put down:

(This is used in Romeo and Juliet, The Great Gatsby, & more)

Use the ABDCE framework.

Here's how it works:

1. Action

"It was a pleasure to burn." 

This is the first sentence of Fahrenheit 451 by Ray Bradbury. 

It immediately drops you into a moment of action. There is no buildup, no need to "set the scene." Boom. You're there. 

And you're hooked (whether you like it or not).

2. Background 

Only once you have the reader's undivided attention should you even consider giving them the background story. 

Remember: it's hard to care about characters or ideas before you see them in action. 

So start with a moment of surprise, then explain why it matters.

3. Development.

Now it's time to build the story back up. 

• Where is this all going? 
• Why does it matter? 
• What happens if things don't go according to plan? 
• What's at risk? 

The development portion of the story is the Action and the Background, compounded over time.

4. Climax

There are 4 types of story climaxes: Oh no!, Haha, YES!, Sigh. Finally., Oops., and SURPRISE! 

The climax is the tipping point. 

The thing you knew might happen all along.

5. Ending

When the climax does finally happen, now is the time to explain why it matters.

What was it all for? 

In the end, who wins and who loses and what does everyone learn about themselves and each other? 

And it's your choice how much you want to explain vs leave open-ended.

-

text:
The biggest lie society tells about writers:

(I believed this for *years*)

"Nobody makes a living as a writer."

But if you fix these 8 faulty beliefs, you can become an insanely profitable writer:

1. “Nobody can afford my services.”

I promise you:

Millions of people in the world can afford your services—you just haven't found them yet.

But here’s the brutal reason why you believe this:

You think what you’re selling is a lot of money.

It doesn’t matter if it’s $3,000, $4,000, or $5,000—if someone says, “This is too expensive!” you immediately agree with them because you secretly think it yourself.

2. “I need more clients.”

The game isn’t more clients. 

The trick is to get your current clients to pay you more and all new clients to pay you more than you've been charging.

3. “I’ll charge more when I’m more experienced.”

Give me a specific metric of what it means to be more “experienced.” 

Because even if you hit this milestone, you'll still feel inexperienced.

The real issue you're avoiding is why don't you think you deserve to charge more… TODAY.

4. “My niche got saturated. It’s time to pivot.”

No, you just have a skill gap you don't know how to solve yet.

So, instead of solving it, you pick a different niche.

But guess what? The skill gap will be still there in this new niche.

5. “I need to hire someone."

One of the biggest faulty beliefs in business is thinking that once I hire someone, then it'll be easier.

Before you hire, you need to get your one-person business working efficiently and profitably.

6. “If I share too much with the client on the sales call, they won’t need me anymore.”

The fear is that if you give away too much away for free, then no one will want to buy from you. 

But the opposite is true. 

The more you give, the more people trust and buy from you.

7. “I need to build an audience.”

If you're under $20K/month, focus solely on cold outreach and free consulting.

Because that’s the beauty of services. You only need two clients to double your revenue.

Trying to get them through building an audience is playing the game on hard mode.

8. “I can just figure this out on my own.”

You can try and figure it out on your own or you can simply pay for the answer.

I’m not telling you this because you’re not smart or capable. 

I’m explaining this because I don’t want you to hit a brick wall and then throw in the towel because you’re so frustrated.

Pay for the ideal scene and work towards it.

text:
I've made $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter.

But I started from zero.

For years, I was stuck charging per word and per hour.

Terrible idea.

And it's a question I get in my DMs every day:

“How much should I charge as a ghostwriter?!”

Before deciding on the price, you have to choose HOW you'll charge.

There are 5 levels:

Level 1: Charging Per Word

This is the “accepted” form of monetization for writing things like:

• Blog posts
• Press releases
• Even newsletters

If you’re at this level, you think: “Nobody makes a living as a writer.”

WRONG!

Escape Level 1 as quickly as you can!

Level 2: Charging Per Hour

This is the default model.

Unfortunately, your income is directly tied to "effort."

Which means:

• You have no reason to create efficiencies
• You have no reason to care about output
• You "waste time" to rationalize "charging more"

Up we go!

Level 3: Charging Per Month

Charging per month isn’t much better than charging per hour.

It just means:

• You get paid more upfront
• You “lock-in” more hours
• Income becomes more predictable

You may even earn LESS than if you'd just charged hourly.

Let's keep ascending.

Level 4: Charging Per Asset

Ah, now we’re getting somewhere!

When you charge per asset, your success isn’t measured by:

• Time
• Effort
• “Month”

Instead, success is measured by:

Not having the asset → creating the asset.

Zero to 1 moment.

Level 5: Charging Per Outcome

This is the ultimate goal.

It can play out in two ways:

• Profit-sharing
• Equity

Both of these vehicles are typically rare and hard to unlock early in your career.

My recommendation:

Accelerate to Level 4 as quickly as possible, then stay there and stack cash:

• Raise your prices
• Create efficiencies
• Build financial security 

Then, start looking for unique opportunities to reach up to Level 5.

If you’re brand new to ghostwriting, I would love to be your mentor.

I will show you how to:

• Productize your service
• Charge a premium
• Find high-paying clients

Here are 9 uncomfortable truths I learned along the way (which most people spend their entire lives avoiding):

1. No one owes you a job.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

2. If you don't grow together, you grow apart.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

• You change
• They change

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

3. Safety is an illusion.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves. Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

4. Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

5. Status symbols only work on people "below you."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

6. It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

7. The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are.

• If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama
• If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people
• If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs

8. Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more and more developed.

Actions tell your story.

9. The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as a game.

Others:

• An adventure.
• An experience.
• A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

And live it fully.

text:
The shortcut to huge personal growth isn't:

• Ice baths
• Meditating
• Dopamine "hacks"

It's writing.

I've been writing every day for over 10 years.

These are the 5 unexpected ways writing daily has transformed my mind, actions, and life:

1. Accountability

Journaling on a daily (or even weekly) basis allows you to: 

• Document your state of mind
• Write (and rewrite) your goals
• Compare what you write vs how you act
• Learn to merge your inner desires with your outer world

There is no better accountability tool.

2. Clarity of feelings

Everyone talks about how writing leads to clarity of thought. 

What doesn't get talked about is how writing leads to clarity of your emotions.

When you force yourself to write how you feel, you peel back the layers—until you find the root of the root.

3. Self-confidence

I was a very socially anxious, insecure teenager. 

And I was terrified of speaking up in class. 

But when you write, you practice:

• Your ideas
• Your beliefs
• Your convictions

The more you write, the better you get at articulating them. 

4. Natural voice

So many writers stress over "finding their voice." 

But when you write & journal (a lot), your natural voice can't help but appear on the page in front of you. 

Even as I write this, I can "hear" how this voice is the same as my journal voice. 

It's all me. You learn to trust yourself.

5. Patience

Writing takes time because thinking takes time. 

• It's not about putting words on the page. 
• It's about stacking thoughts to form a perspective. 

When you can learn to be patient with yourself & your thoughts, it's much easier to be patient with others and theirs.

text:
Many call this author a "fake" writer.

But he has:

• Sold 300+ million books
• Writes 8+ novels every year
• Holds the world record for the most NYT bestsellers

All because of a controversial approach that built his $800 million empire:

First, some background:

James Patterson was born & raised in Newburgh, NY.

• He grew up disliking books
• After college, he landed a copywriting job
• In his free time, he wrote stories NO one wanted

Seriously.

30+ publishers rejected his first novel, "The Thomas Berryman Number."

But one company took a chance on Patterson and, in 1976, published his first book.

A year later, it won the Edgar Allan Poe Award.

Despite this, Patterson spent the next 2 decades stuck working in advertising.

During that time, he published 6 more books.

But everything changed in 1993:

Patterson's 7th novel ("Along Came a Spider") became a NYT bestseller.

And shortly after, he *finally* quit his job to become a full-time author.

But he had a VERY controversial plan:

He'd use ghostwriters.

• He would create an outline for a story
• A ghostwriter would write the entire novel
• Then, Patterson would review, revise, & rewrite

Which is how he's published over 200 novels—and sold 300+ million books.

On top of this, Patterson spends most of his time in the publishing process:

• Jacket design
• Placement in stores
• Advertising campaigns
• Timing of book releases
• Etc.

However, not everyone is a fan of his work:

• Critics call Patterson a "brand," not a writer
• Some reviewers say his novels are "subliterate"
• Stephen King has described his work as "terrible"

But despite the hate, Patterson gives back.

He's donated over $75 million to support teachers, fund school libraries, and help independent bookstores.

And his business model doesn't only work for novels.

Because every:

• CEO
• Founder
• Industry expert

Needs a ghostwriter.

Like Patterson, they have the expertise but lack the time to write.

So, they use ghostwriters as "co-writers" to:

• Save time
• Scale their ideas online
• Attract clients and customers

And every year, thousands of new businesses realize they need to build online.

Which means ghostwriters are in HIGH demand.

At 24, I was a broke copywriter working a full-time job at an ad agency. But 3 years later?

I'd generated $200k as a writer.

Here's my story and 3 ways you can make money writing online in 2025:

Growing up, I dreamed of going pro in hockey. 

But I fractured my spine at 14 and again at 17. My NHL dreams were shattered. 

So, I turned to World of Warcraft for an escape.

WoW had the biggest impact on my life:

• Taught me to fall in love with monotony 
• Introduced me to "social networks" at a young age
• Showed me the Internet could be a competitive outlet

And, at 16, I got my first job writing about World of Warcraft.

Fast forward a few years, and I was working as a minimum-wage copywriter but my income was capped & I was building someone else's future.

So, I made a decision:

I challenged myself to write a Quora answer every single day for 1 year.

• Worst case scenario: I do the thing I love.
• Best case scenario: Something happens.

A few months in, one of my answers went viral.

It was the story of how I went from a skinny teenager undiagnosed with Celiac Disease to putting on 70 lbs of muscle as a gluten-free bodybuilder.

It got millions of views in just a few days.

I started getting hundreds of messages asking about my story and my fitness secrets.

So I spent all weekend pouring my heart into two eBooks: 

1. My workout routine 
2. My meal plan

I called it Skinny to Shredded. 

On Monday, I linked those eBooks to my viral Quora answer. Then, my phone started to buzz non-stop. 

Sale after sale after sale. 

In one weekend, I'd made more money than I did in a month as a copywriter. 

This moment changed my life forever.

Fast forward 2 years, I'd made my $200k writing online for myself & others.

So, how did I do it? 

And, if I was to restart from scratch today, what would I do? 

Here are the 3 best paths to making money writing online ↓

Path 1: Premium Ghostwriting.

This is the best opportunity in 2025. 

Most entrepreneurs, founders, & even influencers need writers. 

You'll be surprised how quickly you can scale to $10K+ per month.

Your knowledge is valuable.

• eBooks

This is simple:

1. Give away your best stuff for free

text:
One of the most interesting “writing rules” I came across in college was Hemingway’s feelings on adverbs:

He hated adverbs.

He called them “lazy writing.”

Adverbs are any descriptor word ending in -ly: 

• Lazily
• Eerily
• Mostly
• Honestly
• Etc.

Learning about this rule changed my writing forever.

And while I still make the mistake of using one too many adverbs here and there, I try very hard not to fill my sentences with them. 

Adverbs are passive ways of saying the same thing the sentence is already saying.

But since learning about this rule, and realizing the ways it improved my writing, I started looking for more rules.

And a big one I’ve come across has been the importance of removing “little words:”

• It
• So
• Just
• That
• Well
• Like
• This
• Much
• Often
• Really
• Better
• Usually
• Probably
• Sometimes
• And, and, and

Little words (especially in turns of phrase like “In so forth” or “So much as”) have a knack for killing what could be a great, powerful, concise sentence — and turning it into a soupy mess.

For example:

[Original sentence]: 

“One of the most common changes that occurs when people go off to college is they become different versions of themselves.”

[Rewrite]: 

“When people go off to college, they become different versions of themselves.”

Both sentences are saying “the same thing,” and yet the second sentence reads 10x easier than the first.

Why?

We removed all the little words:

• One
• Of
• The
• That
• Is

So, the next time you’re staring at a sentence and can’t quite put your finger on why it’s not reading the way you want it to read, ask yourself: 

“What little words can I delete here?”

With writing, less is always more.

—

posted_at.relative: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
I can write a 60,000-word book in 30 days. 

The secret?

Math (and a simple 3-step process).

First, the numbers.

Here's how to break down your 60,000-word book:

• 10 chapters at 5,000 words each
• Each chapter has 5 to 7 sub-questions
• These sections are then only 700 to 1,000 words long

Each section is the length of a blog post or a newsletter.

So your book is a series of shorter posts bolted together.

This should help take some pressure off writing a "60,000-word book." 

Instead, take it in chunks.

Then add in an introduction and conclusion, and you can easily hit 60,000 words.

Second, the process.

(This works even if you've never written a book before.)

Here are 3 steps to write a 5,000-word chapter in 90 minutes:

Before you start writing, you need to have two items in place:

1. Your title
2. Your outline

If you don't have these, you don't know what you're writing about.

Step 1: Prep the Chapter on the page

1. Write out the overarching question for the chapter
2. List out the sub-questions/topics for the question

With the chapter prepped, writing becomes a fill-in-the-blanks exercise (and far less intimidating).

Step 2: Expand each sub-question/topic

For each of these, ask yourself:

"What does the reader need to understand about the sub-question and overarching chapter question?"

The best way to think about this?

Use the 10 magical ways of expansion.

Any piece of writing can be expanded in 10 ways:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

Mix and match these to answer the sub-questions and overarching question of the chapter for the reader.

Step 3: Make a round of edits

Don't get bogged down in this.

Some editing pointers:

• Look out for repetition
• Edit for flow and readability
• Do a final read-through out loud to pick up any clunky parts

That's it—you just wrote a 5,000-word chapter in 90 minutes.

That's it!

📌 My first took me 4 years to write (before I learned this framework).

Since then, I've written 10+ books.

And I earned a lucrative living while doing it.

How?

By ghostwriting.

It's how I got paid to write, learn, and network with industry leaders.

text:
In 2000, Malcolm Gladwell wrote "The Tipping Point"—but his peers ripped it apart:

Despite the hate, it sold over 5 million copies.

Here's why:

Gladwell defines the "Tipping Point" as:

"The moment of critical mass, the threshold, the boiling point." 

In his signature style, Gladwell writes about various stories that show "Tipping Points" in:

• Life
• Society
• Business

But something fascinating has happened over the last 25 years:

No one remembers the stories Gladwell shared.

Readers only remember the definition of the new language—what the "Tipping Point" actually means. 

And they remember Gladwell for creating that definition.

Now, "The Tipping Point" isn't a novel concept.

It's basically how word-of-mouth marketing works.

But legendary writers (like Gladwell) are languaging experts and use language to reshape familiar thinking.

Here's how:

"The Tipping Point" claimed the language of:

• The problem
• AND the solution

Gladwell was the one to say, "Hey, that phenomenon that happens? Let's call that something."

The book is just a bunch of examples of this idea.

But it's the definition that sticks.

Many say, "Gladwell got it all wrong. He got lucky."

But it doesn't matter.

He will be remembered forever as the one who defined "The Tipping Point."

Right or wrong is irrelevant.

So if you want to:

• Write words that change how people think
• Create "sticky" language for familiar concepts
• And become a great writer in the process

You don't need to write a lot of words.

Instead, you need to be strategic with your language.

text:
Brutal truth for writers (I could have made more money with my writing a lot faster if I'd known this):

You can't deposit likes into a bank account.

So, if you want to get clients on LinkedIn, try this:

First, stop trying to go viral.

You don't need to be some kind of "internet celebrity" with hundreds of thousands of followers to land 4-figure (or 5-figure) deals. 

Just because you have a big audience, doesn't mean they're the "right" followers.

This faulty belief leads to 3 outcomes:

• More pressure to write for "the algorithm"
• No content that your ideal clients want (and need) to read
• You stop posting entirely when you don't go viral or land a client

Instead, you should create content in 3 main buckets:

Content Bucket 1: Your industry.

Writing about your niche leads to 3 unlocks:

• It demonstrates to ideal clients your expertise
• It shows you can ghostwrite about your industry
• It increases the chance your content ends up in their feed 

(This post is a good example)

Content Bucket 2: How your service helps your clients. 

• Share testimonials
• Share your frameworks
• Share tips, mistakes, lessons, etc.

You attract the attention of your ideal client by showing you understand their problems and how to fix them.

Content Bucket 3: Other topics that excite you. 

Put together pieces of content that showcase your personality and interests.

They help you connect with like-minded people in the industry to ghostwrite for.

But this shouldn't be a big block of your content (10% is good).

Why do I recommend these Content Buckets?

For 5 reasons:

1. Shows your expertise on a topic
2. Overcomes client objections before a sales call
3. Clarifies your thinking on your niche & service
4. Creates a compounding library of content
5. Sparks conversations with ideal clients

Creating content as a ghostwriter isn’t about going viral.

It’s about building a compounding library of content that helps you and your client understand your industry and service better.

text:
These 8 ghostwriters are making a combined $253k/month. 

But they all started at zero:

Here are the 8 faulty beliefs they fixed to go from broke to rich: 

1. “Nobody can afford my services.”

I promise you:

Millions of people in the world can afford your services—you just haven't found them yet.

But here’s the brutal reason why you believe this:

You think what you’re selling is a lot of money.

It doesn’t matter if it’s:

• $3,000
• $4,000
• Or $5,000

If someone says, “This is too expensive!” you immediately agree with them because you secretly think it yourself.

2. “I need more clients.”

The game isn’t to get more clients. 

The trick is to get your current clients to pay you more and all new clients to pay you more than you've been charging.

3. “I’ll charge more when I’m more experienced.”

Give me a specific metric for being more “experienced.” 

Because even if you hit this milestone, you'll still feel inexperienced.

The real issue you're avoiding is why you don't think you deserve to charge more… TODAY.

4. “My niche got saturated. I think it’s time to pivot.”

A writer picks a niche. They land a few clients quickly. They’re a genius!

Then, two or three months later, a couple of those clients churn. 

And immediately, the writer thinks the niche is dead and the offer doesn't work anymore. They rush into pivoting their niche.

Neither of these extremes is correct.

• You aren't a genius just because you landed three clients.
• And your business isn't dead just because clients churned.

The skill to build is managing the highs and lows of running a business.

5. “I need to hire someone."

One of the biggest faulty beliefs in business is thinking that once I hire someone, then it'll be easier.

Before you hire, you need to get your one-person business working efficiently and profitably.

6. “If I share too much with the client on the sales call, they won’t need me anymore.”

The fear is that if you give away too much away for free, then no one will want to buy from you. 

But the opposite is true. 

The more you give, the more people trust and buy from you.

7. “I need to build an audience.”

If you're under $20K/month, focus solely on cold outreach and free consulting.

Because that’s the beauty of services. You only need two clients to double your revenue.

Trying to get them through building an audience is playing the game on hard mode.

8. “I can just figure this out on my own.”

You can try and figure it out on your own or you can simply pay for the answer.

I’m not telling you this because you’re not smart or capable. 

I’m explaining this because I don’t want you to hit a brick wall and then throw in the towel because you’re so frustrated.

Pay for the ideal scene and work towards it.

text:
In 1884, Mark Twain gambled everything on a dying President—and created a $12M bestseller and America's greatest military memoir:

At the time, former President & Civil War hero Ulysses S. Grant was utterly broken:

• His Wall Street firm collapsed in a Ponzi scheme
• He lost his entire life savings
• At 62, he was diagnosed with terminal throat cancer

His family faced poverty after his imminent death.

Grant's only hope was his memoir.

Publishers hoped to profit from the American hero.

So, Century Magazine offered him the standard 10% royalty.

But Mark Twain sensed a historic opportunity:

Twain offered Grant a 70% royalty—SEVEN TIMES the industry standard.

The publishing industry thought Twain had lost his mind:

"It will never sell enough to justify that rate!"

Twain ignored them. He knew a hero's final testament would be priceless.

The stakes couldn't be higher:

• Grant was racing against death
• His family's future hung in the balance
• Twain's publishing company risked bankruptcy

Despite excruciating pain from cancer, Grant wrote 10-12 hours daily.

But Twain wasn't just a publisher—he became Grant's editor and writing coach.

As Grant raced against time, writing an astonishing 50 pages per day, Twain provided:

• Feedback
• Copy editing
• And encouragement.

Their collaboration intensified as Grant's health failed.

Twain watched in awe as the pages revealed extraordinary military genius and unexpected literary talent.

As Grant's condition deteriorated, Twain brought proof pages to his bedside.

The President finished his manuscript just DAYS before his death.

"Personal Memoirs of U.S. Grant" exploded onto the market:

• 350,000 sets sold in the first year
• Generated $450,000 ($12M in today's dollars)
• Became the bestselling book of the 19th century

Grant's family was saved from poverty. His legacy was secured forever.

This partnership reminds us of a truth:

1. The person with the STORY (Grant) 
2. The publisher with the VISION (Twain) 

Can create something more valuable than either could alone.

Twain's gamble paid off spectacularly:

• He saw value where others saw risk
• Grant's story needed Twain's publishing expertise
• Together they created something neither could alone

This partnership principle is the foundation of modern ghostwriting, where expertise meets storytelling power:

text:
A brutal truth for writers who love to read:

(This is how you become a world-class reader)

Reading alone doesn't make you a better writer.

You have to read closely and STUDY the language.

So, ask these 5 key questions every time you read something:

1. "What is the author really saying here?"

I annotate constantly when I read.

In the margins, I write what the author is saying—below the literal. 

"She walked into the room without hesitation."

OK, what's REALLY happening here? 

The unspoken is the loudest narrative.

2. "What details are staying with me the most?"

As you read, and long after you finish reading, notice what you remember the most:

• Ideas
• Images
• Dialogue

The fact they are sticking with you mean these details are sticky.

How can you make your own writing sticky?

3. "How did the author create this image/feeling?"

Don't just watch the movie.

Ask how the writer got you to see or feel what you did. 

Study that language closely. 

Then keep it in your back pocket for when you want to illicit a similar image in your readers' minds.

4. "How quickly is the author revealing new information?"

In Ship 30 for 30, we call this Rate of Revelation.

• Slow RoR = lots of description, lots of depth, moves like molasses.
• Fast RoR = minimalist, bang-bang-bang, like coffee.

Is the author's choice right for the story?

5. "Why is the author saying this?"

Great writers make every word count.

Nothing is said by accident. 

Like how a painting is realized pixel by pixel, question why the author is describing this detail, shining a light on this trait, etc.

Each choice has significance.

This is a picture of vintage books recovered from my grandma's home which I now have in my library. 

Keeping them in the family:

Jerry Seinfeld.

Seinfeld’s favorite medium was always stand-up.

All great business writing follows this same format.

Template 2: The Frameworks

"Want to solve X? Follow 1-2-3."

Every great business "How To" article or post can be reduced down to this simple formula.

Template 3: The Future—Here's Why

Business readers love speculating about the future.

• Where is the future of your industry headed?

Template 4: Category POV

Master-level business writers don't hammer away about the brand.

They educate readers on the category.

• Share what's wrong with the old category

Take your reader on a journey.

Everybody loves "insider information."

Your client's domain expertise is an opportunity to pull the curtain back and share what's REALLY going on.

• What do most people think but get wrong?
