text:
When I was 23 years old, I kept asking my boss and mentor for a promotion. After asking 3 times, he said:

"You have Early 20s Syndrome.... BAD."

I asked him what that was.

Early 20s Syndrome is a paralyzing obsession with the future, depriving yourself of the present—when being present is what's required to get you to the future you desire.

It's counter-intuitive.

When you're in your 20s, you think obsessing about the future will "get you there faster."

As a result, you:

• Constantly chase the next shiny object
• Constantly question where you are "today"
• Constantly change focus (or jobs, or even careers)
• Constantly compare yourself to everyone else—where they are, how fast they're progressing, etc.

You're exhausted, but you aren't actually moving.

You're running in place.

How do you cure Early 20s Syndrome? 

You have to let go of the future.

Anyone "successful" in their 20s is far less focused on where they're headed, and far more focused on:

• Acquiring skills
• Getting in the reps
• Practicing good habits
• Obsessively improving 1% each day

Because the only way "Future You" comes to be is if "Present You" puts in the work.

Which means every minute you spend daydreaming, wondering, questioning "Future You," that version of yourself gets further and further away.

Whereas every minute you spend working on "Present You," the closer and closer you get to your imagined "Future You" self.

Until enough days pass.

And enough skills are acquired.

And suddenly, "Future You" is You, today.

text:
In 2018, I dropped $6,000 on a book I didn’t even want.

Why?

To land my biggest ghostwriting client to date.

Here’s the story of how I helped one man become a New York Times best-seller (and discovered an ugly truth about the publishing industry):

Back in 2018, I had a ghostwriting client who ran an events business.

He decided becoming a best-selling author would boost his “credibility” (and scale his business).

Little did I know this would involve me.

One day, he came to me with an offer:

“Cole, I love you and I love your agency, so I have an introduction that would be really helpful for your business... but I would like something in return.”

“OK, shoot,” I said.

“I know the CMO of one of the largest technology companies in the world. A multi-billion dollar company. I'll introduce you directly to him and I will put in such a good word that it's almost guaranteed they will want to work with you…

But I want you to buy 300 of my books.” 

At the time, ordering 300 books at $20 a piece was no small amount of money for my agency. 

It would cost us $6,000.

But I had a feeling this would pan out and I liked him. He’d been a good client and I wanted to help him become a NYT best-seller.

“Alright, I'm game.”

So I forked out $6k and ordered 300 copies of the book.

(By the way, the intro he made became our biggest client and would pay us a FULL YEAR in advance.)

When the book launched, 300 copies of this guy's book showed up at my door.

Box after box after box piled up, all across my house.

“What am I gonna do with all of these books?!”

I gave them away to everyone I knew and when people came over, I’d be like “Hey, I have some extra books, why don’t you take one?”

But did my client his the list?

On the week of the launch:

• Every interview he’d done dropped
• Every article we’d written was published
• And every influencer in his network promoted his book

And the week after, he hit the NYT bestseller list.

Then, one week later, the book completely fell off the charts. The book made him zero money.

• He spent tons of money getting his team to promote the book.

• He called in every favor possible (and had to return all those promises he made, costing him money).

Getting the New York Times as a badge probably cost him $250,000+.

I realized that becoming a best-selling author is not about who writes the best book.

It's about who's willing to do the most work on the marketing side and spend the most money. 

Becoming a New York Times best-seller is 100% just a marketing game and a budget game.

The whole experience changed my perspective on whether I should aim to be a best-seller.

There are situations where having that badge is valuable.

But if you get the badge, but nobody reads the book, does it even matter?

—
📌 Ghostwriting helped me build a billion-dollar network.

text:
I have built two paid Substack newsletters to 6 figures:

• Write With AI ($400k/year)
• Category Pirates ($200k/year)

These are the 5 proven steps to build a 6-figure newsletter on Substack:

1. Become Known For A Niche You Own

Don’t write about 100+ different things. 

Write about 1 thing 100+ different ways.

2. The Higher The Frequency, The Shorter The Posts

For example:

if you are curating news and sharing those insights & takeaways each morning with readers, you should be as brief as possible. 

Whereas if you are writing long‑form, in‑depth guides, you should publish your high‑quality work less often—so readers have time to dig into it.

3. Drive Readers From Twitter & LinkedIn

These are clearly the two best platforms right now for driving newsletter subscribers (free or paid). 

You should be able to extract a dozen or more social posts from every newsletter you write—constantly driving readers back into your library of content.

4. Stick With It For At Least 6 Months

It’s very hard to build any sort of meaningful audience, especially a paid one, if you only give it 1–2 months of effort. 

By the 6‑month mark you should have some signal if you are creating a profitable niche for yourself or not.

5. Price It Relative To The Outcome You Are Promising

Most paid newsletters sit somewhere between $5 and $20 per month. 

But the amount itself isn’t really what matters. 

What matters is the amount relative to the outcome. 

For example: it’s hard to convince someone to pay $5 per month to read “interesting things.” 

But it’s very easy to convince someone to pay $20 (or $50, or even $100) per month to learn how to increase their earning potential & make more money themselves.

text:
Most life advice sucks. Platitudes won’t change your life. 

Here's the stuff that will:

17 rules for a well-lived life (that I learned the hard way in my 20s):

1. Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2. Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3. Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4. Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5. Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6. Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7. Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8. Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9. Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10. Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11. Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12. Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13. Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14. Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15. Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16. Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17. Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

text:
In 2014, my Quora post went viral with 1.2M views. But what I did next was the biggest mistake of my career:

Here's the story of how I lost a $1,000,000 business idea:

Growing up, I was undiagnosed with Celiac Disease.

• Had a concave chest & face full of acne
• Weighed less than 100 lbs in high school
• Was insecure, socially anxious & struggled to make friends

Everything changed when I discovered I was gluten intolerant.

I went gluten-free, dove into bodybuilding, and put on 70 lbs of muscle.

My entire life transformed—physically and mentally.

One day, I shared my transformation story on Quora.

I'd been writing there for almost a year, making mistakes and learning as I went.

This post went viral overnight and hit the front page of Reddit.

My inbox exploded with two questions: 

• "What are your workouts?" 
• "What do you eat to put on muscle?" 

For the first time, I saw writing from the READER'S perspective.

They were telling me exactly what they needed—and were willing to pay for it.

This COMPLETELY changed how I thought about writing on the internet.

So "Skinny to Shredded" was born—and I made $5,000 selling it online.

Not long after, I quit my job to go all in on freelance writing.

But I was: 

• Hunting for clients on Upwork
• Charging pennies per word
• Living project to project

I was working harder than ever. 

Looking back, I realize my mistake:

I had built an audience on Quora and made $5,000 from 1 ebook. 

But I'd only mastered 2 of the 7 "Full-Stack Writer" skills needed to build a sustainable business.

The viral Quora post and that $5,000 meant I had 2 skills:

• Viral Writing

These helped me get so far, but I was missing 5 crucial skills: 

• Newsletter Writing (to build loyal customers)

Each skill multiplies the power of the others.

If I'd known even half of what I know today, that $5,000 eBook could have been a $1M business.

That's the $1,000,000 opportunity I missed.

Since then, I've learned from my mistakes, made millions, and built multiple revenue streams as a writer:

All using the exact skills I wish I had back then.

text:
I'm not surprised I became a millionaire by my early 30s.

Because I put myself through hell in my 20s:

A short list of insane things I did:

1. No internet.

When I was in my early 20s, I didn't allow myself to have Internet in my apartment until I finished writing my first book, Confessions of a Teenage Gamer. 

It took me 4 years. 

But I did it.

2. Started my first company with only $5k.

I started my first company at 26 years old with $5,000 in savings and no back-up plan.

3. I convinced one of my best friends to quit his job and build a ghostwriting agency with me. 

And when he said it was too risky, I removed the risk by:

• Working double-time
• Paid him a "salary" out of my savings for 4 months

I did this until we figured out how to land more clients.

4. I got put in the hospital.

When our agency took off, I pushed myself so hard I ended up in the hospital with shingles.

Instead of stopping, I popped painkillers for a month and continued taking sales calls, client calls, and interviews with new hires.

5. I kept almost no cash on hand and constantly bought more Bitcoin.

6. Had multiple side projects.

While working 60+ hours/week, I took on side projects helping crypto companies with their messaging and getting paid in tokens. 

All of them went to 0. Learned a lot though!

7. Took on investors.

We sold 20% of our ghostwriting agency to 2 angel investors, knowing there was massive risk in this being the wrong decision for the company (it was) but also knowing we'd learn a ton in the process (we did).

8. Took hardly any vacation.

When I finally took a vacation in 2019 (my first real vacation in years), I realized I was on the wrong path.

I also realized nobody could get me off the Ferris wheel except me.

Came home & fired 20+ people on 1 Zoom call, shutting down my agency.

9. Spent the first year of the pandemic ghostwriting for a few people to pay rent, and writing my next book, The Art & Business of Online Writing. 

That book has since generated $300K+ and sold tens of thousands of copies.

10. Building Ship 30 for 30 on nights & weekends. 

When I started Ship 30 with Dickie Bush, I was still ghostwriting (to pay the bills) and he was still working at BlackRock. 

We both worked:

• Nights
• Mornings
• And weekends 

To build Ship 30 into a multi-7-figure biz.

11. Started a paid newsletter.

When I started Category Pirates, that was in addition to:

• Ghostwriting
• AND starting to scale Ship 30

Which meant giving up all my hobbies and writing 8-12 hours every single weekend.

I have ALWAYS been a "burn the boats" sort of person.

When I want to do something, I remove the possibility to "turn around" when things get hard. I force the decision to find a way through—and I always do.

text:
This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon:

• He helped save Apple from bankruptcy
• Turned 'Think Different' into $3 trillion
• And crafted the iMac story

When Ken Segall joined Apple's ad agency in 1997, Apple was:

• 90 days from bankruptcy
• Its brand message was a mess
• And sales were plummeting 

Jobs needed someone who could turn complex tech into simple stories.

Enter Ken Segall, the creative director who'd write the words to define Apple.

His first task? 

Create a campaign to tell the world Apple was back.

Segall's team presented several campaigns to Jobs—but one stood above the rest: 

"Think Different"

Two simple words that would become the foundation of Apple's revival. 

Segall used 5 writing rules to bring Apple back from the brink:

1. Simplicity is power. 

• Remove every unnecessary word
• Cut complex explanations
• Focus on emotional impact

The "Think Different" tagline is just 2 words, and the campaign was full of black & white photos.

2. The name tells the story.

Before Segall, Apple's computers had names like Performa 6400, PowerBook 5300, & Macintosh Quadra 800.

Segall convinced Jobs to simplify everything to "i" products:

• iMac
• iPad
• iPhone

Steve Jobs wanted to call Apple’s new computer "MacMan," even though it sounded like a toy. 

Segall pitched "iMac," with a focus on simplicity and the internet—but Jobs hated it.

Yet, after rejecting other names, Jobs quietly approved it.

Segall later joked, "Without it, we’d have PhoneMans and PadMans today."

3. Write like you talk.

Before Segall, Apple's marketing was filled with technical jargon and specs.

He transformed it into copy that was:

• Simple
• Conversational
• Easy to understand

4. Find the drama.

Every product needed a story:

• iPod: 1,000 songs in your pocket
• iPhone: A revolutionary mobile phone
• iMac: The internet computer for everyone

This would capture the customer's imagination.

5. Follow proven frameworks.

His writing framework was simple:

• Start with the problem (make it personal)
• Build emotional tension (highlight the stakes)
• Introduce the solution (make it irresistible)
• Show the transformation (paint the "after")
• End with a clear CTA (drive action)

The power of great writing transformed Apple from near bankruptcy to the world's most valuable company.

But the modern-day Segalls aren't writing for Apple. 

They're ghostwriters scaling the message of founders, CEOs, and businesses.

Because every founder & business leader needs a Ken Segall to scale their message online.

But founders are too busy building to write—creating massive demand for skilled ghostwriters.

I know because I've made $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter.

It's how I learned from industry leaders & got paid to practice my writing.

text:
This writer has sold 20 million books, wrote 3 New York Times best-sellers, and is the ghostwriter for Will Smith:

Before meeting Smith, Mark Manson had a successful career as a writer.

But one day, Will Smith came knocking:

Will Smith wanted to write a memoir but struggled with the writing process.

Mark Manson, a best-selling author, was seen as a serious contender to be his ghostwriter.

So Will got in touch.

But Mark quickly saw how this would be unlike any other project he'd worked on before:

• Restricted meeting time
• Balancing honesty and privacy
• Capturing Will's distinct voice
• Curating from 1000s hours of footage

But Mark was determined to create something truly "Will Smith."

After an extended interview, Mark spent 3-4 days with Will and his family.

One morning, at breakfast, Mark pitched an outline structuring the book around the emotional themes of Will's life:

• Fear in childhood
• Anger in adolescence
• Pride in adulthood
• Ending with love

Will Smith loved it, so they got to work. 

But Mark only had 30-60 minutes with Will at a time. And to realize the book's vision, Mark needed to know everything.

So over 2 years, Mark was given access to terabytes of personal archives like home videos and press interviews.

Mark visited every key location to recreate descriptions vividly: 

• Will's childhood home in West Philadelphia
• The church Will went to growing up

Like every ghostwriter knows, capturing Will Smith's distinct voice was crucial:

• Bombastic
• Larger-than-life
• Prone to enthusiastic exaggeration

As Mark said, "Whatever Will tells you, you've got to dial it back like three notches to get to the truth."

Mark and Will split the work based on their strengths.

Mark did the:

• Research
• Outlining
• First drafts

Will then revised the drafts to infuse his voice.

"Will" was published in November 2021, and it became an instant New York Times best-seller.

The book is:

• Humorous
• Authentic
• Inspirational

And this experience probably opened up more ghostwriting opportunities for Mark in the celebrity memoir niche.

So, how do you ghostwrite for famous people?

Mark Manson got his name from taking a single blog post and turning it into a best-selling book.

This happened after *years* of writing and testing different ideas.

text:
I bet my life savings Christopher Nolan is the last great director of this century—but not because of:

• Inception
• Interstellar
• Or Oppenheimer

But because he found a method to irresistible storytelling:

Nolan uses the Plot Map Technique to visualize his storyline.

He doesn’t want “constraints” to hold him back when writing the story (especially if it's complex).

So he uses this technique to help his production team:

1. Visualize the plot arc(s)
2. Create it on screen

Let's take Inception as an example (one of the most well-crafted movies of the last 50 years).

The intricate plot is a journey around dreams within dreams, jumping between layers of reality.

Nolan's Plot Map for Inception resembles Kurt Vonnegut's "Man in Hole" story shape.

This is where the main character:

1. Faces a problem
2. Gets out of it
3. Grows from the experience

But Nolan added an interesting twist:

In Inception, as Cobb (played by DiCaprio) moves closer to the dream world of Limbo, he travels "down" the plot map toward problems.

Then, he must fight his way "up" to reality.

This not only mirrors Cobb's journey but also the movie's core concept of nested dreams.

By using the Plot Map, Nolan keeps the story focused despite the movie's:

• Multiple “dream” storylines
• Competing character arcs
• And ever-increasing stakes

Without the Plot Map, the story would have become unfocused and hard to follow.

Nolan planned the entire 2.5-hour Inception movie on a single page.

Other notable writers have similar practices:

• Norman Mailer (in Harlot's Ghost)
• Henry Miller (in Tropic of Capricorn)
• JK Rowling (in the Harry Potter series)

Want to use this technique for yourself?

Try this:

• Make a Visual Outline (shapes, arrows, visuals)
• Emphasize Key Points (incident, climax, resolution)
• Map Character Development (impact of events/themes)

This will help you keep your story focused while writing.

—

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The most misunderstood, misused, yet highest-leverage writing skill of 2025:

Copywriting.

It's how I've made over $15,000,000 as a writer.

And these 7 exercises will turn you into a top 1% copywriter:

1. Thinking at the Category Level

People organize info by category FIRST, not brand. 

• Before asking, "which restaurant?"
• They ask "what kind of food?" 

Great copy starts by defining or modifying your category. 

2. Speak in Benefits, Not Features

Nobody cares WHAT your product does. They care WHAT IT ACHIEVES for them.

• Don't say: "Our machine has 17 screws"
• Say: "You'll build quads faster with zero back pain"

Benefits must connect to your category. 

How can you explain the benefits of a smart watch if you don't first understand how it differs from regular watches? 

Category first, benefits second.

3. Name and Claim the Legacy Problem

The most compelling copy names the problem your customer faces. 

When you name it (in 3 words or less), you can:

• Diagnose people with it
• Next, they internalize it
• Then they seek YOUR solution

Once named, problems become "sticky" in customers' minds, and they create urgency for solutions.

4. Name and Claim the Solution

Name your solution in a way that sticks. 

• Bitcoin isn't "better money"—it's "digital capital." 
• No-code isn't "simplified programming"—it's tech without coding. 

This is how word-of-mouth ignites.

There are two ways of naming and claiming solutions:

• A positive solution
• Or an absence of a negative

5. Make it Tangible, Not Intangible

Humans value tangible things over abstract concepts. 

Don't offer "steps." Instead, offer a:

• "Blueprint"
• "Framework"
• Or "Checklist" 

Package your ideas like Apple packages its products.

6. Make it Objective, Not Subjective

Effective copy can't be interpreted differently by each reader. 

Replace subjective terms with objective ones that create the same mental image for everyone.

• Don't say: "Retire happy"
• Say: "Retire with $500K in savings, a 5-bedroom beach house, and a vintage Ferrari" 

Objective statements create cohesive messages that scale.

7. Define Who It's For

Great copy clearly defines its audience. 

Dude Wipes created a multi-million-dollar business by modifying the wipes category for a specific audience: guys only. 

So ask: Who is YOUR offering specifically for?

Don't just define your category audience broadly. 

List specific archetypes of your ideal customers so people can see themselves in your product. 

Connect everything back to who it's specifically for.

——
📌 I earned my first $1 million as a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I got paid to practice my writing, work with industry leaders, and escape my ad agency job.

text:
James Patterson is one of the best-selling authors of all time:

• Written 160+ novels
• 144+ NYT best-sellers
• 300+ million copies sold
• Net worth = $800 million

I am fascinated by his career—so I watched his MasterClass (twice!).

Here are the golden nuggets:

1. A publisher should want to buy your outline.

When I first watched his MasterClass a few years ago, this completely changed my writing process.

Your outline should read like a book, minus all description.

• Short paragraphs 
• Plot-plot-plot
• Pivotal moments only

2. The 1st line matters the most.

"It's way too early in the morning for dead people."

This is the 1st line from Patterson's horror book, You've Been Warned.

If you nail the opener, you've got the reader: hook, line & sinker.

But if you whiff the 1st line, they're gone.

3. Characters don't talk "at" each other.

Most dialogue sucks.

• "How are you?"
• "Good, you?"
• "I'm alright."
• "Just alright?"

This back-and-forth dialogue is boring.

Instead, dialogue needs to push the story:

“I didn't know I could be this happy.”
"Is that your idea of a proposal?"

Which leads to the next golden nugget:

4. Don't make the reader wait.

Patterson loves:

• Short chapters
• Fast-paced plot
• Minimal description
• Plot twists as often as possible

He even admits to being weak "stylistically," but he's the master of pacing.

5. Edit your outline ruthlessly—so you don't have to edit your manuscript.

Another massive takeaway for me was to spend 80% of your time/effort refining your outline, first.

• Edit plot points
• Edit character traits
• Edit backstory details

This will save you tons of time later.

6. Don't be afraid to collaborate with other writers.

Maybe the most fascinating aspect of Patterson's career is his use of co-authors.

1. He writes the outline
2. Then he lets another (established) writer fill it in

This is how he publishes 12+ books per year.

Wild.

It's not just authors like Patterson who need co-authors (a.k.a. ghostwriters).

Every industry leader, CEO, executive, and anyone with expertise needs helping scaling their insights and advice online.

How do I know?

Because ghostwriting is how I went from a broke freelance writer to making $20k/mo in less than 60 days.

I've written for 300+ industry leaders (CEOs, founders, & NYT best-selling authors).

Neil started out like most writers—chasing journalism dreams:

• Then scored a Pop Life column at The New York Times
• Then became a contributing editor at Rolling Stone

This was the early 2000s, and newspapers were struggling.

Why chase paychecks from declining newspapers when celebrities would pay 10x more to help them tell their stories?

Neil's journalism career gave him the perfect entry point.

He’d written a major Rolling Stone cover on Marilyn Manson—helping legitimize him in the ‘90s.

• Six-figure paydays that journalism couldn't match
• A-list connections across entertainment, music, and publishing
• Insider experience crafting bestsellers that sell, not just win awards

By the 2010s, Facebook had eaten the media world:

• Magazines shut down

Most writers and publishers had to change careers completely.

• Storytelling that hooks readers

text:
9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:

I'm 34.

Over the last decade, I've built multiple 7-figure businesses.

And I've had to face every single one of these uncomfortable truths:

1. No one owes you a job

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck—that's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

Whether you like it or not, you are human labor leverage.

2. If you don't grow together, you grow apart

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

• You change
• They change

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

3. Safety is an illusion

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves. Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

4. Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives doing this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

5. Status symbols only work on people "below you"

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

6. It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

7. The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are

• If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama
• If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people
• If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs

8. Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more developed.

Actions tell your story.

9. The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as:

• A game
• An adventure
• A "waiting period between lives"

All of these are right. Or none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

-
📌 PS—Building my ghostwriting business helped me face these truths (and make $3,000,000 in the process).

text:
People think they know what it takes to succeed.

But most don't know:

• How long it really takes to reach a goal
• What real patience & persistence looks like
• How to ignore their ego to accelerate progress

Those who win at everything in life possess these 8 qualities:

1. A winner never stops practicing what got them to where they are in the first place.

They never stop practicing their original skill, that foundational piece that was so influential in their journey. 

They don’t forget where they came from.

2. A winner never says, “I’ve figured it out. I’ve got the answer.” 

There is no answer—not a permanent one, anyway. 

A winner doesn’t see the finish line. 

They see the next wall they’re prepared to run through.

3. A winner is hyper-aware of how they spend every single moment of their day.

To be successful, you need to be prepared to be intentional with the entire structure of your routine and day to day lifestyle, start to finish. 

Every single aspect of your life has to be done purposefully.

4. A winner owns their mistakes so they can learn how to improve, faster. 

You’re not a winner if you can’t own up to your own missteps. 

It’s not just about accountability.

It’s about having the ability to take a good, hard look at yourself and question deeply how you can continue to improve.

5. A winner is focused on mastering their craft, not proving their talents. 

A winner cares far more about becoming the best at their craft, regardless of how many people know it.

A wannabe wants the entire world to know how great they are and simultaneously struggles to spend adequate time mastering their craft.

6. A winner sees their failures as opportunities to prove themselves again. 

When a wannabe falls down, they stay down.

A winner does the opposite. 

They see their downfall as another opportunity to climb the climb again.

7. A winner knows that success can’t be judged by the day or even the week. 

It has to be judged over the long term. Nothing great happens overnight. 

That’s why winners have unrelenting patience. They weren’t born with it. 

They’ve just learned, the hard way, that great things take time.

8. A winner does not care about a title. 

A true winner knows their value. 

And their value is not defined in a title. 

It is exemplified in who they are, what they do, and the way they do it.

posted_at.relative: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
The most underrated, oddly-ignored, yet highest-leverage skill in 2025:

Lean Writing.

It's how Malcolm Gladwell, Ryan Holiday, and Mark Manson make millions every year.

Here's how you can master it:

Do you see a pattern here?

• Mark Manson's "Subtle Art " started as a blog
• Malcolm Gladwell's "Outliers" was a New Yorker article
• Ryan Holiday's "The Obstacle Is The Way" began as a blog

They all use a framework I call Lean Writing.

Most legacy writers spend 10-20 hours on blog posts nobody reads

But Digital Writers can publish a post that gets thousands of views.

Unfortunately, most writers hate social media.

But it's the secret to Lean Writing.

The Lean Writing method has 3 simple steps:

1. Test ideas online with short-form content
2. Listen for feedback & double down on what works
3. Expand those ideas into longer assets & even businesses

The Lean Writing content ladder looks like this:

• LinkedIn Post → Atomic Essay 
• Essay → Free email course 
• Course → Paid digital product
• Product → Business

The next expansion only happens if the previous one resonates with readers.

But how do you expand your writing at each stage?

Use the 10 Magical ways to expand any piece of writing:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

So, how do you test your ideas?

Use social platforms:

• Platforms send your content to readers
• If they engage, it goes to more people
• And so on and so on

Digital writers know that algorithm hacking isn't how you test your ideas.

Instead, let the algorithm do its thing—it will always surface the best content.

Eric Jorgenson knew this when he was writing the Naval Ravikant's "Almanack".

For 3 years, he collected Naval's:

• Tweets
• Podcast clips
• Interview fragments

Naval's ideas were already validated on social media. 

All Jorgenson had to do was curate/expand them into a book.

When James Clear, Mark Manson, or Ryan Holiday share their social posts or newsletters, they aren't trying to write a masterpiece.

They are testing ideas.

When people comment on specific points, they know exactly which ones deserve more attention.

This is the exact framework I used to write social content for 300+ industry leaders (and make millions as a ghostwriter).

text:
The most powerful habit for personal growth:

(I do this every morning for 30 minutes)

Journaling.

I don't have a fancy journal or complex rules.

Just a pen, paper, and 30 minutes to help focus my mind and find clarity.

Here are the 4 dead-simple prompts I use every day:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. 

Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human—I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 10 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to:

• Hear yourself
• Become conscious of your thoughts
• And take actionable steps forward

📌 I used these prompts to start writing online, quit my ad agency job, and become a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I wrote for 300+ industry leaders and made $180k/month in less than 2 years.

Want to get paid to write & work with industry leaders?

posted_at.relative: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text:
Back in 2017, Pixar storyboard artist Matthew Luhn shared his frameworks for Oscar-winning storytelling. 

Here's a break down of the 4 rules anyone can use to tell captivating stories (in their writing, work, and life):

First, some context:

The human brain is a forgetting machine.

After 10 minutes, you'll only remember 5% of the data and statistics you hear. 

But if wrapped in a story, retention jumps to 65%. 

Stories trigger chemical reactions in our brains: 

• Endorphins when characters laugh 
• Cortisol during tense moments 
• Dopamine during victories 

Pixar mastered this "chemical rollercoaster" and it's why their storytelling framework is worth billions.

Here's how they do it:

1. You have 8 seconds to hook an audience

Pixar's formula for an irresistible hook: 

• Create unexpected conflict 
• Challenge assumptions 
• Present impossible scenarios 

"What if a rat wanted to be a French chef?"—Ratatouille's hook in under 5 seconds.

The Up opening sequence is a masterclass in emotional manipulation: 

• Set up joy (marriage, dreams)
• Sudden heartbreak (hospital scene) 
• Hope returns (saving for adventure) 
• Final gut punch (wife's death) 

This emotional whiplash makes grown adults cry.

2. The 3-Act Structure 

• Beginning: Set the scene 
• Middle: Introduce conflict
• End: Outcome and transformation 

This has worked for thousands of years because it mirrors life itself.

3. Characters must transform to keep audiences invested

• A car learning compassion over winning (Cars) 
• A monster choosing laughter over fear (Monsters, Inc.) 
• A helicopter dad learning to let go (Finding Nemo)

These stories make us ask:

"What would I do in this situation?"

4. Authenticity makes stories universal

• Finding Nemo? Written by new fathers. 
• Inside Out? Inspired by a director's struggling teen. 
• Wall-E? Born from an animator's environmental concerns. 

Every Pixar story comes from personal experience.

For example, Inside Out's director tried forcing happiness on his depressed daughter. 

He turned this parenting mistake into a film about embracing all emotions. 

In the end, good storytelling is about how you make people feel.

And that's Pixar's billion-dollar storytelling secret.

The fastest way to monetize your writing skills is to become a ghostwriter.

It's how I went from a broke freelancer to earning $20k/month in 60 days. 

And it's how I got to write all day, every day after quitting my 9 to 5.

text:
Success isn’t a destination. 

It’s a daily habit. 

Here are 30 things successful people tell themselves:

(Learned from ghostwriting for 300+ industry leaders, including CEOs, Olympic athletes, and serial entrepreneurs):

1. Today is a great day to build.

2. No one is responsible for my success besides me.

3. I welcome the learning process with open arms.

4. Everyone was once a beginner.

5. Mastery over my craft is the highest accolade.

6. I will not let someone else define my self worth.

7. To unlock the upside, I must face the risk of the downside.

8. I need to be patiently impatient and have a bias toward action.

9. The enemy of long-term compounding is short-term results.

10. Wealth is built over decades, not days.

11. I have no competition, because I am only competing against myself.

12. The world rewards those who do what others do not.

13. I trust myself and my convictions.

14. Every failure is an opportunity to learn.

15. Every success is an opportunity to start again.

16. Forward is the only option. There is no going back.

17. I own my shortcomings—and leverage them to my advantage.

18. Dwelling on a mistake where the lesson has already been extracted is a waste of precious time.

19. My potential knows no limits.

20. Simplicity is velocity, because the simple thing is the hard thing.

21. It's better to move slowly in the right direction than quickly in the wrong direction.

22. I do not accept any obstacle in my way.

23. "Tomorrow" is an excuse. Do it now or don't do it at all.

24. I don't know how yet—but I will figure it out.

25. What I once wanted, I currently have. (The destination isn't what matters.)

26. Hours are a poor measure of effort. Intentional practice is the only thing that matters.

27. I will not judge my entire life based on any single day.

28. Better to be singular and deep than multifaceted and shallow.

29. Real progress is made in silence.

30. Life is not a zero-sum game. We can all win.

PS—Ghostwriting completely changed my life.
